### Post by trubblemaker on 2006-12-06
> **nippras said:**
> where can I find it
Here's the logs. Can any one see any issues....
-------------------------------------

usrbob@ubuntu:/etc/network$ sudo ndiswrapper -d 14e4:4318 bcmwl5
Driver 'bcmwl5' is used for '14E4:4318'
usrbob@ubuntu:/etc/network$ sudo iwconfig
lo        no wireless extensions.

eth0      no wireless extensions.

sit0      no wireless extensions.

wlan0     IEEE 802.11g  ESSID:"Apple Network 809684"
          Mode:Managed  Frequency:2.457 GHz  Access Point: 00:11:24:EA:00:B1
          Bit Rate:11 Mb/s   Tx-Power:25 dBm
          RTS thr:2347 B   Fragment thr:2346 B
          Encryption key:off
          Power Management:off
          Link Quality:100/100  Signal level:-89 dBm  Noise level:-256 dBm
          Rx invalid nwid:0  Rx invalid crypt:0  Rx invalid frag:0
          Tx excessive retries:0  Invalid misc:0   Missed beacon:0


it's looking good so here you should use 
```

sudo dhclient wlan0

```

if you use:
```
sudo ifup wlan0
```
it reads your /etc/network/interfaces file and uses those settings completely negating the current settings listed above.

think
ifup = </etc/network/interfaces>
dhclient = <current settings>

this also means you might want to edit /etc/network/interfaces to work with that network

### Post by jbinto on 2006-12-06
compwiz18, thanks for your efforts. On a compaq presario v2310ca, using ubuntu 6.10 edgy 32-bit, the script worked effortlessly.

On the other hand, while I tried setting up -amd64 ubuntu, there were two issues.

1) The one being the kernel edgy ships with is not supported and i had to lurk on your site, in the /linux dir, for an up to date link to a good kernel .deb. Perhaps you should mention this in your main post and/or the error screen. (The link to ubuntuforums.org addressing this issue, points to out of date .deb links, FYI).

2) Also in Edgy 64-bit, the script freezes at "Installing ndiswrapper-1.8...". I found out it was indeed asking me for (Y/n) keyboard confirmation. I also found out it was trying to access the edgy CD, as somehow (which I've never experienced in all my 32-bit ubuntu experiences) it had the cd as an entry in my sources.list.

Since I no longer have -amd64 installed on this machine, I can't help test, but just wanted you to be aware of these issues... thanks again.

---

### Post by Jbweld on 2006-12-06
This worked for me using Ubuntu 6.10 
This is My compiled references For USR MAxG 125MB Pci Card Will try this again on  PCMICA MaxG Card next...Cheers!

IN TERMINAL Program...
sudo apt-get install bcm43xx-fwcutter
sudo /usr/share/bcm43xx-fwcutter/install_bcm43xx_firmware.sh
sudo apt-get install network-manager
sudo apt-get install network-manager-gnome
sudo apt-get
sudo apt-get install wpasupplicant
--------------------------------------------------------------------------
sudo gedit /etc/network/interfaces
# Comment out everything other than “lo” entries in that file and save the file 
USE # to comment out!
((Create a file called /etc/default/wpasupplicant, add entry ENABLED=0 and save the file
Sudo Gedit /etc/default wpasupplicant))) add ENABLE=0 into it and save file.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
sudo touch /etc/default/wpasupplicant
YEAH RE_Boot
sudo gtk-update-icon-cache -f /usr/share/icons/hicolor/
Then set it all up with wpa/network names etc..and your done.

---

### Post by trubblemaker on 2006-12-09
checkout you other comuters /etc/network/interfaces file.  it should have settings that you need to add to the other computer. 'man interfaces' will also give you some tips on what to add to the interfaces file so that you can login at boot.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2006-12-12
This happens to me too, I'll tell you how to fix it:

Go to the directory that you compiled ndiswrapper in, do **sudo make uninstall** Now do open Synaptic, and reinstall all already installed packages starting with ndiswrapper.  Now run the script.  After the script is run, **ndiswrapper -l** should show the driver as being installed and valid.  Now go to the directory that you compiled ndiswrapper in, and do **sudo make install**.  Now do **sudo rmmod ndiswrapper** and **sudo rmmod bcm43xx** and **sudo modprobe ndiswrapper**

---

### Post by blumley on 2006-12-12
This is my post from the other day:

Here's my story:

> 
The network manager script ending up being the one that worked for me. I have WPA-PSK enabled on my network. It's working beautifully. The only problem I have is that every time I log in, I'm having to tell it which SSID, security type, and password to use again.

I also have a laptop on the same network, also running Dapper. But it connects to the network every time I log in flawlessly (except for the whole keyring annoyance). My question is: Is there a .conf file or something I can set up to keep from having to re-add the network info every time?

Thanks in advance!

I had a suggestion to check the laptop's /etc/network/interfaces file.  Not a bad idea but I'm using network manager, so the only thing configured is interface lo.  Any other suggestions?  I'm not sure how my laptop is retaining these settings while my desktop loses them every time.

### Post by arron on 2006-12-13
WOW!     :mrgreen: 

I have been having some problems with the other method to get this wireless card to work.  to say the least it would disconnect randomly, and when it was up would be very very slow to download.  

This setup worked like a charm for me (i386, with wep), now it has a stable connection, and oh my, downloads are a lot better!  Doesn't seem to be 100% to windows, but a lot lot lot faster than before.

Thanks for spending the time to develop this setup/install  To anyone with this wireless card, USE THIS DRIVER/SETUP!!!!  And on a fresh install, add the ndiswrapper programs first, its not installed by default.

Thanks
Arron

### Post by UnknowUser on 2006-12-13
I FINALLY MADE IT!!!

I use my Broadcom 4318 on Edgy 64 bit! 

I try to use your script under a new installation with my compilate kernel 2.6.19 but ndiswrapper didn't come installed and i couldn't "make" or "make" install the ndiswrapper1.29.
When i do "sudo apt-get install build-essential", install it, but in my /lib/modules/2.6.19 there wasn't the folde build (but it was only in /lib/modules/2.6.17-10-generic)

As last choice i've installed your compilate kernel 2.6.18-3, and i've run the script and installed ndiswrapper1.29, and with WIFIManager i'm now connected without cable!!!



Thx compwiz18!

### Post by compwiz18 on 2006-12-14
OK: try this:

if you haven't already added universe/multiverse repos: [http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu_Edgy#How_to_apt-get_the_easy_way_.28Synaptic.29](http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu_Edgy#How_to_apt-get_the_easy_way_.28Synaptic.29)

now run **sudo apt-get install ndiswrapper-utils-1.8 ndiswrapper-common**

if there are any errors, post here
if there aren't, rerun the script.

### Post by SethVIII on 2006-12-14
no excuse but that I am an idiot, thanks for the script and instructions and answering stupid questions, all are great.

I am posting this from Ubuntu.

Thanks again.  I spent way too much time on this before I found this thread and then way too much more as you have made it so easy.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2006-12-16
Wait wait wait.  before you reformat: they need to go in just plain /lib/firmware - not in any sub directories

and make sure you chmod 755 them all.

### Post by nardis_Miles1 on 2006-12-17
Just a quick note. I'm running an AMD turion ml-32 so I did install the 2.6.19. The current level of ndiswrapper sources (1.18 ) would not compile. However, by installing the current stable release from sourceforge (1.31) ndiswapper installed. As soon as I did a modprobe on ndiswrapper, the card came up, found its address and worked nicely. So I might recommend that for the 2.6.19 kernel on the AMD64 machines you also recommend a newer version of ndiswrapper. However, thanks very much for posting the HOWTO.

Art Edwards

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2006-12-17
Yes, you will also have to compile ndiswrapper from scratch, as the one in the Repos doesn't work with 2.6.19.

### Post by DeffeDefect on 2006-12-19
It works!
After trying different suggestions, installing Ndiswrapper driver installation tool, installing Network Manager, adding universe and multiverse, installing orginal driver from belking, re running this script, I finaly ended up loosing my working eathernet connection.

- I cleaned up my /ect/wpa_supplicant.conf
adding pre-up line and post-down line which I forgot the first time. This might be why it did not work. And also checking wpa_supplicant.conf
- reboot and the it works. Dissconected network cable and rebooted a couple of times and the wireless is still running.

Some issues / questions:
- The Network Connection icon shows no connection all the time. Suggestions?

- I don't know what driver the card is using (belkin orginal or the one supplied here). How do I find out and how do I change driver if I want to test new ones?

- My wireless network card and my router both support 54Mbps and Belkin 125 High speede mode but when running iwconfig the bit rate is new over 54 but some times very low. How can I get the best bit rate?

- I've sean many different settings in the wpa_supplicant.conf -file. What do all settings mean and what do they do (any url where I can find info)?

Thnk you for your support!

Best regrads/
DeffeDefect

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2006-12-19
> **DeffeDefect said:**
> It works!
After trying different suggestions, installing Ndiswrapper driver installation tool, installing Network Manager, adding universe and multiverse, installing orginal driver from belking, re running this script, I finaly ended up loosing my working eathernet connection.

- I cleaned up my /ect/wpa_supplicant.conf
adding pre-up line and post-down line which I forgot the first time. This might be why it did not work. And also checking wpa_supplicant.conf
- reboot and the it works. Dissconected network cable and rebooted a couple of times and the wireless is still running.

Some issues / questions:
- The Network Connection icon shows no connection all the time. Suggestions?

- I don't know what driver the card is using (belkin orginal or the one supplied here). How do I find out and how do I change driver if I want to test new ones?

- My wireless network card and my router both support 54Mbps and Belkin 125 High speede mode but when running iwconfig the bit rate is new over 54 but some times very low. How can I get the best bit rate?

- I've sean many different settings in the wpa_supplicant.conf -file. What do all settings mean and what do they do (any url where I can find info)?

Thnk you for your support!

Best regrads/
DeffeDefect
@**DeffeDefect**: The network connection icon doesn't work quite right with ndiswrapper...it usually shows either full or none, but nothing in between.  Running **ndiswrapper -l** (that is a lowercase L) will show you the name of the driver being used.  If you need info about wpa_supplicant.conf, you can try running **man wpa_supplicant** and **man wpa_supplicant.conf** - these should give you info.  Also **man ndiswrapper** will give you info about adding/removing drivers.  I am guessing the "Belkin 125 high speed mode" is a windows thing, as my card is identical to yours, but I could be wrong.  And in reality, 54mbps is all you will need, as long as you aren't transfering massive files across the internet.

And glad you got it working.

---

### Post by Forest Ent on 2006-12-22
Thanks a lot for this script. It made everything very simple.

It failed on first attempt. I looked at the log and saw that "ndiswrapper" install had failed. I added the command "apt-cdrom add" to get the pack from the CD, and then it worked.

By the way, this being my first post here, let me introduce myself. I am french and have been using windows for 10 years and got fed up. I got a new computer for xmas 2 days ago and decided that this time it would be linux. I installed kubuntu 6.10 live-CD and it worked perfectly, but for wifi. Now everything is fine and I am so glad I finally changed. I am going to talk all my friends into doing the same.

Thanks a lot for all the people who are making it work. I really am grateful.

### Post by kvorg on 2006-12-23
Hmmm,  compwiz18, I gave a spin to your kernel at [http://linux.punkforjesus.com/kernel/index.htm](http://linux.punkforjesus.com/kernel/index.htm) and was a bit surprised. It does not look like a generic kernel build, it seems to lack some driver modules we came to expect in generic ubuntu kernels, and, above all, it does not, for example, make temperature sensors and fans operate correctly on my laptop (as opposed to stock ubuntu kernels 2.6.17-10-generic 2.6.17-6-amd64-k8 2.6.15-27-amd64-generic and
2.6.15-26-amd64-generic.

So perhaps it would be a good idea to maybe warn laptop users they migh cook their processors silly if they don't know what they are doing.

But thanks for the service, of course!

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2006-12-23
> **kvorg said:**
> Hmmm,  compwiz18, I gave a spin to your kernel at [http://linux.punkforjesus.com/kernel/index.htm](http://linux.punkforjesus.com/kernel/index.htm) and was a bit surprised. It does not look like a generic kernel build, it seems to lack some driver modules we came to expect in generic ubuntu kernels, and, above all, it does not, for example, make temperature sensors and fans operate correctly on my laptop (as opposed to stock ubuntu kernels 2.6.17-10-generic 2.6.17-6-amd64-k8 2.6.15-27-amd64-generic and
2.6.15-26-amd64-generic.

So perhaps it would be a good idea to maybe warn laptop users they migh cook their processors silly if they don't know what they are doing.

But thanks for the service, of course!
Really? I copied the standard Ubuntu configuration file, and built my kernel around that, only removing the Digital Video Broadcasting drivers (they cause the compile to fail)...so in theory it should contain all drivers that Ubuntu provides.  What processor do you have?  It was NOT built for generic 64bit processors, it was built for AMD64 processors - so if you have a 64bit Intel processor this isn't gonna work for you.

---

### Post by Wofl on 2006-12-26
hey, i got one sugestion

change the destination of the log file to the current folder.

i use a commandline interface and was wondering for hours why it didnt install, until i noticed that it tried to write the log in ~/Desktop , witch dosnt exist.


or better even, check if there is a folder called ~/Desktop
if there is then post it there, if not in the current directory variable you already have

but great anyways, helped me a lot

### Post by leducjjr on 2006-12-30
Dude this script rocks.  Many many thanks.

I've a Compaq Presario M2020US.  

02:06.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4306 802.11b/g Wireless LAN Controller (rev 03)

Installed Ubunto 6.10, everything worked out of the box except for the wireless connection.

My ethernet port is fried so I can only use wireless connection.

I set up my lappy's HD to have 4 partitions - NTFS, FAT32, ext3 and swap.

I used my Windows internet connection to get your gzipped script and to copy the instructions to the FAT32 partition.

Then I logged into Ubuntu and used the instructions to get the wireless adapter turned on (blue light special).

I followed the rest of the instructions to go to System|Administration|Networking and to enable the Wireless Connection by going into the properties and typing in my router's SSID and password.  

Rebooted, back into Ubuntu, left-clicked the Network icon up by the clock, and selected the new eth1 option that wasn't there before the fix.

Many thanks again, you're a pro.

John

easy fix: just gotta compile some stuff...don't worry, not hard:

[LIST]
[*]download --> [ATTACH]21980[/ATTACH]
[*]extract using GUI or terminal
[*]open terminal
[*]navigate to directory in terminal
[*]type **make**
[*]type **sudo make install**
[*]reboot
[*]use the internet
[/LIST]

if you have trouble, ask!

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-01
Did you also install the kernel headers from the download site?  This problem can also come from installing the headers and the image in the wrong order I believe, although that is unconfirmed - and I don't know what the so-called "correct order" is, so if you installed both, uninstall everything and install them in the opposite order.

You can put those lines in **/etc/rc.local** - put them at the top, and they will be run automatically at boot.

#!/bin/sh -e
#
# rc.local
#
# This script is executed at the end of each multiuser runlevel.
# Make sure that the script will "exit 0" on success or any other

Try making it look like:
```

#!/bin/sh -e
#
# rc.local
#
# This script is executed at the end of each multiuser runlevel.
# Make sure that the script will "exit 0" on success or any other
# value on error.
#
# In order to enable or disable this script just change the execution
# bits.
#
# By default this script does nothing.

sudo rmmod bcm43xx
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo ifdown eth1
sudo ifup eth1
sudo dhclient

exit 0

```

Sorry, I forgot that it won't work if you put the lines at the top - my fault.

### Post by zes on 2007-01-02
#!/bin/sh -e
#
# rc.local
#
# This script is executed at the end of each multiuser runlevel.
# Make sure that the script will "exit 0" on success or any other
# value on error.
#
# In order to enable or disable this script just change the execution
# bits.
#
# By default this script does nothing.

sudo rmmod bcm43xx
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo ifdown eth1
sudo ifup eth1
sudo dhclient

exit 0

### Post by mrdeeds2324 on 2007-01-02
well what i do is the trouble shoot thing. i redo this code to get the light working:

```
sudo apt-get install ndiswrapper-utils-1.8 ndiswrapper-utils-1.1 ndiswrapper-utils ndiswrapper-common
```

and this one by one to get the wireless working again:

```
sudo rmmod bcm43xx
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo ifdown eth1
sudo ifup eth1
sudo dhclient
```

cat /etc/modules /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist that gave me this:

```
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

lp
# This file lists those modules which we don't want to be loaded by
# alias expansion, usually so some other driver will be loaded for the
# device instead.

# evbug is a debug tool that should be loaded explicitly
blacklist evbug

# these drivers are very simple, the HID drivers are usually preferred
blacklist usbmouse
blacklist usbkbd

# replaced by e100
blacklist eepro100

# replaced by tulip
blacklist de4x5

# causes no end of confusion by creating unexpected network interfaces
blacklist eth1394

# snd_intel8x0m can interfere with snd_intel8x0, doesn't seem to support much
# hardware on its own (Ubuntu bug #2011, #6810)
blacklist snd_intel8x0m

# causes failure to suspend on HP compaq nc6000 (Ubuntu: #10306)
blacklist i2c_i801

```

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-02
> **mrdeeds2324 said:**
> well what i do is the trouble shoot thing. i redo this code to get the light working:

```
sudo apt-get install ndiswrapper-utils-1.8 ndiswrapper-utils-1.1 ndiswrapper-utils ndiswrapper-common
```

and this one by one to get the wireless working again:

```
sudo rmmod bcm43xx
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo ifdown eth1
sudo ifup eth1
sudo dhclient
```

cat /etc/modules /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist that gave me this:

```
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

lp
# This file lists those modules which we don't want to be loaded by
# alias expansion, usually so some other driver will be loaded for the
# device instead.

# evbug is a debug tool that should be loaded explicitly
blacklist evbug

# these drivers are very simple, the HID drivers are usually preferred
blacklist usbmouse
blacklist usbkbd

# replaced by e100
blacklist eepro100

# replaced by tulip
blacklist de4x5

# causes no end of confusion by creating unexpected network interfaces
blacklist eth1394

# snd_intel8x0m can interfere with snd_intel8x0, doesn't seem to support much
# hardware on its own (Ubuntu bug #2011, #6810)
blacklist snd_intel8x0m

# causes failure to suspend on HP compaq nc6000 (Ubuntu: #10306)
blacklist i2c_i801

```
Ok, try this: add **blacklist bcm43xx** to the end of /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist and your problem should be fixed.

---

### Post by mrdeeds2324 on 2007-01-02

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-03
Yep - in terminal do **gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist** and add stuff :D

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-03
> **kdlsibnis said:**
> Okay I just did my second install of Edgy on a PC, this time on a Presario V5000 Laptop. I have the Broadcom 4318 card and follow the tut pretty much to the letter. When I am in the desktop dir and I use sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup I get an error that says the command not found. I have the ndiswrapper_setup file sitting on the desktop. I just installed this version of Edgy and ran Synaptic. I tried the advnaced tutorial and blacklisted the set that it told me too. Rebooted multiple times and still getting the strange error. Its something blantently obvious I am betting, seeing as I can't seem to turn up the answer with searching. Thanks!
Hmm...well if you can see it you can double click on it and hit run in terminal - that should work.  If you don't get a option to press Run in Terminal then try opening terminal, going to desktop, and running **sudo chmod 755 ./ndiswrapper_setup** and then you should be able to run **sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup** ... I am willing to bet this is a permissions error.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-03
OK - run **sudo /etc/init.d/networking restart** and with some luck you should be connected...

### Post by shat on 2007-01-04
Actually, after a bit more fooling around, 

[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=265284](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=265284)

did the trick for me.  Ubuntu thought it was eth1, and ndiswrapper thought it was actually wlan0!  Now all that's left is rolling wpa_supplicant into the mix.  Thanks for your script!

shat

### Post by stealth17 on 2007-01-04
Well it didn't work at first but I searched the thread and found the answers to my problems.

I'm on an eMachines m5312 with the Broadcom BCM4312 wifi card. Your script did everything except comment out those lines in /etc/network/interfaces. Once I did that it worked like a charm!!!

### Post by trubblemaker on 2007-01-05
brandon moore  looks like you installed the wrong driver or still have bcm43xx unblacklisted, I'm going with wrong driver, if you have windows still installed, you can make it tell you the correct driver, otherwise it it's bcm4318 find the bcmwl5.inf and bcmwl5.sys that you need for your card.  (Check the broadcom website for them.)

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-06
Try in this order:
**sudo rmmod ndiswrapper**
**sudo rmmod bcm43xx**
**sudo modprobe ndiswrapper**

and see if that works

### Post by brandon moore on 2007-01-06
SUCCESS!! I figured out what was happening...

I installed the new headers and image from punkforjesus.com each time I tried this with a new install. I also compiled and installed ndiswrapper each time. The problem was..... when I ran the script after installing the new ndiswrapper, the script installed a different ndiswrapper over the top of it.

This time, to get it to work, I re-re-installed the new ndiswrapper, uninstalled the driver, then reinstalled the driver and ***voila***! it's now working and i'm writing this post from my wiereless connection.

Thanks so much for all your help!

---

  Allrighty,  i haven't felt this frustrated since trying to get my ac97 sound card to work in breezy... ;)  Ok, I have a hp pav 5000 series, with the AMD turion64 and the poopy 4318...  I have followed the links at the beginning of the thread... including upgrading to the newer 18.5 kernel, as it was mentioned that the 17.10 and the 64bit system don't get along.  I havent been able to get ndiswrapper to work right, the only way I have even got it to "sort of" run was to cut and paste it into terminal...  Anyhow, after doing that, I no longer even have my wireless connection showing up in the network mismanager...  
  several little quirks that I seem to be having:
  I can get through step 5 but then get this:

> 
ben@ben-laptop:~$ cd ~/Desktop
ben@ben-laptop:~/Desktop$ tar -xf bcm4318*.tar.gz
ben@ben-laptop:~/Desktop$ sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup
Password:
sudo: ./ndiswrapper_setup: command not found
ben@ben-laptop:~/Desktop$ 




ok, so i cut and paste it into terminal, and get a whole lotta text, and my wireless dissapears from my network mgr.

heres the log:

> Log of echo Script version: 0.1.2 
Sat Jan  6 21:47:57 2007
#declare the variables
#############
#set arch to nothing if not 64bit, if it is 64bit it will be set to something (I could care less what it is)
arch=`uname -a | grep x86_64`
#get the md5 of the version file
issue=`cat /etc/issue | md5sum`
#get only the md5, cut the file name off the end
distromd5=`expr substr "$issue" 1 32`

#start with no distro detected, for obvious reasons - we haven't detected it yet
distro="None"

originaldirectory=`pwd`

#the version
scriptversion="0.1.2"

#############
#    NOTES
#############
#
#	The output variable is used only to prevent the programs from writing to the standard out,
#	which makes it look nicer.  The reason I use the md5sum of the /etc/issue file to determine
#	the release is because it is a weird file (it has some end of line chars that I don't like),
#	and because it is easier then using regexp to figure it out.
#
#	This was written Saturday, October 28th, 2006, and has been updated numerous times since then.
#

echo "Starting ndiswrapper install..."

echo "If you have any package managers open (i.e. Adept or Synaptic or apt-get),"
echo "Please close them now."
echo "Pausing for 10 seconds - please read the message."
sleep 10

#write the script version to the logfile before we start
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "Script version: $scriptversion"`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log lspci | grep Broadcom`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "/etc/issue MD5 sum: $distromd5"`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log cat /etc/issue"`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log ps -e"`

#BEGIN DETECTING DISTRO

#edgy devel release
if [ "$distromd5" = "6d47f51c25ec07127e86be15a73c6a9d" ]; then
	logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "Edgy development release detected."
	echo "You really should upgrade to the final release...but it is your computer."
	echo "Installation will continue normally."
	distro="Edgy"
fi

#edgy final release
if [ "$distromd5" = "11046fbe3787d2ba4fc5821d5a41617e" ]; then
	logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "Edgy final release detected."
	distro="Edgy"
fi

#dapper final release
if [ "$distromd5" = "3cb50a593ad7f171a5aa9f5803354aa2" ]; then
	logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "Dapper final release detected."
	distro="Dapper"
fi

#for some reason, the distro did not match any of my md5s...so we will tell them that, and continue with the assumption that
#we are using Edgy
if [ "$distro" = "None" ]; then
	logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "Neither Ubuntu Dapper (6.06.1) nor Ubuntu Edgy (6.10) detected."
	echo "The script will continue, assuming that you are using Ubuntu Edgy (6.10)."
	echo "If you are *not* using Ubuntu Edgy, it may be a good idea to cancel this script right now, and install ndiswrapper right now, however you would go about doing that."
	echo "Pausing for 10 seconds - please read the message."
	sleep 10
	distro="Edgy"
fi

#DONE DETECTING DISTRO

#CHECK FOR BAD KERNEL - AMD64 WITH 2.6.17-10-generic

if [ -n "$arch" ]; then
	#64bit detected
	if [ "$distro" = "Edgy" ]; then
		kernel=`uname -r`
		if [ "$kernel" = "2.6.17-10-generic" ]; then
			output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "Aborted due to 2.6.17-10-generic kernel"`
			echo "WE HAVE DETECTED THAT YOU ARE USING EDGY EFT WITH AN"
			echo "********"
			echo "*INCOMPATIBLE* *64BIT* *KERNEL*"
			echo "********"
			echo "Please read:"
			echo "http://linux.punkforjesus.com/ndiswrapper/howto.htm"
			echo "(the link above can be clicked, so no need to retype)"
			echo "This is a bug, and a bug report has already been filed."
			echo "Aborting...please rerun after the problem is fixed."
			exit 1
		fi
	fi
fi

#DONE CHECKING FOR BAD KERNEL

#REMOVE MODULES THAT COULD CONFLICT

output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log rmmod ndiswrapper`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log rmmod bcm43xx`

#DONE REMOVING MODULES

#print distro to log - makes it easier
output=`logsave -a  ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo $distro`

#CHECK IF SOMETHING IS USING THE DIRECTORY



#INSTALL NDISWRAPPER

if [ "$distro" = "Dapper" ]; then
	echo "Installing ndiswrapper-utils..."
	output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log apt-get --assume-yes install ndiswrapper-utils`
	echo "Done..."
fi

if [ "$distro" = "Edgy" ]; then
	echo "Installing ndiswrapper-1.8..."
	output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log apt-get --assume-yes install ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils-1.8`
	echo "Done..."
fi

#DONE INSTALLING NDISWRAPPER

#REMOVE PREVIOUS DRIVERS THAT MAY CONFLICT

logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5a
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log ndiswrapper -l`

#DONE REMOVING PREVIOUS DRIVERS

#INSTALL THE DRIVERS

#move to a temp directory so we don't make a mess all over your desktop
mkdir /tmp/bcm4318-setup
mv drivers-32.tar.gz /tmp/bcm4318-setup
mv drivers-64.tar.gz /tmp/bcm4318-setup
cd /tmp/bcm4318-setup

#check to see if $arch is nothing
#the extract drivers depending on which one got picked
if [ -n "$arch" ]; then
	#64bit detected
	logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "You appear to have a 64bit system...installing the 64bit drivers..."
	output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log tar -xf drivers-64.tar.gz`
	echo "Done..."
else
	#non-64bit detected
	logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log echo "You appear to have an i386 system...installing the i386 drivers..."
	output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log tar -xf drivers-32.tar.gz`
	echo "Done..."
	
fi

#log the contents of the current directory for trouble shooting purposes
#maybe someone isn't in the same directory as the drivers, this can cause problems
#but I'm going to comment it out cause some people probably don't want the contents
#of their desktop published to the internet
#output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log ls`

#this command should work on all arches
echo "Adding drivers to ndiswrapper..."
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf`
echo "Done..."

cd $originaldirectory

#DONE INSTALLING DRIVERS

#MODPROBE NDISWRAPPER
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log modprobe ndiswrapper`
#DONE MODPROBING

#PERMANENTLY SET MODULES ENABLED/DISABLED
#blacklist bcm43xx
echo "#module below does not work with Broadcom 4318 wireless cards" >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
echo "blacklist bcm43xx" >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
#make ndiswrapper always load on startup
echo "ndiswrapper" >> /etc/modules
#DONE PERMANENTLY SETTING THINGS UP

#GATHER SOME BASIC INFO FOR THE LOG, FOR TROUBLE SHOOTING PURPOSES

echo "Gathering wireless card information..."
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log iwconfig`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log ifconfig`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log iwlist eth1 scan`
output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log iwlist wlan0 scan`
echo "Done..."

#DONE GATHERING INFO

echo ""
echo ""
echo "----------------------------------"
echo ""
echo ""

#INSTALL SOME SORT OF WIRELESS MANAGER
clear
echo "You should probably install an wireless connection manager"
echo "The best choices are"
echo "			network-manager OR wifi-radar"
echo "Strengths/weaknesses of network-manager:"
echo "+ Is able to use WPA, WEP, or any other kind of standard encryption"
echo "+ Easy to change networks"
echo "- Asks for keyring password on boot to connect to encrypted networks"
echo "- No network profiles, or at least you are unable to see them"
echo "- Is impossible to connect to a wired and wireless network at the same time"
echo "- No static IPs"
echo "- Network strength does not work"
echo ""
echo "Strengths/weakness of wifi-radar:"
echo "+ Works 99% of the time"
echo "+ Static IPs or DHCP"
echo "+ Supports WEP"
echo "+ Network strength sort of works"
echo "+ Doesn't need password at boot"
echo "+ Able to connect to wired and wireless at the same time"
echo "- No WPA"
echo ""
echo "After you have connected to the internet,"
echo "I would recommend installing one of these programs."
echo "To install network-manager, open Synaptic (Ubuntu) or Adept (Kubuntu)"
echo "and find the package network-manager-gnome, and install it."
echo "To install wifi-radar, open Synaptic (Ubuntu) or Adept (Kubuntu)"
echo "and find the package wifi-radar, and install it."

#DONE INSTALLING WIRELESS MANAGER

#CHMOD THE LOG SO PEOPLE CAN DELETE IT
chmod 777 ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log
#DONE CHMOD

#DONE WITH ALL
#PRINT THE FINISHED MESSAGE
echo ""
echo ""
echo "----------------------------------"
echo ""
echo ""
echo "Script completed."
echo "If your wireless light is on, give your internet a shot."
echo "If you are interested in install a connection manager, please"
echo "scroll up and read the pros and cons of a couple."
echo "You will have to connect to use the internet now."
echo "This can be accomplished (in GNOME) by"
echo "going to System / Administration / Networking,"
echo "and configuring the wireless card by selecting the network name."
echo "If something is not working, please try rebooting."
echo "If you need more help, please post ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log on"
echo "the forum thread where you downloaded it from for help."


Script version: 0.1.2

Sat Jan  6 21:47:57 2007
----------------
Log of lspci 
Sat Jan  6 21:47:57 2007

00:00.0 Host bridge: ATI Technologies Inc RS480 Host Bridge (rev 01)
00:01.0 PCI bridge: ATI Technologies Inc RS480 PCI Bridge
00:05.0 PCI bridge: ATI Technologies Inc Unknown device 5a37

### Post by brandon moore on 2007-01-06
ben, did you set the "ndiswrapper_setup" file to executable? if not, it's easy to do. go into the folder where ndiswrapper_setup exists, right-click on it, go to properties. when to window opens, click on the permissions tab, and check the box by "Allow executing file as program". Close the window, then try to run "sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup" again.

### Post by brandon moore on 2007-01-07

### Post by Ginigrace on 2007-01-07
Brandon,

I am an absolute newbie with Linux and not the best with computers! When my HP Pavilion dv1000 quit running, rather than reinstalling Windows XP and looking forward to buying and installing Vista and a whole bunch of other expensive programs, I bought a how-to book with a Ubuntu 6.06 disk and installed it. Everything thing seemed to work, but my wireless internet connection. 

I followed your directions for configuring the BCM4318 and the light came on indicating I had the wireless device configured properly, but I still couldn't pull up a web page. I read the terminal screen which suggested rebooting if the light was on and the wireless was still not working. I rebooted and am sending this message using the wireless connection!

Thanks so much for your help! I'm looking forward to this new computer experience!

G

### Post by jbonll05 on 2007-01-07
GREAT INSTRUCTIONS;
   Being user I needed help from our opensourceHouston.org lug group. "Chuck" read your tutorial and did the work. My HP/Comp nx6110 came up ready to go and on restart it connected. It has worked in Houston and here on Lake Livingston. Nice work sir. 
jbonll05

---

### Post by Ginigrace on 2007-01-09
Double thanks! I successfully followed the directions for establishing wireless Internet for Dapper which I had just installed from a disk in a "how-to book." Realizing there was a newer version and lots of security patches, etc. for Dapper I decided to download and install Edgy. I then had to repeat the same process to get the Broadcom 4318 wireless card to work again, except this time I also had to use the command: 

sudo apt-get install ndiswrapper-utils-1.8 ndiswrapper-utils-1.1 ndiswrapper-utils ndiswrapper-common 

and insert the installation disk I had created to install Edgy. Wonders of wonders I now have wireless Internet access again!

As an additional benefit, I now know something (very little of course) about terminal and some other aspects of this operating system which is totally new to me, a somewhat technically challenged person!

Thanks again!

G

---

Ben, yes, run "ndiswrapper -m" as sudo. See what "dmesg | grep ndis" gives you after that.


EDIT: Just noticed you're a fellow North Carolinian! I'm near Shelby/Gastonia area at Gardner-Webb. Glad to see you here!

After I ran the script for the first time on a brand new 6.10 Edgy install (only thing I did was download and install the 2.6.19.1 Kernel from the Kernel link on the first page) , the card was no longer in System->Administration->Networking. My system says that the driver is installed, but it doesn't show up there.

After I installed Network Manager If I try to click on the icon that should be for my wireless connection it gives me the error message of "SIOCGIFFLAGS error: No such device".

nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ sudo iwlist scan
Password:
lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth1      Interface doesn't support scanning.

nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ iwconfig
lo        no wireless extensions.

eth1      no wireless extensions.

nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ ifconfig
eth1      Link encap:Ethernet  HWaddr 00:E0:B8:90:22:21  
          inet addr:192.168.0.102  Bcast:192.168.0.255  Mask:255.255.255.0
          inet6 addr: fe80::2e0:b8ff:fe90:2221/64 Scope:Link
          UP BROADCAST RUNNING MULTICAST  MTU:1500  Metric:1
          RX packets:4513 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 frame:0
          TX packets:3349 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 carrier:0
          collisions:0 txqueuelen:1000 
          RX bytes:3440063 (3.2 MiB)  TX bytes:599716 (585.6 KiB)
          Interrupt:18 

lo        Link encap:Local Loopback  
          inet addr:127.0.0.1  Mask:255.0.0.0
          inet6 addr: ::1/128 Scope:Host
          UP LOOPBACK RUNNING  MTU:16436  Metric:1
          RX packets:2 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 frame:0
          TX packets:2 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 carrier:0
          collisions:0 txqueuelen:0 
          RX bytes:100 (100.0 b)  TX bytes:100 (100.0 b)

nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ dmesg | grep ndis
nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ ndiswrapper -l
Installed drivers:
bcmwl5          driver installed, hardware present 
nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ cat /etc/network/interfaces
auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

auto eth0
iface eth0 inet dhcp

auto eth1
iface eth1 inet dhcp

auto eth2
iface eth2 inet dhcp

auto ath0
iface ath0 inet dhcp

auto wlan0
iface wlan0 inet dhcp

nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ lspci | grep Broadcom
05:02.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller (rev 02)
nibs@nibs-laptop:~$ sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
FATAL: Module ndiswrapper not found.


Any help or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

The only thing I notice is that your /etc/network/interfaces should have every device commented out except the lo. It should look more like this:

```

auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

#auto eth0
#iface eth0 inet dhcp

#auto eth1
#iface eth1 inet dhcp

#auto eth2
#iface eth2 inet dhcp

#auto ath0
#iface ath0 inet dhcp

#auto wlan0
#iface wlan0 inet dhcp
```

Edit that file as sudo, making the changes I suggested, restart and see what happens. I have not contacted Broadcom to complain... not sure about anyone else. The main tarball - bcm4318all.tar.gz - has both 32 and 64 bit drivers. I got it working with the 64 bit on my Turion 64 machine with a little work, and with the 32 bit on the same machine with *much* less work.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-11
Nope, use the link that it says you should download, not anything in the attachment.  It should be bcm4318.all.tar.gz, and should work on Dapper/Edgy 32/64.  However, on Edgy 64 you may get a message telling you to visit a site to fix a problem, follow that link and do those steps.

edit: btw, what cards are you using?  apparently the ubuntuguide website put this tutorial up there for ANY Broadcom card, not just the 4318s...

You didn't have your Ubuntu CD in, did you?

If you did, install everything starting with ndiswrapper in Synaptic (System -> Administration -> Synaptic Package Manager) and then run the script again.

### Post by ddom87 on 2007-01-12
> **compwiz18 said:**
> Looks like you should be all set - did you try pushing the button on the comp?

yea i did i hit the "fn" key plus f2 which its alternate function is to enable wireless and the light will not come on. 


This is the only problem i have with ubuntu at all. i can get all the programs i need to install smoothly and everything is there for me. Lol but when it comes to trying to get my wireless to work it seems i am SOL sometimes. Only thing i have needed help on. 

Anything else i should try. ?

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-12
> **ddom87 said:**
> yea i did i hit the "fn" key plus f2 which its alternate function is to enable wireless and the light will not come on. 


This is the only problem i have with ubuntu at all. i can get all the programs i need to install smoothly and everything is there for me. Lol but when it comes to trying to get my wireless to work it seems i am SOL sometimes. Only thing i have needed help on. 

Anything else i should try. ?
Sure.

```

sudo rmmod bcm4318
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo ndiswrapper -m
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo iwlist scan
sudo uname -a

```

and post the results, please.
obviously, if the light comes on, you don't need to post the results.

---

### Post by nibsa1242 on 2007-01-12
So I finally got it working... except that I didn't do it using Ubuntu I downloaded Linux Mint Bea [http://lt.k1011.nutime.de/](http://lt.k1011.nutime.de/) which is 98% Ubuntu anyway. Then I proceeded to follow the instructions here: [http://lt.k1011.nutime.de/forum/viewtopic.php?t=458](http://lt.k1011.nutime.de/forum/viewtopic.php?t=458) . While I realize that these instructions ** will not work in Ubuntu ** as they are written, since Ubuntu doesn't have a System->Administration->Windows Wireless Drivers. Perhaps someone with more technical knowledge than I have will be able to figure out why it works and make it work for Ubuntu as well. Also I think prior to following the steps I had done a sudo rmmod bcm43xx, don't know if that'll need to be done as well.

BTW this works simply running from the live cd for me, thus no need to actually install MINT to find out if it'll work for you if you'd like to try Mint.

I used the drivers from: [ftp://ftp.compaq.com/pub/softpaq/sp33001-33500/sp33008.exe](ftp://ftp.compaq.com/pub/softpaq/sp33001-33500/sp33008.exe)  and extracted them with cabextract my card worked with the bcmwl5.inf driver from that file.

---

### Post by Rule on 2007-01-12
the "Windows Wireless Drivers" is the "ndisgtk" package which you can install in synaptic.

---

### Post by ddom87 on 2007-01-12
> **compwiz18 said:**
> Sure.

```

sudo rmmod bcm4318
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo ndiswrapper -m
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo iwlist scan
sudo uname -a

```

and post the results, please.
obviously, if the light comes on, you don't need to post the results.

it worked .. Thank you very much. I tried that before and it never worked. lol. But i guess something kicked in this time. the light doesnt stay solid but it blinks as it sends packets.. so its good now strength is 100%. lol. 

It scanned the network it found it i switched it to connect to eth1 and it went perfectly. 

thank you again.

---

### Post by nibsa1242 on 2007-01-12
Thanks. Its no longer working for me anyway. The native driver is touchy with this card, so back to ndiswrapper it is... hopefully I can get it working.

> **Rule said:**
> the "Windows Wireless Drivers" is the "ndisgtk" package which you can install in synaptic.

### Post by nibsa1242 on 2007-01-12
And two mins later, its working. I would suggest to anyone having issues to see if they resolve by using the 32 bit version of Ubuntu rather than the 64.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-12
Wait wait - question.  Are you using the 32bit or the 64bit?  And native or ndiswrapper?

If you are using 64bit with native, may I have a copy of the drivers you used?  I'd like to use native as well, that is the only way to do packet sniffing... :P

### Post by nibsa1242 on 2007-01-14
> **compwiz18 said:**
> Wait wait - question.  Are you using the 32bit or the 64bit?  And native or ndiswrapper?

If you are using 64bit with native, may I have a copy of the drivers you used?  I'd like to use native as well, that is the only way to do packet sniffing... :P

I'm using 32bit with ndiswrapper because its the only way I could get it working. I tried 64 with native and it didn't work, I tried 64bit with ndiswrapper and it was a no go, I tried 32bit with native and that was extremely touchy (could connect every now and then). Finally, I tried 32bit with ndiswrapper and it worked like a charm, I even use WPA-PSA. Due to my slow highspeed connection (1.5Mbps/256kbps) I see no difference using the web between wire and wireless connections (max dl speed hovers in the range of 210Kb/sec).

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-15
> **mupets_revenge said:**
> Having some trouble with this all it seams to have done is made the card disappear all together, im running i386 edgy


try following the instructions again - it would appear that something went wrong during the **tar -xf bcm4318.all.tar.gz** step.

---

### Post by vrattsild on 2007-01-15
Hello, I'm a complete newbie to Linux. I'm having the same problems as others with the Broadcom chipset in my Dell 1370 wireless card. I've read a lot of threads on this subject and tried a lot of things, but to no avail.  I would really appreciate some insight into the problem I could be facing. The wireless light does not come on, and attempting to turn it on manually using Fn+F2 keypress does not do anything (I get 'invalid keycode' messages when I try 'dmesg'). 

My computer is a Dell Inspiron 6000 with a 1.6GHz Pentium M type processor. I've installed Edgy Eft and everything is working perfectly except the wireless -- which is really important for me.

As per the instructions in the first post, I installed Ubuntu from the CD, and then proceeded directly to run the script. Here are the logs:

### Post by Win2Mac2Linux on 2007-01-16
Hello!!!  I'm new to the Linux seen and stumbling my way along, but not complaining.  I don't mind trying to learn new things, It's just frustrating trying to make your way around new territory so...

I followed the steps and installed the gnome network manager.  It shows my wired connection, but nothing about wireless.  Also, when I go to the System>Admiinistration>Networking, it shows the wired and modem options, but the wireless option is no longer there.  Don't know what happened with that.  I also did the steps just after reinstalling the OS.  Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.

Aluminum Powerbook (v5.9) 17" G4
Intermediate level end user (mostly Windows, some OSX), but with the real techie stuff, well...

I noticed in the logs it makes reference to this being an i386 system and installed i386 drivers.  I don't know if that has anything to do with this since I have PPC laptop. ### Post by Augustus on 2007-01-16
I used this guide with my last Edgy install to get my wireless working...it was great.

However, I recently did a clean install, and now it doesn't work.  I think I've narrowed it down to the reason:  wireless doesn't even appear in the "system -> administration -> Networking" connections tab.  It just, isn't there.  I know I have the hardware, and when I run "lspci | grep Broadcom\ Corporation", I get my broadcom card, the same one that is being referred to in this tutorial.  Anyone have any ideas?  I'd really appreciate the help.

---

### Post by domer on 2007-01-18
> **The Raven said:**
> Here are some compiled logical steps that I retrieved from several posts in this thread and a few other places regarding the identification, maintenance, and installation of wifi hardware and/or firmware:


 Steps:

---------------------------------------------------------------
    Conventions:

	  UN = your user name 
	  CN = your computer's name.

	  Notes: = side-bar elaboratives to explain a step
	  Warning: = Warnings are just that; you had better read them.


	Tip:

          You do not retype your UN@CN, just the code after
          the $.

	  UN@CN:~$ and UN@CN:~/Desktop$ are place holders merely
	  referencing that you should see a prompt in the terminal's
          window where UN is your user account's name @ your computer's
	  name, both of which were assigned when you installed and 
	  configured your Linux O.S. distro.


 Warning: Do the steps in order and stick to the 
          procedure, don't add any steps unless you know
          what it is that you are doing here.
---------------------------------------------------------------

---------------------------------------------------------------

	1.)Open your terminal (obviously).

---------------------------------------------------------------

	2.)UN@CN:~$ lspci | grep Broadcom\ Corporation
	  	A.)Press the Return/Enter key



 You should now see the following output return in your terminal's display:

     0000:05:02.0 Network controller:Broadcom Corporation BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g
     Wireless LAN Controller (rev 02)


 If this is what your terminal command returns in the display you have nothing to worry about.

 Proceed to the next step.

---------------------------------------------------------------

	4.)UN@CN:~$ echo blacklist bcm43xx | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key


 Your terminal will process the command and notify you that the 43xx driver file has been
successfully 'blacklisted'.


Warning:
	This will 'blacklist' an older version of the 43 driver series known as 43xx, which is 
	pre-installed with UBUNTU Dapper Drake and possibly other Debian based Linux Distros. 
	The 43xx driver will conflict with some other drivers in the 43xx genre. A lot of problems
	concerning oddly behaving cards could have something to do with the exclusion of this 
	step, so just do it!
	
	It's better to be safe than sorry.

----------------------------------------------------------------

	5.)If you haven't already done so, go ahead and move your 'bcm4318[1].tar.gz' package
		to your desktop (a.k.a.- shell, be it Gnome ,KDE; etc.)

----------------------------------------------------------------

	6.)UN@CN:~$ cd /home/UN/Desktop
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key



 You should now see the following output return in your terminal's display:


     UN@CN:~/Desktop$


 This output is telling you that the Desktop directory is your active directory and all actions
will be initiated starting at this point. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------

	Note: It works the same way a browser navigates through a web site. 
    (Investigate this topic on your own as it is beyond the scope of this block of instruction.)

	The UN@CN:~/Desktop$ is your new base directory that you will be working out of. It stream-
	lines the process of file access. If you are going to working with a lot of files on your 
	desktop you make the Desktop your base directory and the terminal will assume on your behalf
	that you will be doing all of your work in the Desktop "directory." This saves you time, in 
	that, you do not have to write out the complete file path to every object on your desktop, just
	a command of execution and the file that the command will take action(s) upon.

------------------------------------------------------------------

	7.)UN@CN:~/Desktop$ tar -xf bcm4318[1].tar.gz
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key


 You should now see the output return in your terminal's display stating that the 'bcm4318[1].tar.gz'
package is effectively being decompressed and extracted to the desktop. Once the terminal notifies you 
that the process of extraction was successfully completed and you see the prompt:

	  UN@CN:~/Desktop$

 ...proceed to the next step.

-------------------------------------------------------------------

	8.)UN@CN:~/Desktop$ sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key

 You will now witness the installation execution step by step as it is carried out automatically by the
terminal. When the terminal returns a message stating that the installation was successfully completed
you will be queried again with the terminal prompt:

	  UN@CN:~/Desktop$

 ...proceed to the next step.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

	9.)System menu on your Desktop toolbar at the top of the screen,then:

	   Administration > Networking, click the wireless connection of the 'Connections' tab and click
	   the 'Properties' button. Click the "Enable This Connection check Box Radio Button", set your
	   ESSID id to the active network name that you will use to access your router for wifi, and set 
	   your configuration to DHCP to automatically obtain IP addresses, just like in Windows.

 Warning:

	Make sure that your wireless lan is activated, only after inspecting the properties of your
	wlan connection setup properties, this will prevent wasted time, stupid questions, and people 
	feeling stupid for asking stupid questions because they didn't pay attention to detail.


	   Setting up WEP is simple, but you can consult your router's user/set-up documentation for the
	   exact specifications of performing such a procedure.

		A.)Click "OK" 
		
		...and wait for the process to complete the update to your configuration settings.



	   I have a Linksys 54G Wireless Router myself, and I use the router default for wifi which is
	   "linksys," without the quotations, the ethernet ESSID is "Linksys."

---------------------------------------------------------------------

	10.)Click "OK" of your "Network Settings" dialog window.
	  
		...and wait for the process to finalize your configuration settings.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

	11.) Open your browser and surf to a web page like Google.

		Click the Google search text entry field, in the upper right corner of Firefox, and press
			the Retern/Enter key on your key-board and wait for Firefox to open Google's default 
			Home Page.

		Close your browser and proceed to the next step...

---------------------------------------------------------------------

	12.)UN@CN:~/Desktop$ ndiswrapper -l
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key



 	Your terminal should return the following output in it's display:
 

		bcmwl5        driver present, hardware present

 
 	At the terminal prompt:


		UN@CN:~/Desktop$

	...proceed to the next step.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

	13.)UN@CN:~/Desktop$ sudo -i
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key
		B.)Key in your administrator password
		C.)Press the Return/Enter key

	14.)UN@CN:~/Desktop# modprobe ndiswrapper
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key

 Now push the wifi network connect/disconnect button on your computer...

	- it should come on/light up, or turn off as the case may be. If
	  you turned it off, turn it back on now.

	15.)UN@CN:~/Desktop# ifdown eth1
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key

	16.)UN@CN:~/Desktop# ifup eth1
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key

	17.)UN@CN:~/Desktop# dhclient
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key

 	18.)UN@CN:~/Desktop# iwconfig
		A.)Press the Return/Enter key
		B.)Verify that the output displays
			the correct settings for
			your wifi network.

	19.)Close the Terminal utility, and browse/surf as desired.



 indicis: compwiz18
	     naught 101

P.S. Let me know how it works, as this will aid me in correcting and/or adding info to help out Compwiz18 in getting you all on-line effectively.





It worked perfectly fine for me. But at the very end (when the device and the driver were detected, I had to refer to this site [http://ccsd.msoe.edu/faq/linux/Ubuntu.jsp?IDFaq=223](http://ccsd.msoe.edu/faq/linux/Ubuntu.jsp?IDFaq=223) for the final step). 

Thanks a lot. Your post helped me a lot :) 


Adi

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-19
> **CalaverX11 said:**
> The 2.6.19-1 kernel doesn't include ndiswrapper.ko. I can't do anything with this kernel regarding ndiswrapper. Did I miss something?
Yep - gotta compile ndiswrapper your self.

Instructions: [http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/bcm4318/howto.htm](http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/bcm4318/howto.htm)
Read Downloading Required Files, and download _just_ the ndiswrapper one, not the kernel stuff.
Then skip to number 6 and 7 (Extract ndiswrapper and Compile ndiswrapper) under Installing and Compiling. Now exit that page, and reboot.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-19
> **bilbobagins said:**
> Hi  just to say thanks for the howto followed the recipe and it "just worked" now have 100 % signal strength from a miserable 7-10% with the old linksys card. and 50 M/Bits Thanks):P
No, actually you don't :D Ubuntu's version of ndiswrapper is a bit buggy, and will always report 100% strength - if you don't want that, then compile the new version:

Instructions: [http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/bcm4318/howto.htm](http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/bcm4318/howto.htm)
Read Downloading Required Files, and download _just_ the ndiswrapper one, not the kernel stuff.
Then skip to number 6 and 7 (Extract ndiswrapper and Compile ndiswrapper) under Installing and Compiling. Now exit that page, and reboot.

---

### Post by ljamie82 on 2007-01-19
Ok I have what could be VERY important information.

I, like a large minority, failed to get the internet to work by following this procedure.  After trying again and again (I know I did it correctly, I'm not a complete newb), I decided to try another distro to see if this was an Ubuntu issue.

Well I installed Mandriva 2007 and used the gui to configure ndiswrapper and boom!  100% signal strength!  Now I'm not convinced this is an ubuntu issue just yet.  One of the prompts told me it had to install wpa_supplicant ... i never bothered to install that because I don't use wpa encryption.  The guide did not say to install it either.  But for some reason mandriva found it necessary.

If you've followed the guide and still can't get it to work, try installing wpa_supplicant (i think that's the way it's spelled).  One thing to note though, I did get the driver installed with ndiswrapper in Ubuntu... I was one of the few that when I typed iwlist scan I saw networks, just couldn't connect, even when I turned off wep on the router. 

I look forward to hearing how this works for someone... in the meantime, i'm sticking with what works.

---

### Post by feed_sparky on 2007-01-20
OK, i realized my mistake...I didn't have ndiswrapper installed when I ran the script! :) So now I can see my card in the network settings and can see my network but I have no signal strength and I can't connect

EDIT:
OK, it works! Finally! For some reason I had to change the encryption from Hex to Plain (ASCII) even though I have no ecryption right now... when I did this my card connected immediatly

---

### Post by wncben on 2007-01-20
GRRRRRR!





I tried this again on a brand spanking fresh install of edgy and still no sucess, it seems like the only thing I have accomplished is to once again lose the wireless connection from network manager.   I tried installing wifi radar with no sucess....

  I wonder if this has something to do with HP "upgrading" the firmware for the wifi card during routine upgrades of drivers in windows?  

Anyhow,  did fresh install of edgy, changed the kernel, (as I am running an hp dv5000 series amd turion64 laptop) and ran the script was still unable to connect, so I followed the troubleshooting, tips, after that (I think it was at the point of commenting out the non "lo" lines in /etc/network/interfaces)  network manager stopped seeing my wifi card, leaving me with just the ethernet and modem connections.....

### Post by feed_sparky on 2007-01-21
wcnben,

That was the same thing I was going through, then I realized I never installed ndiswrapper ](*,)  I was getting the same error messages. Go to synaptic and search for ndiswrapper (make sure you have universe repositories enabled) and install it.

I hope this helps...

(I am not very good at explaining this because I am a linux newbie :D )

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-21
> **wncben said:**
> GRRRRRR!

I tried this again on a brand spanking fresh install of edgy and still no sucess, it seems like the only thing I have accomplished is to once again lose the wireless connection from network manager.   I tried installing wifi radar with no sucess....

  I wonder if this has something to do with HP "upgrading" the firmware for the wifi card during routine upgrades of drivers in windows?  

Anyhow,  did fresh install of edgy, changed the kernel, (as I am running an hp dv5000 series amd turion64 laptop) and ran the script was still unable to connect, so I followed the troubleshooting, tips, after that (I think it was at the point of commenting out the non "lo" lines in /etc/network/interfaces)  network manager stopped seeing my wifi card, leaving me with just the ethernet and modem connections.....

Here's the log:

I am at a complete loss as to what to try now.....

several other folks on this thread have similar problems as far as having network manager drop the wireless after running the script, or the troubleshooting steps....   Unfortunately I don't have the ubuntu kungfu to figure this out, am just glad i am not the ONLY one this is happening to,  ;^)

ok: can you run **uname -a** in the terminal and show me the output?  thanks!

### Post by wncben on 2007-01-21
according to synaptic and the script I have the newest ndiswrapper installed..... but thanks sparky, I wish it was as easy as that....  (it prolly is, but I don't have the kungfu to figure it out either,  somewhat of a long term noob here m'self)

compwiz, here's the printout from uname -a:

```
ben@ben-laptop:~$ uname -a
Linux ben-laptop 2.6.18.5 #1 SMP Fri Dec 8 22:20:58 JST 2006 x86_64 GNU/Linux
ben@ben-laptop:~$ 



```
 
I know that there has to be a way to get this dang card to work, and I look forward to be able to add my name to the "yes" list in the poll ;)


any thoughts on the firmware?

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-21
wncben: Remove everything starting with ndiswrapper from Synaptic, then go to [http://sourceforge.net/project/showfiles.php?group_id=93482](http://sourceforge.net/project/showfiles.php?group_id=93482) and download the latest copy of ndiswrapper, then go to [http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/bcm4318/howto.htm](http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/bcm4318/howto.htm) and start at number 5 under Installing and Compiling.

---

  Network manager will show the other networks available, and show if they are protected or not, and will attempt to connect, but is not actually able to...


  This is exciting, since I used fwcutter, i have activity from my wifi card, i think perhaps i either need to tweak a setting somewhere, or perhaps use a different driver, since it seems like the computer thinks I have the bcm43xx driver... ### Post by wncben on 2007-01-24
HEY COMPWIZ,


   I have wifi working!   YAY!   However, I had to do some other tweaking, and, while I am writing this unhindered by wires, there are a few issues, that I think, as you seem to have a better understanding of the process than i do, we can hopefully work out, and have a working setup for those with the amd64 an d the 4318 that the other howtoos haven't helped.

   I have network mgr working somewhat, while it doesn't seem to want to scan for available networks, it let me add them manually, then it sees them.  the only real malfunction is that my touchpad is acting a little wonkey, which I think is due to using the bcm43XX driver.

  What I did:

   Fresh install of ubuntu
   Kernel 2.6.19.2
   ndiswrapper 1.34
   fwcutter
   wl apsta.o driver
   this got my card up
   then I commented out the lines in etc,networking
   now I am up AND running....   (sort of)  lol
  I hope it is stable
lol

anyhow, if there are anysuggestions for how to tweak this to get my touchpad running right, perhaps a non bcm43xx driver to cut the fw from?  (as I am convinced that my problem came from hp screwing with the fw, and I think my current prob is a conflict between bcm43xx and the touchpad driver)

Also, if there is anything you'd like me to run and give you the output from, so you can see what my system is doing, let me know.

 I may try to do a fresh install of ubuntu, and try your howto again, and see if having loaded working fw into my card has fixed the problems I wah having...   

  It is frustrating to not have my scrolling and drag and click working on my touchpad

Thanks for all your help!

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-24
Awesome.

Could you do me a favor a post the wl_apsta.o driver here?  There are far to many of them to try to find the same one you have :D

```

lsmod | grep ndiswrapper
lsmod | grep bcm43xx
ndiswrapper -l
uname -a

```

I think that a lot of other people are having the same trouble you are, so you have the key to success for them.

Again, good job :D

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-01-25
Make sure you install the kernel headers.  These must be installed _before_ the image is, otherwise it doesn't work for some reason...

sorry if that is a bit confusing, ask and I can clarify.

---

### Post by mike.thorton on 2007-02-01
Thank you!!! I've just got my wifi (bcm 4318 ) on my nx6110 laptop finally working! I use Ubuntu 6.06 LTS, so:

Downloaded and ran the script above, Installed network-manager:

```
sudo apt-get install network-manager
```

Turned on the wireless with the wifi button and had to go to "networking" and activated the eth1 interface.

After logout the gnome-network applet appeared in my system bar and then I could see all the broadcasted ssids and connect to them! I've just connected to my wpa2 personal network and did nothing with wpasupplicant. Probably it's been already installed!

Ubuntu rulezz!! compwiz18 rulezz!! :D :D :D

### Post by wncben on 2007-02-03

### Post by wncben on 2007-02-03
OK, so I did a fresh instal, and ran your x64 howto, with kernel 2.6.19.2 and ndiswrapper 1.37 and am now wireless, though the little network icon on the top tray shows no connection. I had to pust the wifi button on keyboard to initially get light, then i had to set up the network under the admin menu, then reboot to get to this point.  I am convinced that the fw was the culprit, and that i needed to reset whatever HP did using fwcutter which both fixed the fw problem, and broke everything else, but allowed a fresh place to begin from.  I would recomend that if folks are running hp laptops and the howto did not work that they try using fwcutter to get the firmware from a current driver, and then they may have to reinstall, or somehow start fresh. but before I did the fw cutter thing I had a dead card, and on this reinstall i did everything the same as before, but have a working card.
  I am going to try network manager to see if i can manage connections.

to clarify, I did NOT use fw cutter on this reinstall, I think it reset the crappy hp fw and allowed the script and ndiswrapper to talk to the card.

---

### Post by Hans de Visser on 2007-02-08
Worked great! 
I installed Ubuntu 6.06 Dapper Drake on my Compaq Presario M2000 in october 2006.
I had to search a long time to find a way to get my wireless network connection working
At last I found a description on a debian forum (HowTo: Broadcom BCM43xx Wireless Card
, [http://forums.debian.net/viewtopic.php?t=7949](http://forums.debian.net/viewtopic.php?t=7949)) to get it

### Post by Interestedinthepenguin on 2007-02-08
@ mmceachern:  

I just fixed the same problem using these instructions: 


Type this in the terminal. 

```
iwconfig
```

Output should look something like this:

lo no wireless extensions.

eth0 no wireless extensions.

eth1 IEEE 802.11b/g ESSIDff/any Nickname:"Broadcom 4318"
Mode:Managed Access Point: Invalid
RTS thrff Fragment thrff
Link Quality:0 Signal level:0 Noise level:0
Rx invalid nwid:0 Rx invalid crypt:0 Rx invalid frag:0
Tx excessive retries:0 Invalid misc:0 Missed beacon:0

sit0 no wireless extensions.

```
sudo gedit /etc/iftab 
```

the file will open and you will see, (note your mac will be different)

eth0 mac 00:16:36:86:a7:08 arp 1
eth1 mac 00:14:a5:e8:4f:8f arp 1

change ethernet1 by commenting it out like this:

eth0 mac 00:16:36:86:a7:08 arp 1
#eth1 mac 00:14:a5:e8:4f:8f arp 1

Reboot.  

Source: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=332135](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=332135)

---

### Post by bswindell77 on 2007-02-14
[SIZE="2"]Worked for me, but please note:[/SIZE]

First off, the light doesn't stay lit like it does for windows... it works [SIZE="1"](Like I think it it suppose to, maybe bill couldn't figure it out)[/SIZE] only when it is up/downloading. Ingenious.

Network must me open or unsecured... at first.

Do not even attempt this script or howto, or any howto, if you are using a router with DD-WRT (or any other linux) firmware! 

After days and days and days.... I decided to revert my router back to the oem firmware just to see if that was what was causing a 29 year old man to cry like a little baby. And poof, (enter magic ferry dust and a tear) it worked!!

I did some tickering and found that no matter what I did, If the router had VxWare or DD-Wrt linux firmwares, It would only connect in windows. 

_Just so you know:_
[U]
Computer:[/U]
Dell B130 Inspiron                            
512mb RAM                                       
15.4 Widescreen                              
BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller (rev 02) 

_Ubuntu:_
6.10 (downloaded today 02/14/07) 
Fresh install Dual w/ WinXP
Did not Update [COLOR="Red"](Yet, Scared to try)[/COLOR]
Unpluged the wired @ reboot after script

_Me:_
Cigarette in mouth
Beer, still hidden outside, wife will catch me.
Full ashtray
And a Moutain Dew

Thank you for the help Compwiz18!! [SIZE="1"](my wife thanks you too, now I have to get back to house work.)[/SIZE]

One last thing, I know off subject


[COLOR="Red"]UPDATE:  partition that I was trying was not fat32..  it was CPM ... a Dell thing, but still fat32... just erased partition (lost data) and formated as fat32[/COLOR].
fixed: I still can't seem to access my fat32 partition that I made to share btwn xp and ubuntu?

### Post by hotshot23t on 2007-02-15
Hey I think I can help with your fat32 shared partition problem.  Can I have your output of **sudo vol_id -u /dev/hda4** if your shared drive is /dev/hda4.  If you don't know what it is, run **sudo fdisk -l** and find what it says under DEVICE BOOT next to the fat32.  Also post your fstab file.  (**sudo gedit /etc/fstab**)

### Post by wncben on 2007-02-17
Hey compwiz, I just wanted to thank you again for your help, my 4318 card works flawlessly in my amd64... the light stays on full time when the card is on, and goes off when i push the wifi button for what it's worth...
A little off topic but relavent since I think it may be an issue with the fix here:
 I was hoping I could pick your brains a little for some help with fglrx, as I am having issues I think since I used the 2.6.19.2 kernel, and have not got fglrx to load successfully yet... even glxgears logs down horribly... :( anyhow, I noticed you had fglrx on your kernel page for the 19.1 kernel, and was wondering if I should redo the howto here with the 19.1 kernel, or if the 19.1 fglrx would work with the 19.2 kernel... it seems whatever fglrx howto i've used seems to load the driver into the 17.11 kernel modules, and the 19.2 kernel isn't finding them.

Thanks~Ben

 I was having problems with both the Admin/networkin set up, as well as network manager, but wifi radar works flawlessly for me, the signal meter even works somewhat, and it finds signals that neither of the other ones would locate. ok I have gotten somewhere but stuck again. I did this..
```
sudo gedit /etc/network/interfaces
```
and changed it to look like this:
```
auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

#auto eth0
#iface eth0 inet dhcp

#auto eth1
#iface eth1 inet dhcp

#auto eth2
#iface eth2 inet dhcp

#auto ath0
#iface ath0 inet dhcp

#auto wlan0
#iface wlan0 inet dhcp


iface eth1 inet dhcp

auto eth1

iface eth0 inet dhcp
wireless-essid myweb

auto eth0

```

Now my card is available in the network manager and is also seen by iwconfig. I was running WPA but turned all of it off to eliminate any complications. I want to get it working first, then I'll add that stuff in. So far no luck at gaining an IP address, I also tried using wifi-radar with no luck.

--------------------------
Edit
--------------------------

After getting fustrated with this, I turned the comp off and came back to it today. Now I am getting an IP address but still not connecting... wierd.

### Post by OnkiDonki on 2007-02-22
Hello,
the maker of the driver 

[http://bcm43xx.berlios.de/?go=home]("http://bcm43xx.berlios.de/?go=home")

says, that the driver still works unstable with 4318.

Is there a bigger problem with the bcm4318? 
Can anyone tell how stable this bcm43xx driver works?
There is an other way to get that bcm4318 work.
You can also use "ndiswrapper" and the original windows driver!

Does anyone have experice how stable this alternativ way works???????

Bye

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-02-22
I used it when it worked.  It doesn't work for me anymore, but other people have had luck with it, and I recommend that you try it if you are interested.  There aren't really any benefits from it unless you want to packet sniff (which is what I wanted to do) - it runs at 11mbps instead of 54mbps like ndiswrapper does and is slightly less stable if you can actually get it to work.

Like I said though, if you are interested, I recommend you give it a go.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-02-22
@**Dark Cerberus**:  First, open Synaptic and remove all packages starting with ndiswrapper (do *mark for complete removal*).  Then try redownloading the file from the link on the front page and running it over again.

@**SuperTeece**:  If you are getting an IP, run **sudo route** and give me the output - sometimes it just doesn't know where to send stuff.

---

### Post by brandon moore on 2007-02-22
This How-To worked beautifully for me on my Edgy x86 install. I've since played around with Mepis 64-bit and was glad to discover that this card worked right out of the box. Mepis automatically blacklisted the bcm43xx driver and installed ndiswrapper with the bcmwl5 driver. The only thing that doesn't work at this point is WEP (not sure about WPA).

Also, the latest beta release of Mepis 64 I am using comes with flash pre-installed, so websites like YouTube are accessible on a 64-bit OS where flash is not typically available. I've been pretty impressed so far. Of course, Mepis comes with KDE as default, so Gnome may be the first thing you install after running it for the first time.

I'm not trying to be a Mepis evangelist or anything, I just wanted to let some others know about my luck with this card in another distro.

---

### Post by tarek on 2007-02-22
hi,

I'm using it and no problems at all. 

Before I knew about this driver, I had to install libraries, compile, download the windows driver and in the end no luck getting it to work.

I recommend trying it first and if it does not work, then try other ways such as the windows driver.

Just remember to reboot after the installation and try it in a fresh installation of edgy.

tk

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-02-22

> This How-To worked beautifully for me on my Edgy x86 install. I've since played around with Mepis 64-bit and was glad to discover that this card worked right out of the box. Mepis automatically blacklisted the bcm43xx driver and installed ndiswrapper with the bcmwl5 driver. The only thing that doesn't work at this point is WEP (not sure about WPA).

Also, the latest beta release of Mepis 64 I am using comes with flash pre-installed, so websites like YouTube are accessible on a 64-bit OS where flash is not typically available. I've been pretty impressed so far. Of course, Mepis comes with KDE as default, so Gnome may be the first thing you install after running it for the first time.

I'm not trying to be a Mepis evangelist or anything, I just wanted to let some others know about my luck with this card in another distro.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-02-23
@**simply.jonah**: Make sure you run
```
**sudo** ./ndiswrapper_setup
```
when you run step six.  From the log file, it looks like you may not have gotten the **sudo** in there.  Without the sudo part, the script doesn't have administrative privileges and can't install some of the required components.

---

### Post by futron on 2007-02-25
Thank you so much for this tutorial!! After searching through countless posts for the past five hours, I used the script that you provided and was instantly able to connect to my wireless network. The wireless light actually stays on now (it had been flickering erratically when I tried some of the other suggestions) and the signal bars have since shown up. Thanks for the help! BTW I'm using Edgy Eft with a Broadcom 4306 on a Pavilion zx5000 notebook.
