### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-02-28
> **Dracar said:**
> ok um i loked at that link, it led to the wiki page, and i am completly lost... and also,  what is edgy? cause idk if thats what i have or not. this is the file i dled and installed 
ubuntu-6.06.1-desktop-i386.iso.
OK, I'll try and help you out.

Based on the file you dled, you have Dapper, which is a version lower then Edgy, but has long term support and is more stable.

I found the link for Dapper: [http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu_dapper#How_to_add_extra_repositories](http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu_dapper#How_to_add_extra_repositories)

When you click, you should see "How to add extra repositories" at the top of the screen.  Follow the steps there, then go back to the first page of this thread and repeat all of the steps.  Good luck :D

### Post by Dracar on 2007-02-28
ok, il try that and then il get back to you.
ok lol i dont get any of this, i clicked the link with the pacage manager or sumthin, and im completly lost yet again. like it looks like it wants me to enter web sites into the terminal? and my pc cant connect to the internet yet...

so do i just do this and the wifi card shows up in networking again?
"
Settings -> Repositories 
In the Installation Media tab, click Add. There are three separate repositories; Dapper Drake, Security Updates and Updates. Select each repository and check Officially supported, Restricted copyright, Community maintained (Universe) and Non-free (Multiverse). Ensure you click OK between each repository to save your changes 
You should now see those three repositories under Channels. Make sure Officially supported, Restricted copyright, Community maintained (Universe) and Non-free (Multiverse) appears under each repository 
"

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-01
Ohhh... that's right.  No internet could be a slight problem, but not to worry.

Put the CD you installed Ubuntu with into the computer.
Open the package manager.
Find the entries starting with ndiswrapper
Select the ones that have the Ubuntu logo in front of them (if you aren't sure what the Ubuntu logo is, it is in the top left corner of this page)
Mark them for installation
Click apply
Rerun the script

Of course, if you have any trouble, feel free to ask.

---

1. boot into ubuntu
2. insert ubuntu cd (used livecd to install ubuntu)
3. open Pacage Manager (in administrator, or on cd?)
4. select any entries that have the ubuntu logo and start with ndiswrapper
5. install them
6. rerun the script(the one were i entered ndiswrapper or watever in the terminal?)

### Post by tjod on 2007-03-02
Looks like  I spoke too soon.  

This method (and the associated ones) worked to get my wireless working. 

ONCE.

When I disconnect, it won't start again until I reverse all the installation steps and then re-apply all the steps.   (i.e. uninstall the Ntwork Manager, etc.)

Needless to say that's not going to work as a long-term solution. 

So now the question remains ... how can the Broadcom 4318 Wireless be installed in Ubuntu so that it works when needed?  Is this a problem with with wireless in Ubuntu in general ? Or is it just Broadcom in the Acer TravelMate 2480?

Thanks in advance for any information!

Tim

---

### Post by steveyos666 on 2007-03-02
Why is it so hard for the Ubuntu team to get this stuff working? Are they not allowed to work with wireless cards because it's illegal like mp3s? :guitar:

---

### Post by tjod on 2007-03-02
I think it's more likely that the vendors want to concentrate on the Windows drivers and don't give a lot of care to the Linux  or Mac market.  Half the battle is prying info from the vendors.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-02
Yeah, it has partly to do with the "free" software policy...the drivers used in this process are the Windows version, so they aren't free and open source, which is Ubuntu's criteria.  Work on an open source driver is in progress, and it works now with mixed results.  Also, Broadcom drivers are completely closed source, so the open source version has to be reverse engineered.

@**tjod**: That's weird, although not totally uncommon.  Mine works every time I turn the computer on, apparently your's doesn't.  When you turn the computer on, try running **sudo rmmod bcm43xx** and **sudo rmmod ndiswrapper** and **sudo modprobe ndiswrapper** in that order.

@**steveyos666**: The version of Ubuntu shouldn't matter...  You are downloading the file in Windows, right?

---

### Post by tjod on 2007-03-02
I'll try that.

Does it need to be run each time I restart? If so, is there an equivalent in to the Windows Startup folder or a batch that can autorun? (I figure, what good is a computer if it can't do the repetitive stuff for you... :)  )

Thanks!

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-02
Using Network Manager?  There are other programs, some of which work better then Network Manager when it comes to WPA: wifi-radar (look in the package manager), wicd (the link in my signature)

just fyi, /etc/rc.local will run commands you put in it as root at boot (it rhymes!)

### Post by steveyos666 on 2007-03-03

### Post by tjod on 2007-03-03
I'm new at this too, but I'll explain it as I know it

The ndiswapper is *part* of a Windows driver that gets extracted and used to communicate to the Broadcom 4318 card. 

Why? because it's a relatively new card and few if any Linux-native dfrivers have been implemented so far. So, the Windows information can be leveraged to learn about the BC 34xx cards and develop drivers that will work in Ubuntu natively

---

yes I'm confused with the bcm34xx and the ndiswrapper I'm not sure if i got a wrong version of the ndiswrapper but when i install the broadcom drivers with ndiswrapper 
and then 
ndiswrapper -l

it shows that the driver is present and lists the PCI:ID for my card but it then says alternative driver:bcm34xx 

i also get that same problem when using Suse 10.2 but in 10.1 it was really easy with ndiswrapper everything went fine. the only problem i have with Suse is that it really sucks up my ram and is sometimes slowish so i wanted to try out a different Linux so i decided to pick Ubuntu.

Anyways i don't want to use the bcm34x drivers because it has been really annoying for me so i went a used the instructions on how to blacklist and also did rmmod bcm34xx. But yet again it still some how uses the bcm34xx and when i ndiswrapper -l

it says that the driver is present and lists the PCI:ID for my card but it then says alternative driver:bcm34xx 

so i was wondering if anyone could help and give me a tutorial on how to get it to work.

Thanks please reply

```

me@computer:~$ ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4318) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
me@computer:~$ 

```

is the output of mine (and it works), so it would appear the (alternate driver: bcm43xx) thing is normal.  Is your's not working?

### Post by joedoro on 2007-03-03
Wow - after days of trying I got my Gateway MX6427 that has the Broadcom 4318 chipset. working with Mint Linux - but did have to do a fresh reinstall.

### Post by kosson on 2007-03-03
BCM 4318 WiFi Card on Feisty Fawn Herd 4 x64
Kubuntu (KDE 3.5.6)
Acer Aspire 5101 ANWLMi

1.I've updated the standard installation (there were about 280 packages to update and there was a little trouble as I tried to upgrade with Adept Manager, better go in Konsole upgrade your 'su'per powers and apt-get -u dist-upgrade )
2.After upgrade 
uname -r returns 
2.6.20-9-generic
3.Install ndiswrapper
dpkg -i ndiswrapper-utils-1.9_1.30-1ubuntu1_amd64.deb ndiswrapper-common-1.30-1ubuntu1_all.deb
4.Install 64bit Win driver - version 4.40.19.0 &#8211; (Thanks to Fedora guys [http://fedoramobile.org/fc-wireless/hpsp33008.tar.gz/view?searchterm=hpsp33008](http://fedoramobile.org/fc-wireless/hpsp33008.tar.gz/view?searchterm=hpsp33008))
ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
5.At this very moment there's a problem: WiFi card is not working in spite of its presence  in  Network Connection &#8211; cannot be activated manually. Don't loose faith. Go and edit /etc/network/interfaces and add 
iface wlan0 inet dhcp
6.In this moment all the ghizmos lit up and I was happy, happy like a penguin from Antarctica.

dmesg | grep ndiswrapper
[   55.538873] ndiswrapper version 1.30 loaded (preempt=no,smp=yes)
[   55.667622] ndiswrapper (link_pe_images:578): fixing KI_USER_SHARED_DATA address in the driver
[   55.668909] ndiswrapper: driver bcmwl5 (Broadcom,03/23/2006, 4.40.19.0) loaded
[   55.677373] ndiswrapper: using IRQ 22ores.

wlan0     IEEE 802.11g  ESSID:"MSI"
          Mode:Managed  Frequency:2.442 GHz  Access Point: 00:11:09:0D:A3:7C
          Bit Rate=54 Mb/s   Tx-Power:32 dBm
          RTS thr:off   Fragment thr:off
          Power Management:off
          Link Quality:62/100  Signal level:-56 dBm  Noise level:-96 dBm
          Rx invalid nwid:0  Rx invalid crypt:0  Rx invalid frag:0
          Tx excessive retries:0  Invalid misc:16   Missed beacon:0


Hope it will help you. It took me three days of reading experimenting, failing and tying  once again; reinstalling Kubuntu several times included :)...

Thank you guys for hints and really  useful directions.

### Post by RedBoot on 2007-03-04
First, thanks to compwiz18 and kosson for helping me get much further than ever with wireless on Ubuntu. 
As of today, the script compwiz18 has worked so hard on shows that it was last updated in October, 2006 and cannot work on Feisty. It checks for versions through Edgy.
However, if you look through it, it's not too hard to figure out what to do. I'll give the todo list a shot here (and if you want the blow-by-blow see [my notes]("http://techprojectsprogress.blogspot.com/2007/03/feisty-fawn-herd-5-wireless-broadcom.html")).
[LIST]
[*]Read the 1st post in this thread from compwiz18
[*]From Synaptic Package Manager, install ndiswrapper, ndiswrapper-utils, ndiswrapper-utils-1.8, and ndiswrapper-utils-1.9.
[*]Download compwiz18's [file]("http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/bcm4318/get.php?file=bcm4318.all.tar.gz") (copied from the 1st post) and extract it to the desktop. Read through the ndiswrapper-setup script.
[*]Extract drivers-32.tar.gz to the desktop which contain bcmwl5.inf and bcmwl5.sys
[*]Run "ndiswrapper -l" to see if anything else is already installed. I found none, but you'd re-run it with "-e" to erase any previously setup.
[*]From the terminal prompt, in the Desktop directory, run  ```
~/Desktop$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
~/Desktop$ sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```I received 15 lines of stuff after the first command (apparently it had worked) and the second command should activate the driver.
[*]Use "gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist" and add to the end the line```
# module below does not work with Broadcom 4318 wireless cards
blacklist bcm43xx
```
[*]Use "gksudo gedit /etc/modules" and add the line ```
ndiswrapper
``` as the last line, so it will start the driver every time you boot.
[*]Currently, the wireless connection is doing fine, but Network Manager seems to be out of the loop. I'm finding I have to run from the terminal:
```
sudo ifdown eth1
sudo ifup eth1
```
To get DHCP to work with it properly.
[/LIST]
When I figure it out why NetMan is not working, or if you know what's wrong, please let me know and I'll post an update.  
Hope this helps someone.

---

### Post by mmendez on 2007-03-04
I have an Acer Ferrari 4005 and when the acer_acpi would not work for me I checked out the actual web page, the writer explicitly said that it would not work for the Ferrari 4000. I believe that it was written before 5000 or 1000 came out so I do not know if that pertains to them also. Anyway I looked around and found [COLOR="Blue"]**_[[COLOR="Blue"]this[/COLOR]]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx")_**[/COLOR] in the wiki. I tried the firmware cutter straight from acer's website and it worked like a charm with no problems in the install. You can also try the other methods if that wont work.

Just wanted to give a heads up for any one searching through the posts for Acer Ferarri (Did that on purpose)

\\:D/ I'm so happy that my wireless FINALLY works \\:D/

Manny

### Post by Apotheosis on 2007-03-12
I finally got it to work!  I'm so happy!  Just the script alone had worked for me the past 4 or 5 times, but this time it took a bit of effort.  The problems I had were quite common in this thread, so I shall state what I did in hopes of helping someone else out.

I have a Compaq Presario V5000.  It is an i368 (this may not be the exact name, but you get the idea).

When I first tried the script it seemed to do nothing, save erase eth1.  I couldn't figure out how to get it back, so I reinstalled.  I ran the script again (brilliant, I know) and it did the same thing (imagine that).  From here, I started going through the recent pages and tried the fixes that were supplied for others with my problem.  It turns out they were working with AMD64 computers, which as far as I could tell, would help me none.  Since I had tried so many fixes and figured I messed everything up, I reinstalled.  Here is where I started making forward progress:

Last night I stumbled across ndisgtk. When I installed this, it automatically installed some of the ndiswrapper files, so I went ahead and installed the 1.8 file.   As you may have guessed, ndisgtk is a graphical interface that installs drivers.  I downloaded the file from the original post and used this to install the driver.  While sifting through the posts I came across Redboot's instructions for doing this of Feisty Fawn and decided to use this as a refrence for me.

His instructions say to install the driver via the terminal.  I didn't think it would make a difference, but I thought it would be better to follow the instruction of someone who knows what they are doing.  Here I uninstalled the driver with ndisgtk and installed it via the terminal using this:

```
~/Desktop$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
~/Desktop$ sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

From here I ran the setup script on the original page and turned on the light.  I believe it found the signal, but wouldn't connect, but that could have happened after I rebooted, which I did next.  When the computer came back up, the it could find the signal, but wouldn't connect.  I attempted to mess with it for a couple of minutes to no avail.  Then I attempted wifi-radar.  It said I was connected, but I couldn't get online.  I hit disconnect, reconnect and I finally had internet!

---

### Post by esauve on 2007-03-13
I just wanted to add my 2 cents to this huge thread...

I have an Acer Aspire 5002WLMi with the infamous Broadcom AirForce 54G rev 02 card in it and I was able to get it to work reliably because of Compwiz18's wonderful script. (Many thanks to you Compiz18!!!:) ).

There's one thing that caused me trouble at first though, for some reasons the profile created by Network Manager works only if you broadcast your ESSID. Actually it does work but you to reenter ALL the information to recreate the profile after every reboot. Once I enabled ESSID broadcasting on my AP, the information is kept and now I only have to enter my Keyring password, which is a very acceptable compromise (and I think I saw a fix for this).

Here's all the details of my config:

Acer Aspire 5002WLMi
NetGear WPN824 v1 router (configured as an AP only)
Ubuntu 6.10 (32-bit) 
WPA-PSK
Network Manager

---

### Post by tjod on 2007-03-13
removed duplicate

---

### Post by tjod on 2007-03-13
The same applies to my Acer TM 2480, though I ended up using WICD 1.09 instead of Network Manager. 

I also decided that broadcasting the ESSID was a small price to pay for an otherwise working wireless system. The WPA-PSK with TKIP works well and fast, so I'm a happy camper too. 

Thanks to Compwiz18 and all who contributed to the discussions!

Tim O  :p

### Post by Golf4Fun on 2007-03-14

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-15
@**tjod**: Check out wicd 1.1.0.1 ([http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/wicd/phpbb/download.php?id=24&f=4](http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/wicd/phpbb/download.php?id=24&f=4)) and you can connect to hidden networks automatically (I think, not sure it works properly with ndiswrapper, but worth a try)

@**mmmhobnobs**: you did follow [http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/linux/bcm4318/howto.htm](http://compwiz18.blackhole.cx/linux/bcm4318/howto.htm) and compile ndiswrapper, right?  (steps 6 and 7, and see the Download Files section for info on where to get the needed file)

### Post by mmmhobnobs on 2007-03-16
okay I'm not sure what network manager or wifi radar(?) or any other program is - all I've been using is the default program for enabling/disabling and networking under system->administration->networking (I think it's networking, anyways...).

I installed wicd and it detected my wireless network and showed me the signal strength etc:) which made me happy, although I had trouble connecting.

When using automatic ip assignment it attempted to connect and appeared to be obtaining an ip address for a long time - I never actually left it until it stopped trying to obtain one as it seemed to be an abnormal amount of time.
When assigning my own ip address (same settings as I would in windows) it went through the process quickly upon clicking connect, although went straight back to "not connected".

I'm using a plain text WEP key -don't really know if it's important but I couldn't really see what the problem is as there doesn't seem to be much documentation on wicd...any (more) help? please:):)

Thanks for your help so far:) this is my make or break of linux lol

### Post by thomaswp on 2007-03-17
> **k_der said:**
> Man, you are a f***ing legend. Three re-installs ](*,) after my own failed attempts to get this working, and your method worked straight out of the box first time. Again you are a legend!!! Thank You =D> 

My system for anybody interested is:

Dell Inspiron 1300 
02:03:0 Network controller: Broadcom Corp. BCM4318 [Airforce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller (rev 02)Totally echo all that.  Almost exactly the same story (inspiron 1300, my own failed attempts.  Also it did not work properly until I rebooted - I was getting an IP address but no connectivity.

One thing though - after a reboot it appears that the wireless has to be manually connected (using wifi-radar) once you are up and running?  No doubt I will work that out in time - starting to STF now!

Really pleased.  Thanks.


[I]Later:  I realised that I needed the key in the network manager field/edit /etc/network/interfaces - and added the extra  "wireless-key" line to it

wireless-key xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
wireless-essid yyyyyyyyyyy

auto eth1[/I]

So I was a bit dim - it works flawlessly now.

---

I recently installed Ubuntu 6.10 and of course the new native driver doesnt work with the 4318. ### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-18
@**dacool25**: Can you download the file in the first post, and open it by double clicking.  Extract the 64bit drivers (I can't quite remember the name, something like drivers-x64.tar.gx) and double click them.  Now open a terminal and type **cd ~/Desktop** and then run **sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf** and see if that does anything... (you may have to reboot to get the light to come on)

> **jmagick07 said:**

if the light comes on, it should work.

I followed the instructions to get this card fired up but the script appeared to run into some problems. Heres the output from the script.
```

bourne@bourne-desktop:~/Desktop$ ./ndiswrapper_setup

Gathering wireless card information...
Done...


----------------------------------



You should probably install an wireless connection manager
The best choices are
                        network-manager OR wifi-radar
Strengths/weaknesses of network-manager:
+ Is able to use WPA, WEP, or any other kind of standard encryption
+ Easy to change networks
- Asks for keyring password on boot to connect to encrypted networks
- No network profiles, or at least you are unable to see them
- Is impossible to connect to a wired and wireless network at the same time
- No static IPs
- Network strength does not work

Strengths/weakness of wifi-radar:
+ Works 99% of the time
+ Static IPs or DHCP
+ Supports WEP
+ Network strength sort of works
+ Doesn't need password at boot
+ Able to connect to wired and wireless at the same time
- No WPA

After you have connected to the internet,
I would recommend installing one of these programs.
To install network-manager, open Synaptic (Ubuntu) or Adept (Kubuntu)
and find the package network-manager-gnome, and install it.
To install wifi-radar, open Synaptic (Ubuntu) or Adept (Kubuntu)
and find the package wifi-radar, and install it.
chmod: changing permissions of `/home/bourne/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log': Operation not permitted

scroll up and read the pros and cons of a couple.
You will have to connect to use the internet now.
This can be accomplished (in GNOME) by
going to System / Administration / Networking,
and configuring the wireless card by selecting the network name.
If something is not working, please try rebooting.
If you need more help, please post ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log on
the forum thread where you downloaded it from for help.

### Post by RedBoot on 2007-03-20
Compwiz18,
Since Network Manager is the only thing still not working right for my in Feisty herd 5 (it just always acts confused!) , I look forward to trying out **Wicd**.

BTW, it took me a few seconds to get it, but it's a great name!

Scott
 
> See the link for wicd in my signature? You could try it (it seems to work more often network-manager) and use it to connect to the network.

Disclamer: wicd is my little project
__________________
Wicd: A new (wireless) network manager | HOWTO: Broadcom 4318 Wireless Cards | HOWTO: Make GAIM Pretty | HOWTO: Add Extra Repositories

> Hey whats up. I'm back again to attempt to try and get wireless working with this devil card on my desktop. I have a little more knowledge now then I did before so hopefully this time I will be able to figure it out. I also have a wired connection with my desktop temporarily. 
I followed the instructions to get this card fired up but the script appeared to run into some problems. Heres the output from the script.
```

bourne@bourne-desktop:~/Desktop$ ./ndiswrapper_setup
Starting ndiswrapper install...
If you have any package managers open (i.e. Adept or Synaptic or apt-get),

mv: cannot stat `drivers-32.tar.gz': No such file or directory
mv: cannot stat `drivers-64.tar.gz': No such file or directory
You appear to have an i386 system...installing the i386 drivers...
Done...
Adding drivers to ndiswrapper...
Done...
./ndiswrapper_setup: line 187: /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist: Permission denied
./ndiswrapper_setup: line 188: /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist: Permission denied
./ndiswrapper_setup: line 190: /etc/modules: Permission denied
Gathering wireless card information...
Done...


----------------------------------



You should probably install an wireless connection manager
The best choices are
                        network-manager OR wifi-radar
Strengths/weaknesses of network-manager:
+ Is able to use WPA, WEP, or any other kind of standard encryption
+ Easy to change networks
- Asks for keyring password on boot to connect to encrypted networks
- No network profiles, or at least you are unable to see them
- Is impossible to connect to a wired and wireless network at the same time
- No static IPs
- Network strength does not work

Strengths/weakness of wifi-radar:
+ Works 99% of the time
+ Static IPs or DHCP
+ Supports WEP
+ Network strength sort of works
+ Doesn't need password at boot
+ Able to connect to wired and wireless at the same time
- No WPA

After you have connected to the internet,
I would recommend installing one of these programs.
To install network-manager, open Synaptic (Ubuntu) or Adept (Kubuntu)
and find the package network-manager-gnome, and install it.
To install wifi-radar, open Synaptic (Ubuntu) or Adept (Kubuntu)
and find the package wifi-radar, and install it.
chmod: changing permissions of `/home/bourne/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log': Operation not permitted


----------------------------------


Script completed.
If your wireless light is on, give your internet a shot.
If you are interested in install a connection manager, please
scroll up and read the pros and cons of a couple.
You will have to connect to use the internet now.
This can be accomplished (in GNOME) by
going to System / Administration / Networking,
and configuring the wireless card by selecting the network name.
If something is not working, please try rebooting.
If you need more help, please post ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log on
the forum thread where you downloaded it from for help.

OK, looks like you are missing some files.  Redownload the file on the front post, delete all the versions you have already, and extract the entire thing.  It looks like the the version you have may be corrupted.

---

### Post by brandon moore on 2007-03-20
another update... this card works beautifully in my WITH WEP encryption in the newest feisty release (64-bit). bcm43xx still has to be blacklisted, but with ndiswrapper and bcmwl5, i can log on to my wireless network with no problems!

---

### Post by dragonlor20 on 2007-03-20
Compwiz, I have used your tutorial probably about 10 times now and I thank you very much for posting it and building the script! There are a few things I think you should include in the tutorial to smooth things out:

1. If the script doesn't initially start up the wireless, and one must move on to the troubleshooting section, and they make it through the "rmmod" and "modprobe" commands, and the wireless light is STILL not on, go back to the beginning of the tutorial, delete all of the log files and the ndiswrapper_setup file (essentially delete everything but the initial tarball). Repeat the top steps of the howto. This gives the install a chance to do things correctly AFTER all of the ndiswrapper stuff has been installed. 

I usually have to reboot at least once in there. AND

2.  Don't forget to push the wireless button on the Presario laptops, it doesn't seem to come on by default.

I just thought that might keep a lot of people from turning away saying it doesn't work...

---

### Post by dragonlor20 on 2007-03-20
> **tmasboa said:**
> I have this card and Presario V2000. I have a very very fresh Ubuntu installation, and it won't work. If some would be kind enough to give me a list of things to run and give you the outputs, I will happily do so ;).

Also, after running the script, when go to the System_Administration_Networking, my wireless no longer shows up :|

If you are still having problems, look at my comment a little below yours. I have the Presario V2000 and that is how I have got my wireless working...

---

### Post by Mathiasdm on 2007-03-24
For the curious ones: the bcm43xx driver in development works at 54 Mbps already. Once they solve the power issues (trouble connecting when you're too far away from the access point), ndiswrapper will no longer be required!  :guitar:

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-24
> **Mathiasdm said:**
> For the curious ones: the bcm43xx driver in development works at 54 Mbps already. Once they solve the power issues (trouble connecting when you're too far away from the access point), ndiswrapper will no longer be required!  :guitar:
Really?  I've never heard that before, although I didn't notice any speed difference when I was testing it out.

### Post by rekahsoft on 2007-03-24
My wireless in festy is not working properly :'( Network manager can see the connection but for some reason i cannot connect to it :'( when i go sudo ifdown eth1 i get:```
ifdown: interface eth1 not configured
``` and if i go sudo ifup eth1 i get:```
Ignoring unknown interface eth1=eth1.
```i know that eth1 is my interface for my wireless card (because of ifconfig). What do i do to get this working?

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-24
> **rekahsoft said:**
> My wireless in festy is not working properly :'( Network manager can see the connection but for some reason i cannot connect to it :'( when i go sudo ifdown eth1 i get:```
ifdown: interface eth1 not configured
``` and if i go sudo ifup eth1 i get:```
Ignoring unknown interface eth1=eth1.
```i know that eth1 is my interface for my wireless card (because of ifconfig). What do i do to get this working?
Try wicd in my signature.  It is a replacement for network manager, and generally works better.  And I've heard that network-manager has some issues in Feisty.

disclamer: wicd is my project.

---

### Post by rekahsoft on 2007-03-24
ok i got my broadcom 4318 working without a hitch in festy...The problem is the network manager. Simply use wifiradar and everything works fine :D ```
sudo apt-get install wifiradar
```Hopefully network manager will be fixed by the release of feisty...although AFAIK feature freeze has already came so i doubt it. :(

---

### Post by tuffguy on 2007-03-25
ok, so i followed all x64 the steps for the x64 processor, and i use a hp 8225nr, and now my wireless card doesn't even show up on the network settings window, and whenever i reboot, i have to re-enable my wired connection( and sitting in a pantry with a wired connection sucks, btw).  I've been wanting to switch to linux for a while now, but this just plain sucks.. i've gone through and installed kubuntu multiple times and now ubuntu, doing formats everytime.  When i updated the kernel(i think) that's when i first lost all sorts of stuff, then after yet another fresh install, i did the how to for the x64 hp setup(7th effort as of 8am this morning).  Can anyone explain what i did wrong?  

-also, my computer seems to like randomly pasting text that can be found on webpages or terminal windows as i type, and it's not information i deliberately cut...??

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-25
> **tuffguy said:**
> ok, so i followed all x64 the steps for the x64 processor, and i use a hp 8225nr, and now my wireless card doesn't even show up on the network settings window, and whenever i reboot, i have to re-enable my wired connection( and sitting in a pantry with a wired connection sucks, btw).  I've been wanting to switch to linux for a while now, but this just plain sucks.. i've gone through and installed kubuntu multiple times and now ubuntu, doing formats everytime.  When i updated the kernel(i think) that's when i first lost all sorts of stuff, then after yet another fresh install, i did the how to for the x64 hp setup(7th effort as of 8am this morning).  Can anyone explain what i did wrong?  

-also, my computer seems to like randomly pasting text that can be found on webpages or terminal windows as i type, and it's not information i deliberately cut...??

To answer the random cut paste thing: in Ubuntu, when you highlight a word (select it) with the mouse, it becomes copied (not to the clipboard though) and when you middle click it is pasted.  Middle clicking can be accomplished one of two ways (on a standard laptop touchpad): pushing both mouse buttons down together, or by tapping in the top right hand corner - very irritating, and I don't know how to turn it off, although I have no doubt that it can be done.

If you ran the script on the first page, there should be a log file on the Desktop, could you post that up here?  It gives most all of the troubleshooting info we need to help you. Also, the output of **uname -a** would be helpful.

---

### Post by SuperTeece on 2007-03-25
I don't know if this is available in Dapper or Edgy but I'll throw it out anyway. I upgraded to feisty in hopes that the bcm4318 would work out of the box in this latest edition. It did not.

I was pointed to this golden little piece of code called FWCUTTER.

It automatically downloaded stuff for the card and the card worked!

I suggest this as the way to go.

---

### Post by Feenix3k on 2007-03-26
I have a Dell Inspiron 5150. The card is bcm4309.  The driver in that link did not work, but this one did and Im so happy. I can dump W$ from my laptop:razz: \\:D/ ):P

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-03-26
> **SuperTeece said:**
> I don't know if this is available in Dapper or Edgy but I'll throw it out anyway. I upgraded to feisty in hopes that the bcm4318 would work out of the box in this latest edition. It did not.

I was pointed to this golden little piece of code called FWCUTTER.

It automatically downloaded stuff for the card and the card worked!

I suggest this as the way to go.

It works in Edgy if you use a custom kernel above 2.6.20, I think.  It might work on Dapper, too, and it may work below 2.6.20, but 2.6.20 was the lowest I've had success on 64bit.  It does work on 32bit on lower kernel versions, I believe.

---

### Post by tuffguy on 2007-03-26
Followed the link, did another fresh install of ubuntu 6.1 last night, did all the updating of the kernel, ndiswrapper, etc, and once more my wireless connection vanished.   One thing i've noticed, and this is of course after doing all the 150+updates, i have an error on my network icon on the taskbar: SIOCGIFFLAGS error: No such device 
Perhaps this is the bain of my new linux existence..  from what i could see in terminal, everything installed as advertised without errors...  btw, thanks for all the support Compwhiz18, means a lot to have help switching over.

### Post by wncben on 2007-03-27
THANK YOU AGAIN COMPWIZ! and thank you redboot!  Using compwizes excelent tutorial I fought and slayed the 4318 beast under edgy (with amd 64 no less!) and I am Very pleased to say that, while Feisty beta did not have wifi right out of the box with this card, it was MUCH MUCH MUCH easier to fix, using REDBOOT's easy to follow howto in this thread.  

15 min of playing around and wifi works!

Two things:

 First of all, as I am using a 64bit system, used the 64bit drivers instead of the 32 bit (which seems obvious)

 Second, when I reeboot, i noticed that while network manager 'sees' the available networks, it can't connect, it just idles... HOWEVER, if I turn the card off and on again, it seems to connect okay... not sure why, but it is a small sacrifice.

 Just to let y'all know, so far Feisty has exceeded my expectations like no previous release of ubuntu: wifi was easy fix, ATI 3d works right out of the box, sound seems to be working great.... come on in bruthas and sistahs, the feisty water is mighty fine!

~Ben

---

It might be rather long, I tried it twice with the settings you recommended. I don't know if you even look at this thread anymore but it was worth a shot.

I also tried several other tutorials and at one point I had the wireless icon in the network manager, but I did something else to remove it and now I can't duplicate the steps I took.

The light on my laptop is not coming on either, I have a HP Pavilion ZV6000 series with the broadcom device that shows in the output you have in the first post (that's why I thought this was going to work out smoothly, but it did not). 

Any help would be appreciated from this point, right now I'm downloading the 64 bit version of the distro and I'm just going to reinstall it on top of this one.

Luckily I just started so I don't have any information that I will miss (i'm also using a dual boot laptop) the wireless for the windows xp side works without a problem. (light comes on and everything)


Thanks for any help .

### Post by BinaryMadman on 2007-03-29
Wow, I just tried this and it was very easy, thanks!  I tried another how to but it got really confusing.  Your awesome script took care of everything. :)  I installed wifi-radar then ran it from Applications-->Internet-->Wifi-radar and local networks started popping up.

---

### Post by ericesque on 2007-03-29
Hey, just an fyi, I used the driver provided in the archive provided on the first page, then manually followed the script in Feisty beta and it is working.  I'm only running WEP @ 11Mb/s because my router is b, but it's functional and I'm geeked.

For a little further clarification, I have the 4318, Feisty beta 32-bit, and am using Network Manager to manage access.  As mentioned, WEP @ 11Mb.   

I think the trick for me was manually switching my wireless card on and off after I finished all the other steps of the script, and a reboot that the script doesn't seem to require (I may have missed it).   ATM I cannot recall if I also manually switched the wifi off and on after the reboot.  I'll follow up if it seems to require it.

### Post by phreakshew on 2007-03-30
THANK YOU!!!  I sold my old HP Pavilion laptop along with the Linksys WPC54G ver.2 card (with the Texas Instruments chipset) and traded up to a Toshiba Satellite.  Bought a new Linksys PCMCIA card ubt it turned out to be ver.3 with the Broadcom 4318 chipset!  All I had to do was run the ndiswrapper setup per your instructions and voila!  It works.  SUPER EASY!  :guitar: you rock!

### Post by sirkism on 2007-03-30
Alright, so I reinstalled with feisty fawn-I used the script and the wireless light did come on, but still can't connect to the wireless network in my house.

The connection is encrypted in WEP, I tried out wicd (which is very user friendly) but all it did was see the wireless connections in my area (step above where I was before), but when I try to connect after putting in the same WEP it would try to connect but still nothing. I tried restarted, no go.

I removed the rj45 from my laptop and tried just the wireless connection-again it sees all the wireless connections, gives me 100% signal but I can't obtain an IP from my router.

### Post by r3dO on 2007-04-13
so... in this moment i'm posting with the wireless connection!

after the installation of ndis pkgs i have tried the WiFi-Radar. with it i was able to view all the networks but with a problem with connection. sad.

it was the right time for the tibetan pause of the cigarette... and then i have tried to disable all the modules (ndis and bcm) with 'rmmod' and afterwards re-enable only the bcm43xx. the tibetans wins!

the nm-applet discovers the network and finally i have the connection.

i really don't know the matter but is good. 
i must remember only to remove and re-enable the bcm module everytime i need the wireless.

thanks a lot for the support!

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-04-13
> **r3dO said:**
> so... in this moment i'm posting with the wireless connection!

after the installation of ndis pkgs i have tried the WiFi-Radar. with it i was able to view all the networks but with a problem with connection. sad.

it was the right time for the tibetan pause of the cigarette... and then i have tried to disable all the modules (ndis and bcm) with 'rmmod' and afterwards re-enable only the bcm43xx. the tibetans wins!

the nm-applet discovers the network and finally i have the connection.

i really don't know the matter but is good. 
i must remember only to remove and re-enable the bcm module everytime i need the wireless.

thanks a lot for the support!
You can modify **/etc/modprobe.d/blacklist** and add a new line to the bottom with **blacklist ndiswrapper** on it and edit **/etc/modules** and add a new line with **bcm43xx** on it, then you shouldn't have to remove and re-enable every time.

### Post by rivera151 on 2007-04-15
Nice tutorial.  I used it to get my wifi working on a 32-bit Ubuntu install on my HP laptop.  I also used your 64-bit install procedure (since the new kernel won't let ndiswrapper be) and it worked.  

However, I should let you know of a downstream error I got after using your install procedure.

Using the most recent kernel, I had gotten hardware acceleration (HWA) to work.  I used this post:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=321766](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=321766)

to get it working; although the post says it's for getting beryl working, the procedure for getting HWA working is the one I used (the first part of the procedure).  Also, it only works when I download the file ati-driver-installer-8.32.5-x86.x86_64, which is supplied on that post.

After downloading your latest kernel for getting ndiswrapper working (uname -r: 2.6.20.3), I follow the installation procedure.  When I try to build the kernel module (sudo module-assistant build fglrx), I get an error.  I would post it, but I cannot post the entire error since I can find the log file.  

In any case, the command "sudo module-assistant build fglrx" produces an error.  From the looks of it, it looks line its using an #include directive, but it can't find the file.  The exact error section is like this:

```

&#9474; /usr/bin/make -C /usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.20.3                           &#9618;
&#9474; SUBDIRS=/usr/src/modules/fglrx modules                                     &#9618;
&#9474; make[1]: Entering directory `/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.20.3'              &#9618;
&#9474;   CC [M]  /usr/src/modules/fglrx/firegl_public.o                           &#9618;
&#9474; /usr/src/modules/fglrx/firegl_public.c:89:26: error: linux/config.h: No    &#9618;
&#9474; such file or directory                                                   

```

I took a peek at /usr/src/modules/fglrx/firegl_public.c.  Line 89 has the following code:

```

#include <linux/config.h>

```

It looks like I'm missing a header file, and that is preventing me from building the ATI kernel module.  I'm looking at kernel.org to find the source to the kernel I downloaded from your 64-bit tutorial page (v2.6.20.3), but I don't see the config.h file anywhere in the tarball.  

Do you have any idea how I cold solve this problem?

[EDIT] 

The source file /usr/src/modules/fglrx/firegl_public.c references the linux/config.h file.  This file has been deprecated.  References to it must be commented out of the source file, and rebuilt into the package fglrx-kernel-source_8.32.5-1_amd64.deb.  Does anyone know how I can do this?

The package file worked perfectly for me under Fiesty.  One note, I did not have to reboot, but simply disable and reenable my card (hardware switch).  To connect to a WEP network I had to first configure the settings then disable and reenable the wireless connection in the network manager.

### Post by Sabot on 2007-04-20
After trying to install the .deb it locked.  I force quit the install now when I try to use synaptic I get this error "E: The package bcm43xx-firmware needs to be reinstalled, but I can't find an archive for it.
E: Internal error opening cache (1). Please report." If I find a way clear this I will post it below.

Here is how you can remove this error:

> sudo dpkg --remove --force-remove-reinstreq bcm43xx-firmware

### Post by arron on 2007-04-20
With the firmware on 6.10 ad 7.04 i got slow transfer rates from the local network.  With NDIS wrapper for both 6.10 and 7.04 i got a LOT faster, under the same conditions (location, pc, fresh installs).

With the firmware i got a max of 500-600k.  With the NDIS wrapper i got a solid 2600-2700k.  This has been constant results for me on my 4318 on my Compaq r4000 series.

Can you update the script for those to try the NDIS wrapper for Feisty please so others can try it?

Directions for those to do NDIS wrapper:

use this command with the driver file to get the ndis wrapper:

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf

then run these 2 commands (not sure why):

sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo ndiswrapper -m

now you need to blacklist the kernel module that does nto work, add "blacklist bcm43xx" in /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist, then add "ndiswrapper" to /etc/modules to load ndis wrapper on startup, reboot and see if the light comes on.

Hope this helps others, I can now watch large uncompressed video's from the network with no lag.

### Post by chipbennett on 2007-04-22
This worked for me!

I've got Feisty installed as part of a dual-boot system: Gateway MX6445 (custom Best Buy machine, I think) laptop, AMD64 Turion, 1GB RAM, 120GB HDD, with the Broadcom 4318 integrated wifi. The ndiswrapper with the Feisty network manager were able to recognize my card, and use it to recognize/attempt to connect to my WAP (Linksys WRT54G, ver. 5.0, I think).

One note: My network is protected via WPA2-personal, using a TKIP-AES encryption algorithm. The network manager seemed unable to recognize this algorithm, and was unable to connect. I changed the WAP to use AES encryption only, and once I did so, the Feisty network manager was able to connect. I am now once again happily using my wireless connection!

Thanks for the tutorial; hopefully one day I will have the opportunity to help someone else in return.

(I suppose this post could be my "Hello, World!" to the Ubuntu Community. I am a Windows-convert-in-process. I'm off to explore more of the forums...)

---

### Post by Silentheero on 2007-04-22
I tried this for my bcm4306 and it worked:

sudo apt-get install bcm43xx-fwcutter

accept the driver dl

sudo modprobe bcm43xx

### Post by thomaswp on 2007-04-23
Word of warning to fools like me:

I upgraded to Feisty, but my wifi didn't work.  I came right back to this thread, downloaded the .deb file and thought "oooh, that is a bit easier than last time".

Then wifi was very flakey and inconsistent which was surprising since it had been rock solid under edgy.

Returned to this thread in "trouble shooting" mode and immediately saw that I had stupidly installed the native drivers - so I installed the ndiswrapper ones instead.  This appeared to kill my network control applet but a reboot restored me to my prior rock-steadiness.

That, no sound (fixed by turning up PCM in alsamixer) and ruined blur in beryl are my only problems with the upgrade (so far!!)

Hi

I then installed ndiswrapper-utils-1.9 and ndiswrapper-common, restarted, and the light came on. Activation stage: Configuring device", where it just stops.  Here's my log file, if it helps (thanks!)


Hi

Not sure if I can help as I setup via Gnome ala network-manager. KDE does not seem as flexible to me :) When I :

sudo apt-get install kubuntu-desktop

it worked after a fashion, but like you had it halat at the mid-upper 20% if I remember. DEfaulted to my Gnome and apart from wpa key problems when I restart all is well.

SUggestion:

sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop

Log into Gnome making sure you have the network-manager and gnome-network-manager installed and get back to see if we can help any further. Apologies I cannot help more :(

kind regards

john

### Post by joshjani on 2007-04-24
This worked for me! I have a Compaq V2000 laptop with a Broadcom 4318 pci card in it, and was unable to get wireless working until I did this.

The only thing I did differently from the instructions was that I used point-and-click to unpack and run the fwcutter. (I didn't know how to navigate to a folder using the terminal!) :confused: 

I was asked if I wanted to run it or see the contents, so I ran it in the terminal, where I was asked for my password. Input that, rebooted, and when I configured my card in the network settings box, I was up and running!:guitar: 

So right now I've only had it working at home- we'll see how it works here at work when I try to hook up.

But anyway, thanks for the how-to. It'll make my Ubuntu experience SO much better!
JC

---

### Post by beta.tester on 2007-04-24
> **joshjani said:**
> This worked for me! I have a Compaq V2000 laptop with a Broadcom 4318 pci card in it, and was unable to get wireless working until I did this.

The only thing I did differently from the instructions was that I used point-and-click to unpack and run the fwcutter. (I didn't know how to navigate to a folder using the terminal!) :confused: 

I was asked if I wanted to run it or see the contents, so I ran it in the terminal, where I was asked for my password. Input that, rebooted, and when I configured my card in the network settings box, I was up and running!:guitar: 

So right now I've only had it working at home- we'll see how it works here at work when I try to hook up.

But anyway, thanks for the how-to. It'll make my Ubuntu experience SO much better!
JC

Welcome to the wireless club and congratulations :)

Basic commands in a terminal:

pwd = present work directory (where am i? 
cd /    (change directory to root
cd /home   (change to my home directory)
mkdir downloads  (make a directory called downloads immediately under the directory I am now)
cp /home/jb/test.txt /home/downloads  (Copy file called test.tx in /home/jb to /home/downloads)
Lots more but pretty easy after time and practice.

All the best

kind regards

john

---

### Post by iwarp62 on 2007-04-24
Important Info for all those running fiesty:

[Bug #105977]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/105977") is currently preventing ndiswrapper drivers from connecting to wpa secured networks. If you follow the ndiswrapper instructions on this post but cannot connect to wpa secured networks you can try compiling the latest version of ndiswrapper by following steps 11 through 14 on [this wiki page.]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper") You will then have to follow this thread's first post again to reinstall your drivers.


Good Luck!

---

### Post by H.E. Pennypacker on 2007-04-25
> **iwarp62 said:**
> Important Info for all those running fiesty:

[Bug #105977]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/105977") is currently preventing ndiswrapper drivers from connecting to wpa secured networks. If you follow the ndiswrapper instructions on this post but cannot connect to wpa secured networks you can try compiling the latest version of ndiswrapper by following steps 11 through 14 on [this wiki page.]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper/FAQ?highlight=%28ndiswrapper%29") You will then have to follow this thread's first post again to reinstall your drivers.

Good Luck!

Following iwarp's advice, I am able to connect.  I first followed the instructions from 11-14 in the wiki.  I then installed ndiswrapper from source (the latest version - download it from ndiswrapper's website).  Then, I went to the first page of this thread.  I tried the Feisty script, but that did not work.  I then tried the general method, and that did work.  I am not sure if I had to reboot several times.

Make sure Network Manager is installed.  Without Network Manager being installed, Ubuntu's Networking Adminstration thingie will not allow you to roam.  All roaming means is that you are able to switch from one wireless network to another. 

I still have a problem with connecting to my own WPA2 network.  I am right now connected to my neighbor's unencrypted network.

### Post by kybKenny on 2007-04-26
Correction to the ndiswrapper installation script:

it says:

```
In Feisty just right-click on the network-manager icon in the top right of the screen,
you should see your wireless access point now.
```

you see the available wireless networks, if you do a LEFT click on the network manager.

I know, it's easy to figure out, but so is kernel configuration if you know how to do it :-)

I think my mom would be scared by the pop up that comes when you click RIGHT, 'cause that brings you the option to turn your network off...

Nice script btw. don't know yet if it worked, alas, no light on up until now.
But thanks anyway so far :-)

---

### Post by joshjani on 2007-04-26
Well, I gave another try to getting my wireless to work, and after some careful copying into the terminal, I have it going! Absolutely perfect at home, where I have a strong signal and WEP encryption, and OK here at work, where I find 2 networks in range, both open, but only one has a signal strong enough. So- so far so good.

I started this time by installing (or re-installing?) ndiswrapper from my Feisty CD. Then I followed carefully the directions in Nickm's post, but used compuwiz18's downloaded file. I'm talking out my ear here, but I think my success came after I blacklisted the 43xx driver and had that fresh install of ndiswrapper.

Now, when I sign on at home, I am asked for the security passphrase. I changed that to HEX key, and input said string. That connected me, then I got a popup asking me to add a password to my keychain. I input a password, confirmed it, and all is well. Will I need that each time I boot to Ubuntu at home, where my secure network is? So I'll log in, then as the wireless tries to connect, I'll be asked for that keychain password? Pardon me, but what's a keychain? 

Compuwiz18- thanks for this excellent work you've done. I can see that it has helped tons of folks get their troublesome wireless cards working. I have a question- in your signature you have a link to WICD. Is that a better network manager than what comes in the Ubuntu install? I guess I'd have to uninstall network manager to use WICD?

And thanks to nickm, too, whose guide is also excellent and useful. Since compuwiz18 references it, I followed it and I think it ultimately proved to be the winning combination.

---

### Post by RedBoot on 2007-04-29
hotshot23,
I wish I could give you a good answer about the uninstall. 
One thing I did notice that was interesting was that there are 3 SSID APs listed in your log and two of them are on channel 11 and one of them seemed to be your strongest signal. I don't know if you have any access to these APs, but one of the 11s should be turned to channel six so there is not interference.

---

### Post by hotshot23t on 2007-04-30
No that shouldn't be a problem with the network thats set up.  I live at an Olympic training center and the top guys from AT&T hooked us up and setup up a network to cover the entire center.  They are upgrading us to a D3 soon (different than T3 but same speed) so I was hoping to get the ndiswrapper working cause we'll be getting 40+ mbps pretty soon.  I installed the native driver and no luck, I'm going to go remove the ndiswrapper module and restart.  Hopefully that will get me somewhere.

### Post by cymoril on 2007-04-30
Hello!

I followed the instructions contained in this Guide to get my Broadcom 4318 card working on Edge.

When I upgraded to Feisty yesterday, I was prompted to replace the /etc/mobprobe.d/blacklist file with a newer version.

I didn't know what to do and as the previous configuration had worked fine, I chose to keep the older version of the file.

After checking the Forums and this thread, I discovered that native bcm43xx support had been included with Feisty.

I would like to continue using ndiswrapper.

However, I do not know what I'm using at present. 

Has my upgrade to Feisty overwritten the original ndiswrapper settings? How do I check? Will I need to configure ndiswrapper once more by following the instructions in this HOWTO?

The light on my Wireless is on!

Thank you.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-04-30
> **P5791 said:**
> ndiswrapper works fine here
however I had to install version 1.42 to get it working
Yeah, you may have to download the latest ndiswrapper from SourceForge, then compile it.  I can give you a link to a tutorial to do that:

[http://compwiz18.ig3.net/linux/bcm4318/howto.htm](http://compwiz18.ig3.net/linux/bcm4318/howto.htm) (yes, I know it says for Edgy amd64, ignore that ;))

Download the attachment to the Desktop, then do** only **parts 6 & 7 in the link above.

---

### Post by hotshot23t on 2007-05-01
OK thanks guys.  Should I just be able to uninstall the native driver and then run ndiswrapper or am I going to have to do a fresh install?  Either way the native driver isn't that great.  I'll be going along and all of a sudden the light will go off and I have to reboot in order to get it working again.  Thanks for your help!

-I just read your how to compwiz, it was the way I got everything working on edgy64!  So considering there's no new kernel to change I need to 1. Get the headers 2. Run your script 3. Extract the newer ndiswrapper 4. Compile  5. Reboot and enjoy?  Thanks again

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-05-01
> **hotshot23t said:**
> OK thanks guys.  Should I just be able to uninstall the native driver and then run ndiswrapper or am I going to have to do a fresh install?  Either way the native driver isn't that great.  I'll be going along and all of a sudden the light will go off and I have to reboot in order to get it working again.  Thanks for your help!

-I just read your how to compwiz, it was the way I got everything working on edgy64!  So considering there's no new kernel to change I need to 1. Get the headers 2. Run your script 3. Extract the newer ndiswrapper 4. Compile  5. Reboot and enjoy?  Thanks again
Yeah, just use Synaptic to grab the headers, then follow the steps you have listed above.

You won't need to do a fresh install, my script will automatically disable it for you.

Just FYI though: If you suddenly feel an overpowering urge to use the native driver, you can just **sudo rmmod ndiswrapper** and then **sudo modprobe bcm43xx** (I tell you this in case something goes wrong during ndiswrapper installation and you need wifi)

---

### Post by DavidLamp on 2007-05-01

I researched for one week not able to find this information.

I am very new to Ubuntu and it worked properly.

Best Regards,


David J. Lamp

### Post by joshjani on 2007-05-02
My internal card works great now at home, where I have WEP encryption, passphrase, the whole deal. Very happy- at home.

At work, when I first boot, I am given the choice of two open networks. Both have relatively weak signals, but I am able to connect to the stronger one of the two every time in XP, but only very occasionally with Ubuntu.

I know the card works w/ ndiswrapper- since it works at home; but at my office, I get just the spinning comet and no network connection. 

Any reason I can connect to my own secure network, but not to an open one? Is it because of a weak signal? Can I only configure my wireless settings one way, that is, WEP with password at home, so that I can't connect to a different open network?

JC

### Post by P5791 on 2007-05-03
> **rsvirani said:**
> Hey guys I am in desperate need of help.  I have een trying all the how tos and forums for 3 or 4 days and I did fresh installs of 7.04 in between just to increase odds but I still cant get this to work.  This is what I did last:

1.  Fresh install
2.  blacklisted bcm43xx or whatever
3.  downloaded tar ball
4.  unzipped and ran ./ndiswrapper-config
5.  No luck, no light, and wifi completely disappeared from network window
6.  Found post saying to do:
    lsmod | grep ndiswrapper
    lsmod | grep bcm43xx
    ndiswrapper -v
7.  Found out ndiswrapper is not even installed.
8.  Followed post 1404 or something saying to do steps 6 & 7 of another how to to install latest ndiswrapper (1.43)
9.  Installed ndiswrapper and 'build-essentials'
10.  Rebooted
11.  tried ./ndiswrapper-config again
12.  rebooted, no luck, no light


Wed May  2 22:52:24 2007
----------------[/code]


The script installs ndiswrapper version 1.38, so please check your current version.
Also when you upgrade ndiswrapper you might need to reinstall the firmware 
You can extract the firmware from the bcm4318.tar.gz file [http://ubuntuforums.org/attachment.php?attachmentid=11584&d=1150897000](http://ubuntuforums.org/attachment.php?attachmentid=11584&d=1150897000)
the files you 're looking for are bcmwl5.inf and bcmwl5.sys
extract them to your home folder
then from a terminal type
ndiswrapper -i /your_home_folder/bcmwl5.inf
ndiswrapper -m

---

### Post by frunzaverde on 2007-05-03
On a Ubuntu Feisty 32-bit install, with a Broadcom 4318 card running on an Amilo A1667G system, using the ndiswrapper (script) method (the firmware/bcm43xx method did not work at all), I have run into the following problem : 

Wireless works, but only if I do the following : Attempt to connect using network manager (which detects the networks available), followed imediatley, while nm-applet shows that it attempts to connect, by asking for a dhcp lease manually by issuing sudo dhclient3 from a shell. Network manager will show as unconnected, but wireless will work (with the exception that i have to manually get my lease when it expires, every 10 minutes - cron solved it). This was tested on both a WEP and a non-encrypted network. Network-manager works fine on wired, and the system is updated, and all the fixes proposed by a similar ubuntu bug did not have any effect on the behavior. Have I done something wrong - and if so, what can I do to fix it? Thank you in advance.

---

### Post by P5791 on 2007-05-03
> **frunzaverde said:**
> On a Ubuntu Feisty 32-bit install, with a Broadcom 4318 card running on an Amilo A1667G system, using the ndiswrapper (script) method (the firmware/bcm43xx method did not work at all), I have run into the following problem : 

Wireless works, but only if I do the following : Attempt to connect using network manager (which detects the networks available), followed imediatley, while nm-applet shows that it attempts to connect, by asking for a dhcp lease manually by issuing sudo dhclient3 from a shell. Network manager will show as unconnected, but wireless will work (with the exception that i have to manually get my lease when it expires, every 10 minutes - cron solved it). This was tested on both a WEP and a non-encrypted network. Network-manager works fine on wired, and the system is updated, and all the fixes proposed by a similar ubuntu bug did not have any effect on the behavior. Have I done something wrong - and if so, what can I do to fix it? Thank you in advance.

it seems that the combination of bcm4318+ndiswrapper+knetworkmanager is really buggy :(
at home I was able to solve the conenction problems by upgrading to ndiswrapper (1.42)
you might want to try this on your own system, you can find the instructions in one of the previous posts

---

### Post by rohan! on 2007-05-03
amilo pro v8010d

it worked for me :) but i had to remember to activate it using fn f12

so very happy! had been banging my head against a brick wall over this.

---

### Post by goodtimetribe on 2007-05-03
I popped the new Feisty LiveCD in last night and it booted my ASUS laptop after I disconnected the USB keyboard/mouse. I haven't tried installing it yet because I'm waiting until the semester's over (another week), but it did detect my wireless card as this broadcom. Is there something special I need to do to get it to work with the LiveCD? I wasn't able to get it to connect last night, but I was going to browse those instructions some more.

eth0 no wireless extensions.

Quote:
Originally Posted by hotshot23t View Post
OK thanks guys. Should I just be able to uninstall the native driver and then run ndiswrapper or am I going to have to do a fresh install? Either way the native driver isn't that great. I'll be going along and all of a sudden the light will go off and I have to reboot in order to get it working again. Thanks for your help!

-I just read your how to compwiz, it was the way I got everything working on edgy64! So considering there's no new kernel to change I need to 1. Get the headers 2. Run your script 3. Extract the newer ndiswrapper 4. Compile 5. Reboot and enjoy? Thanks again

i resolved my problem... just did this:
**sudo ndiswrapper -a 14e4:4319 bcmwl5**
and now it works

**ndiswrapper -l** gives me this:
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4319) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)

and everything is working fine!

### Post by michaelrupert on 2007-05-09
Thanks, got it working on the first try on my HP Pavilion dv8000 with a fresh install of Feisty Fawn. I should note that the same did not work on a not so fresh install of edgy. It definitely helps to start fresh if you have already tried other things.

### Post by Jonathan Harford on 2007-05-09
My story of success (in the hope it might help others).

I met with only limited success using the bcmxx drivers on my Compaq V5101US -- it would work for a while, then suddenly websites just wouldn't resolve. These drivers also had the hilarious side-effect of making my trackpad seriously misbehave.

I couldn't get ndiswrapper to work for a while. I could install the drivers, but
```
ndiswrapper -l
```
would only tell me
```
bcmwl5 : driver installed
```
but not that a device was present.

But then I found [this page](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper), which said specifically to get my bcmwl5.inf & .sys from the [list of hardware at the ndiswrapper website](http://ndiswrapper.sourceforge.net/joomla/index.php?/component/option,com_openwiki/Itemid,33/id,list_b/). There I found the driver that came with the V5105US laptop (not quite mine, but pretty close). When I installed this driver, ndiswrapper finally could see my device!

YAY

Strangely, "ndiswrapper -m" did not update my "modules" file, so that had to be done by hand. Oh, and ndiswrapper seems to have declared my card to be wlan0, but knetworkmanager sees it as eth1. Huh.

So: now wifi works, though I can only connect to the unsecured linksys router of a neighbor's. I get stuck at "Configuring Device" when I try to connect to my WPA-enabled router. Hopefully that won't be too difficult to fix.

---

Thanks for the help so far.

Edit--bug found:
Now I discover a bug with NdisWrapper as installed by your script.
After suspend--wait a few hours--resume, I can't get a wired internet connection.  I fixed the problem by reading your script and undoing it.  I removed Ndiswrapper with Synaptic, and deleted lines added to **/etc/modprobe.d/blacklist** and **/etc/modules**.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-05-12
> **Max Roswell said:**
> Quick question before I run this script.

I'm an Acer user (don't mean to brag), and I used your previous scripts for Dapper and Edgy with no problem, but I don't recall having to use special drivers.  I just remember running the script and being good to go.  Has something changed with Feisty?  Or is it likely I'll have the same easy experience?

With Feisty, there are two options: you can use the open source driver, which is slower, but has a couple extra features (like monitor mode) or you can use the script, which will get your full 54mbps, but without monitor mode.

If you don't know what monitor mode is, use the script :D

NEVER MIND! Fixed it. Reinstalled Feisty and used sudo apt-get install bcm43xx-fwcutter. And that was it. I'm going to restart just to make sure, but it seems that after 2 days of mucking around, a fresh start and a simple command got it working.

---

### Post by cjbrooker on 2007-05-15
After trying many things to get my wifi working for my Broadcom card (including fresh installs and running through this HOWTO many times), I finally decided to look at the log file generated by the script installation (RTFM!).

In it I noticed this error:

```
Reading package lists...
Building dependency tree...
Reading state information...
E: Couldn't find package ndiswrapper-common
apt-get died with exit status 100
```

So after another fresh install (probably not necessary but just in case), I installed ndiswrapper-common and ndiswrapper-utils-1.9 using Synaptic Package Manager. That installed fine. Then I ran through this HOWTO once more and by step 7 my wifi was working like a charm!!

**UPDATE:**
After a reboot the wifi was no longer connecting. Will try some other stuff and update when successful. I'm using an Acer 3004WLMi - damned thing!

---

### Post by rdk on 2007-05-15
hey all

just installed feisty after couple months of edgy.. love ubuntu :) the only problen for me so far is this broadcom issue.

after fresh instalation i used the first method.. at first sight everything was ok.. network manager sees wifi networks, sometimes even connects me; unfortunately i cant get ip adress anyhow :( 

all the networks that i use are open and should work without any prolblems.. what else can i do?

thx in advance

### Post by zeifertstc on 2007-05-16
> **gustavocm said:**
> i resolved my problem... just did this:
**sudo ndiswrapper -a 14e4:4319 bcmwl5**
and now it works

**ndiswrapper -l** gives me this:
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4319) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)

and everything is working fine!

Thank you for posting this fix. It has saved my sanity. Thanks still go to Compwiz18 for brilliant work with the initial driver that had initially saved my sanity when I started working with this laptop. Your thread is the only reason that Ubuntu (linux in general) has continued to exist on this system. Thank you all, who may have contributed.

---

### Post by turnovg on 2007-05-19
I figured out the problem - it was wrong driver it was wrong driver I was using. ...Or the driver could not be wrong (bcmwl5, bcmwl5a), but using this driver I always get the kernel bcm43xx driver active (alternate). Cannot remove it, cannot blacklist it. It's always their. So I took the original driver from the CD - NET2G54L.inf. Works like a charm!
(Using this driver the system does not add the kernel bcm43xx driver as alternate.)

Here's the procedure in short:

-----------------------------
0. wireless card not plugged in.
1. bcm43xx-fwcutter.deb - install
2. bcm43xx-fwcutter -w /lib/firmware/2.6.20-15-generic/ wl_apsta.o
3. dpkg -i ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils
4. ndiswrapper -i NET2G54L.inf
5. plugging wireless card...
8. ndiswrapper -m, ndiswrapper -mi, ndiswrapper -ma

Good luck everyone!


P.S.
Ubuntu 7.04 was fresh installed. (If you do many tries of installing the procedure described above could not work.)
(Or use the liveCD for trying. If successful - install Ubuntu and your wi-fi card :)

P.S.2
No need of any special actions like noapic at boot, blacklisting or rebooting...

---

### Post by Bosonator on 2007-05-21
> **H.E. Pennypacker said:**
> Following iwarp's advice, I am able to connect.  I first followed the instructions from 11-14 in the wiki.  I then installed ndiswrapper from source (the latest version - download it from ndiswrapper's website).  Then, I went to the first page of this thread.  I tried the Feisty script, but that did not work.  I then tried the general method, and that did work.  I am not sure if I had to reboot several times.

Make sure Network Manager is installed.  Without Network Manager being installed, Ubuntu's Networking Adminstration thingie will not allow you to roam.  All roaming means is that you are able to switch from one wireless network to another. 

I still have a problem with connecting to my own WPA2 network.  I am right now connected to my neighbor's unencrypted network.

I'm trying to get WPA to work. You say that after installing ndiswrapper from source the Feisty script doesn't work, but may I ask what is the "general method" that you refer to here? Is that covered in this HOWTO, or is it somewhere else? Thanks a bunch, eh!

### Post by Max Roswell on 2007-05-27
Quick and dumb questions:

I used this script under Dapper and Edgy; it worked great.

 When upgrading to Feisty, I was happy to see that the native bcm43xx driver seemed to work right away.  The card works and I can KIND OF get on my wireless network.  That's progress.

HOWEVER - the connection doesn't seem as strong (expected) and more importantly, my connection gets dropped a lot AND most of the time when I start up, my machine tries to connect to my neighbor's wireless network instead of mine.  I choose my network and sometimes it connects, but when I reboot, we go through the process again.  I assume that's the flakiness the OP is talking about.

So here are the dumb questions:

1.  The trying to connect to the wrong network thing:  is that network manager weirdness?  Would turning off roaming mode help me?
2.  Is it advisable to run this script now, even though the card's kind of working?
3.  If I switch from network manager to something else, how likely is it that my ability to connect will go up in smoke?

Thanks in advance for a response.

169.*.*.* is a local subnet also as far as I know, and also I think eth0:avah is for avahi??? maybe?  no idea :D

you may want to try a different wifi manager, as network-manager (the default on in the systray) doesn't work well sometimes.

wifi-radar (in the package manager) and wicd (which is in my sig) are two you can check out.

disclamer: wicd is my project.

oh yeah, and it looks like your connection is working fine, but network-manager isn't.

### Post by grivako on 2007-05-30
Well I have never posted on one of these sites as I never expect a response but im at a desperate point.  I've just installed Ubuntu Feisty Fawn for the first time on my Compaq Presario V2000 with a Broadcom 4318 wireless adapter built in.  When it was first installed it showed eth1 as wireless in the Network Settings window.  But even after inputting the IP address for DHCP and static even, neither one worked.  Also the wifi button did not light up blue as it had with Windows.   So I found this page, followed the steps laid out in the first area.  And when it got tot he part where I was supposed to go the System>Administration>Network, there was no longer a wireless card detected, only the ehternet as eth0, eth1 was gone.  

When i tried to configure anything in Terminal it usually told me 'E: Couldn't find package ndiswrapper-common' over and over no matter what i put in.  Nothing seems to work and the wireless card seems to have vanished.  I went to the Synaptics manager (as a side not im not connected to the internet as the wireless router is far from my PC so I can't stay in the other room to look at the scripts for what to do next so I am not connected to the internet through the linux) and looked under repositories, but network manager and etc were already there and it seemd Univers was already selected and running, as far as i could tell anyway.  As to what happened to my wireless card IDK.  I actually reformatted and reinstalled Ubuntu after a day or so of that, it came back up again, i went through your steps again and it disappeared once more.  I have no idea what to do now and it seems after going through tons of forum sites everyone assumes that once you install the ndiswrapper you card is supposed to appear in the Network Settings window, which it has not.  And they dont say what to do if that happens.  

Please give me some advice here, I switched to Linux but I did not realize it was going to be so difficult to do the most minor tasks compared to Windows.  Why is there so much complexity in this thing? 

Signed---Frustrated

### Post by Bosonator on 2007-05-30
Hi Grivako,

Well, take heart, because I've found the people in the Ubuntu forums are both responsive and courteous. This really is my favourite forum in which to post. 

As for your laptop, I have the exact same model, and I think I've seen the same problem as you. In any case, here's what I do:
1. Install Ubuntu. Actually I use Kubuntu, but I think the only difference is that I use the "knetworkmanager" instead of "network-manager-gnome".
2. Download and run Compwiz's script (on the first page of this thread)
3. Find a wired connection somehow and install the "ndiswrapper-common", "network-manager", and "network-manager-gnome" packages (supposedly this shouldn't be necessary, but it's what I always seem to have to do).
4. Run Compwiz's script again.

This is typically enough to get it working for me. Hope it does it for you too! If not, try posting again and including the "log" file that got created when you ran the script, and someone should be able to get you off the ground. 

- Bosonator

P.S. As an added (unrelated) bonus, take a look at this post to get your ATI graphics card configured for speed: [ATI Radeon Xpress]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=321766&highlight=radeon")

---

OK: I've used the very user-friendly code on the first post of this thread and even several of the small tips ppl have added along the way, including the reinstall of network-manager that Bostonator suggested. I am so close I can practically taste it. here's what I have...

### Post by Bosonator on 2007-05-30
> **bootiec84 said:**
> I'm very frustrated and out of ideas!

...This works, enabling me to see my wireless network in the manager. BUT when i try to connect, it just sits there and then times out, never connecting.

Do you have WPA encryption enabled on your router? If so, that would be your problem. There's a bug in the Feisty ndiswrapper drivers that doesn't allow connection using WPA. Frig, eh? Although I haven't tried it myself, there is a way to fix this by compiling the newest ndiswrapper from source. Maybe one of the nice men or women in this forum could point you to info about how to do that.

Or you could do what I did... roll back to Edgy Eft, where everything works beautifully. But I'm not a paragon of patience, so maybe I'm a bad example ;) Good luck, whatever you choose!

---

### Post by bootiec84 on 2007-06-01
Thnx Compwiz, I figured it out at 5am last night. Oddly enough it connected once I enabled WEP encryption. I don't know why, but i think i had made it too easy for the manager to connect and it was looking for some sort of encryption. SO...i switched over to wifi-radar and all is well now! It works like a charm and i don't have to retype my WEP code every time i boot.

For anyone who is still have having trouble w/ feisty on an AMD64, there are a few good posts on pg 118 of this thread.

---
