### Post by Supertonic on 2007-06-02
Hey guys-  First off I just want to say I'm a brand new Ubuntu user, and I want to thank the community for all the helpful knowledge contained on this forum.  

I just installed a dual boot WinXP/Ubuntu (Feisty Fawn)  about a week ago on my HP Pavilion dv5030 laptop.  Since then, I had been trying to configure my broadcom 4318 wireless card.  I would like to report with great overbearing joy I am finally writing this on my couch, away from the wireless router without the constriction of a LAN cable! 

**I must let you all know at this point that I am running a 64-bit install of Feisty**

I would like to share my efforts and processes that allowed me to come to my final, fully functional wireless (WPA) connection.     

First, I went and picked up a copy of _Beginning Ubuntu Linux_ by Keir Thomas.  This provided some helpful information, and continues to do so.  The only thing is that the book was written for 6.10 which I believe is Edgy?    Everything is still quite similar in Feisty as I have come to find.

After opening a terminal, run the command: 
```
lspci | grep Broadcom\ Corporation
```

My wireless card was recognized as 
*Broadcom Corporation BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller*

So it was recognized, but the native drivers were obviously not working because there was no success of connecting to my Linksys WRT54G router.

With the advice in Keir Thomas' book I began the process by identifying the wireless network hardware and sourcing drivers.  That can be accomplished by going to System>Preferences>Hardware Information.   

Scroll down to "WLAN Interface"  In the right panel click the "Advanced" tab and look for the entry *net physical_device*     It should show something to the effect of:
*strlist   /org/freedesktop/Hal/devices/_pci_14e4_4318_*

The "14e4_4318" is important because in the driver .inf file (when opened with a text editor) should correspond to your version of Broadcom card, which in this case is 14e4_4318.

**Onwards to the process of blacklisting the existing native drivers.**  This is accomplished by opening a terminal and issuing the following commands:
```
gksu gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```
This will open up the blacklist config file.  At the bottom I added the following:
```
blacklist bcm43xx
```
Save this file.
After a reboot, the wireless device is no longer recognized in the network manager.

**Onto installing ndiswrapper so you can install the WinXP drivers for the Broadcom device.**  This is where things got a little hairy for myself because: 1. I'm new to Linux, and 2. I didn't know that nidswrapper did not come installed on Feisty, which I guess it doesn't normally.

So, after connecting to my wired network (which connects to the internet just fine, as it should for almost everyone)  I ran Synaptics Package manager, and installed:
     ndiswrapper-common
     ndiswrapper-source
     ndiswrapper-utils-1.9  

I would also recommend running update manager at this time as well, and get all necessary updates to your system if it is a fresh install.   After all this updating, reboot.

**Now to download the necessary drivers to make the Broadcom device not only be recognized, but function as well.**
After much trial and error in installing drivers, I came upon this guide that pointed me to the correct driver that works.  You can download it here: [ftp://ftp.support.acer-euro.com/notebook/ferrari_4000/driver/winxp64bit/80211g.zip](ftp://ftp.support.acer-euro.com/notebook/ferrari_4000/driver/winxp64bit/80211g.zip)
*Please keep in mind this is the 64-bit driver*

**Now for the extraction and installation of the 4318 driver**
After you have downloaded the .zip file,  Select open with Archive Manager.  At this point you should create a folder on the desktop titled "Driver"   Drag the .inf and the .sys file into this new folder on the desktop.  In this case the files should be "bcmwl5.inf" and "bcmwl564.sys"

At this point just to be safe, if there are any existing drivers somehow installed with ndiswrapper we should remove them. Open a terminal and use these commands:
Put yourself in the correct folder:
```
cd /etc/ndiswrapper
```
then in this case, if there is any driver installed it will most likely be titled "bcmwl5"  So issue this command to remove it:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
```

Now to install the driver you just downloaded: 
 
In the same terminal type:
```
cd /home/<yourusername>/Desktop/Driver
```
This will put you into the folder in which you extracted the .inf driver file.
Next type:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```
Yay!
Ok now to make sure ndiswrapper loads this driver each time you boot up Ubuntu.  Issue the command:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -m
```
then open the modules configuration file:
```
gksu gedit /etc/modules
```
At the bottom of the list add:

  ndiswrapper

Make sure you save the file.   After this, reboot your box, and omg like weeeeeeeeeee! You should be able to use the wireless utility in the upper right corner and connect to whatever wireless network is detected.  WPA is working fine for me as I'm sure WEP would as well.

Hope this helps anyone out there.  Keep in mind I am pretty novice when it comes to Linux, and this is my first attempt to give back to the community, so enjoy!

---

### Post by Das Goat on 2007-06-10
Hi Everyone!

I had lots of trouble with the "easy" method, it only marginally worked. and for some reason i couldn't get the second method to work every time, plus i really wanted to use CompWiz's WICD project. So, after a few days and CompWiz's help, I got WICD and my wireless working like a champ. 

So to give back, here is a step by step guide to getting WICD and your Broadcom 4318 to work together.

I recommend saving this HTML page to your desk top before you do anything. Durring the part of the instructions you will be with out the internet for a few steps and you will probably want to be able to reference these instructions.


1) Start with a spanking fresh copy of Ubuntu 7.04.

2) Used your wire connection to get all your updates.

3) Go To [http://sourceforge.net/project/showfiles.php?group_id=194573](http://sourceforge.net/project/showfiles.php?group_id=194573) and down load wICD (I used 1.2.9)to your desk top

4) Go to System > Preferences > Sessions. Uncheck Network Manager. This will stop it from being loaded when you boot up. 

5) Time to remove Network manager and Network Manager Gnome. Open terminal window. Enter sudo aptitude remove network-manager.
(You can also use the Synaptics Package Manager to remove Network Manager which will also remove Network Manager Gnome automatically.)

6) Reboot. This will stop the error messages about Network Manager.

7) After rebooting, install the wicd package you downloaded to your desktop.

8) Go to System > Preferences > Sessions
Press "Add", call it WICD, enter /opt/wicd/tray-edgy.py
The box for WICD should already be checked so it starts automatically.
(When this is all done, you can come back and delete the Network Manager from sessions if you want to and if you like a tidy system)

9) Reboot. Now WICD will be running in the upper right corner where Network Manager was before.

10) Right click on the icon and select "connect". WICD manager will open asking for setting for your Wired connection. Normally, all you need to do is give this connection a name, like "Home-Wired". Hit Add. Then you can click connect.

11) You should now be connected via wire and can go back to broswing the Interenet.

12) Now to get the wireless working.
Go to [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=197102](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=197102) (the first page of this thread)

13) Be sure to use the SECOND method (Dapper and Edgy (and Feisty with ndiswrapper)). I could NOT get it to work with the "easy" method

14) Follow CompWiz's instructions 1 through 6 exactly. (I cut and past his commands so there are no errors)

15) Your wireless light should be on now. Take out the Unbuntu Installation CD and reboot, unplugging your wired connection.

16) When you come back up, right click the WICD icon, select "connect". You should see your wireless access point (make sure you set your router to Broadcast your SSID, you can probably turn it off later, but it is lots easier if you broadcast for the intital set up)

17) Next to your SSID, there is a drop down arrow where you can set your settings, like encription (I use WEP). I DO NOT recommend setting "connect automatically" untill you have used it for a few days to be sure you are working good. Then click the blue connect button.

18) Broswe the internet, you are done.

:popcorn:

---

### Post by goodtimetribe on 2007-06-11
This worked great for me on an ASUS A6Km (z9200km) with a 64bit AMD Turion ML-40. I couldn't install wifi radar, so I used the network manager. The only problem I had was selecting the encryption. There's 3 selections for 128bit WEP (128 bit WEP, 128 bit WEP ASCII and 128 bit WEP HEX..) the default is the first one (doesn't specify ASCII or HEX maybe they want me to type all 128 bits in...), and with my password it didn't work. I selected the 128bit WEP ASCII and it worked for me.

### Post by rsvirani on 2007-06-11
1.  Does anyone know how to edit the wireless profiles for a network?  Network Manager only asks for credentials the first time you connect to a new network.  After that, you're stuck with what you got.  

2.  Also, does anyone know how the edit the priority of the wireless networks?  NM also only takes the order you choose a network the first time as its priority.  It'll connect in that order every time.  This is annoying.

3.  Does anyone know how to get WPA support if you turn off roaming mode and use regular old network settings instead of NM?

---

### Post by Das Goat on 2007-06-12
> **rsvirani said:**
> 1.  Does anyone know how to edit the wireless profiles for a network?  Network Manager only asks for credentials the first time you connect to a new network.  After that, you're stuck with what you got.  

2.  Also, does anyone know how the edit the priority of the wireless networks?  NM also only takes the order you choose a network the first time as its priority.  It'll connect in that order every time.  This is annoying.

3.  Does anyone know how to get WPA support if you turn off roaming mode and use regular old network settings instead of NM?


this is a repost from back on page 45. It should point you in the right direction:

Randall, you fixed it!

this is what I was missing the whole time! I really didn't think it would work, since I have tried so many other fixes, but this is it!

When you can connect with out any encription, do this:

system > administration > networking

put your key in there. Let ubuntu time out with the new settings.

edit your interface file like Randall suggests:

iface wlan0 inet dhcp
wireless-essid Starfleet
wireless-key open XXXXXXXXXX

the key is to add the word 'open" before your key. I don't know why the network manager doesn't have this in there already, but it doesn't. and I don't know why wifi-radar doesn't add it either (I didn't test this, I only know it doesn't work) but I suspect that, as a Windows user, I am used to haveing the program do what i say. Perhaps there is a way to run wifi-radar as sudo that i don't know about or understand. But that is the fix! put open in there and it works!

I am going to reset all of my devices to use open, bring the network back up, and post to you all my successes (I KNOW it will work, i can feel it!).



PS: there is still no icon in network manager in the upper right that let me know the wireless is connected, but who cares if ti works, right?

---

### Post by apollocre on 2007-06-13
Actually, I just got it working and am posting this from within ubuntu.  What I did was I went to the ubuntu package collection online([here]("http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/")) and manually downloaded ndiswrapper-common and ndiswrapper-utils-1.9 (listed under miscellaneous.)  After that, I installed them and then installed the drivers from your package and after a restart, boom, it worked.  Thanks, after a two days of searching, I'm finally able to stay in ubuntu without having to go to windows for the internet.

---

### Post by IAmWhatWasDavidBowman on 2007-06-13
Hey All! I wanted to say thanks for all the collective work and knowledge in this thread (hell, in the whole Ubuntu community).

I have a Compaq 2414nr (v2000) laptop with this wireless card in it, and I was unsatisfied with the native driver's speed as I often pull over files from my tower and 54mbps is so much better than 11.

After 3 fresh installs of Feisty, I managed to get this to work using: a) the install script in the first post, and b) [[COLOR="Blue"]the instructions on how to compile my own updated ndiswrapper[/COLOR]]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper")

I currently have a WPA-enabled wireless network at home, and I didn't want to turn off my security to use ndiswrapper, and this fixed that problem.

Curiously, and as a note to compwiz18, wicd wouldn't connect to my network. I had to switch back to network-manager to get it to work. My network has the ESSID broadcast off and is running WPA-AES by the way.

I've been a fan of linux for a very long time, using various distro's off and on, but never really caring for any of them. Ubuntu really impressed me right away with it's live/install DVD option, and I think I may actually take the time to learn linux now. I certainly don't want Vista. ;)

Again, thanks for the help.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-06-13
> **rsvirani said:**
> I have the exact same laptop as you and this worked for me up until a couple weeks ago.  Some distro updates broke it.  I can't connect to my network using WPA anymore.  What version of ndiswrapper are you using?  I am using 1.9 from feisty.  Did you compile a different version per the link you said you used?

the Feisty ndiswrapper doesn't work with WPA for some reason, you will have to compile your own I think.  See **[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper)**

### Post by IAmWhatWasDavidBowman on 2007-06-14
> **rsvirani said:**
> I have the exact same laptop as you and this worked for me up until a couple weeks ago.  Some distro updates broke it.  I can't connect to my network using WPA anymore.  What version of ndiswrapper are you using?  I am using 1.9 from feisty.  Did you compile a different version per the link you said you used?
I compiled version 1.46 of ndiswrapper. The page linked in my above post and in the one from compwiz18 is what you need to follow. All is working well now (but don't turn off the card using any physical off switch as the drivers will not re-initialize afterwards).

> **compwiz18 said:**
> the Feisty ndiswrapper doesn't work with WPA for some reason, you will have to compile your own I think.  See **[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper)**

Yes, this is true. It's not hard, just follow the directions linked here, rsvirani.

### Post by JimGvo on 2007-06-15
It sort of worked.  I had a hardwired cable connected to my Compaq Presario 2610 and followd the instructions.  it installed fine and the light came on and ifconfig showed a connection.   I then rebooted, unplugging the wired ethernet to see if it would work without a connection.  No dice. The light was on but ifconfig showed no ip address.  I attempted a /etc/network restart but that caused eth1 to go away completely.

  I had to perform the tar and ./ndis... steps again to get it to work.  The ./ndis... step didn't work because the original install apparently deleted some of the necessary files.  However I'm happy I know how to make this play with the wireless card.

It worked for a while when I was using SuSE 10.1 but never worked after I upgraded to SuSE 10.2, so Installed Edgy and am quite happy at this point, even though it takes a few extra steps to make it work.

Jim.

### Post by rsvirani on 2007-06-20
> **compwiz18 said:**
> Rumor has it that WPA is broken in Feisty with ndiswrapper.

You may be right, but I must say that when I initially installed feisty from the cd that shipped from the man himself, WPA did work with this exact how-to.  I didnt even need to go a step further and do the wpa supplicant how-to.  However, after recent updates (May+June) including to the kernel, WPA has stopped working for me specifically on my router+wifi adapter combo.  I hope that my conflict becomes common to others and we can find the source of the issue.  However, I am afraid that instead the conclusion that 'all wifi adapters not atheros based suck' may be as conclusive as it gets.  If anyone else has their setup working just fine but cant get WPA to work, please post so we can see if it is common.

### Post by hamishau on 2007-06-21
Hi everyone

Just out of interest, I previously posted here that I was using Ubuntu 7.04 and a Broadcom 4318 card in an Asus A6Rp laptop, and had gotten it to work using this wonderful script.

Some feedback on it: it did not work every time, and sometimes I had to restart to get it to work (sometimes restarting X was enough). It occasionally took a very long time to connect, and it sometimes refused to connect to other networks at all (including at cafes which are unencrypted). The signal strength was not great, as it regularly says 50-60% when I am not more than 6 feet from the router. Boosting the wireless power in the router (I am using a Linksys WRT54GL with Thibor 15c firmware, so I can do that) made little difference. When I did get it working, which was most of the time, it was rock solid and didn't drop out. I have left the lappy on overnight torrenting things, and it was fine. However, it was not an optimal solution, and if I needed just to check my email from a cold boot, or do something online quickly, I wouldn't use Ubuntu, but boot into Windows. The hit on the extra boot time (about 2-3 times longer) was allayed by the reliability of the wireless connection.

Today I downloaded Mepis 32 6.5.02, and booted from the LiveCD. Interestingly, Mepis supports the Broadcom 4318 card "out of the box". Mepis also supports WPA (PSA-TKIP in my case) also "out of the box". The signal strength is 100%, which is the same as when I use Windows. I am now using the LiveCD to post this. This is a wonderful thing and I am now seriously looking to change to Mepis, as it also seems to have everything I use on a daily basis pre-installed (GPG for de- and encrypting my files on this laptop, Ksensors, automounting of all the drives I have, torrent client, etc). Although it seems that I will still have to solve issues with the webcam and the sound.

Also interestingly, running the base OS and Firefox to post this, it is using only 134MB RAM (out of 883MB available on my lappy), and no swap, so it isn't exactly resource hungry.

I hope this little review helps someone :-) 

Hamish

---

### Post by Tha-Fox on 2007-06-22
I executed the script in the first post. It went well but I didin't have acer_hk nor acer_acpi. Acer_acpi didn't installa properly so I installed acer_hk. After reboot nothing happened. Then I re-executed the script and my wlan0 disappeared. I read through the script's log file and the problem seems to be that for some reason the driver file hasn't installed properly. Could someone help me? I've been trying to get this damn thing to work since February and the hope is fading out :( Some things are in Finnish but I assume it's still pretty readable.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-06-22
> **tgbrowning said:**
> I confess myself stumped. ndiswrapper has installed the file okay. 

But I cannot get any response from the network, at all. No internet. Can't query the router, nothing.

So, here's the log file.  Hope somebody can figure out what I'm missing because I'm surely missing something.

It looks fine - are you sure the wireless card button is pressed and the light is on?  It says there is no wireless AP in range.
> **Tha-Fox said:**
> I executed the script in the first post. It went well but I didin't have acer_hk nor acer_acpi. Acer_acpi didn't installa properly so I installed acer_hk. After reboot nothing happened. Then I re-executed the script and my wlan0 disappeared. I read through the script's log file and the problem seems to be that for some reason the driver file hasn't installed properly. Could someone help me? I've been trying to get this damn thing to work since February and the hope is fading out :( Some things are in Finnish but I assume it's still pretty readable.

Try redoing the script from the beginning again. edit: rephrased: go to the first post and run the script, starting at step 1.  You are missing files.

---

### Post by tgbrowning on 2007-06-22
Yes, the light is on and the router is within range. In point of fact, this connection is via an an eight foot ethernet cable! 

What files am I missing?  The way the script is written, I find it hard to figure out -- plus I'm still having a hard time understanding precisely what the script is doing.

Query: Does the script automatically try to install a number of things and then when it fails, simply drop through? I find the fact that the log shows a number of places where ndiswrapper says words to the effect that it can't install the needed driver, disconcerting to say the least. But then, it hits that "assuming your system is 'appear to have a i386 system" and installs okay. I am reading that correctly, right?

I'm a bit hesitant to rerun the script because at this point, I've done something like six or seven installs of Ubuntu and frankly, I'm prepared to move on for now. I do NOT want to have to go back and start over again. I have far too much to do before I'm set up the way I want to be and frankly, at this point, since I took the network down and moved it to my office, I don't really need to have a wifi connection. I can get Internet access via a cable.

A nuisance, however.

Is there someone who would be willing to show me how what lines in the script I can comment out to clarify what's going on? If the script is indeed checking all possibilities before making the assumption that I'm running an i386 system, I see no need to screw around checking and it might make things clearer for me. Also, I have a sneaking suspicion that some of the checking might be confusing the system, somehow, and perhaps it would be better not to.  

A vague guess, but I spent too many years as an assembler programmer not to consider it. 

This is kind of fun in a threatening, frustrating way. Which is probably why I liked assembly language, now that I think of it.

Browning>>>

---

### Post by tgbrowning on 2007-06-22
Bingo!!

That did it.  

Had to remove several packages due to conflicts, make bloody sure that the Gnome  Package Manager wasn't running, and then tinker with the settings but I'm up and connected via the Broadcom card as I type.

Thank you!

Browning>>>

### Post by wallaper on 2007-06-23
I am really new to this and not that computer literate.  I've had Ubuntu dapper up & running with a wired connection but have now gone to a wireless, or at least am trying to!  i've been struggling with this for over a week and don't know what I'm doing wrong!

I followed the instructions at start of this post and those of linked thread of 'The Raven' but still cannot resolve it.

The wireless card is an 'ASUS' WL-138G V2 with the Broadcom 4318 chipset.   As I said I've followed the instructions (I think) and the wireless card has a steady green light.  I didn't mention it but it is a desktop PC not a laptop, does it make any difference to settings?  It shows up in network settings as wireless connection interface eth1 and all properties settings look ok.  When I try to save the default gateway device however it always reverts back to eth0 the wired ethernet connection.

I have also started it without the wired connection, same result.

When I look at  WIFI Radar it shows the correct SSID, full 'blue' signal strength, but 'connected to none IP'.  It just wont acquire the IP address.

### Post by tgbrowning on 2007-06-23
Walliaper,

Hang in there. It took me quite some time to get mine working but it can be done. 

You appear to be about the same place I was yesterday afternoon, in point of fact. From looking at what you sent, I would suggest you mess aroung with wicd, specifically with the encrption. That's what I changed around around a couple of times to get the connection up and running. I'm using WEP 1/2 with a nice long hexadecimal string in the router.    

Also, what router are you using?  Look over what you've got the router set to and make sure that it's what you think it is. Somewhere along the line, you should be able to determine just what the router is "seeing" and that's what is stopping you now, I think.  By the time I got to this point, I kind of lot sight of the fact that one can have a wifi card up and working fine, but the router has to *agree* that all of the protocols have been met. Sometimes the router is balking and not your card.

Browning>>>

---

### Post by wallaper on 2007-06-24
Thanks, I'll go back & check.

Router is a Motorola SBG900 'Surfboard' gateway (I am in OZ!)

I have another computer linking wirelessly with it using a USB and it works fine.  The settings for that are a WEP key 26 character hexadecimal? but it works fine

I've used exactly the same settings (SSID & WEP) here but just cannot find it!  There aren't any hidden tricks or other settings/protocols I should change are there.

As I said, I'm new to this and this is probably a stupid question, but WICD?? and encryption where?

Thanks for the time, no rush for reply, but I'll go to the other (3rd) computer & have a look at the router settings!

I wont give up, but hopefully will have it solved (with others help)  before insanity kicks in!

cheers

### Post by tgbrowning on 2007-06-24
wicd is a network manager that compwiz18 told me about. I was able to get the system to work only after I used it. Network manager didn't seem to be able to do it. 

I'm not sure I understand exactly what you mean -- you have gotten to the point of entering the WEP hex string you've used with your router, no?  

That particular dialog pops up when you start messing with the properties of the card if I remember correctly.

Using wicd, you would put it in under advanced setting. Note, I used wpa 1/2 and *NOT* wep.  I see no place under Network Manager to use wpa 1/2.  I imagine you probably can, somehow, but I'm not sure how.

If you don't know the difference between wep and wpa 1/2 go to 

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wired_Equivalent_Privacy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wired_Equivalent_Privacy)

My best guess is that half of the problems people are having with  wifi / ubuntu has to do with which wireless  security method is chosen.  Since wep was expanded to a 128 bit size -- the same length as wpa 1/2, it's easy to forget what you've got it set for.

Browning>>>

---

### Post by jocheem67 on 2007-06-24
Got my 4318 working again, now on an other machine. 

funny thing is that trying on a "old" feisty install , it didn't work out. Then did a clean install and after just doing the normal ubuntu update routine ( wired ), it's working again...so the suggestion that a clean install is mandatory seems in order...
Further on: it's very important I think to get rid of all ndiswrapper linking already present in a clean feisty...
Besides: it's a good thing to compile ndiswrapper yourself, it's not that hard...I'm using the 1.42. 1.47 is the newer one, but the 1.42 is now working on two machines for me, so it's a sure thing....

I'd like to note here that I really think that basically all the security settings are not essential in getting ndiswrapper to work with this chip....I really can't see the connection here...
Any thoughts?

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-06-26
@**tgbrowning** and **jocheem67**

> **jocheem67 said:**
> Well...wouldn't it be a good thing to be able to extract the issue of 

1)  ndiswrapper properly installed and not being able to connect

against

2) ndiswrapper not being properly installed


This would make it easier to find solutions I guess. But may be a very hard thing to accomplish.......

Let's see if we can get this figured out.

ndiswrapper being properly installed can be determined by this:
```
ndiswrapper -l
``` (that is a lowercase L not a 1 or an i)
the output from mine looks like```
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4318) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
```

If your output looks similar to mine, then ndiswrapper can't connect.  What does the output of ```
ndiswrapper -v
``` give you?  Also, what type of encryption (WPA, WEP, etc?) does your network use?

---

### Post by tgbrowning on 2007-06-26
Well, actually, the problem I had was that I got distracted by the looking over the log for the install.  It drops through a couple of attempts of doing things depending upon the actual sytem one has. 

You determine the distro running but if there's a problem discovering exactly what it is, it defaults to one type, dropping a few disconcerting error messages.  Then you drop through which CPU (?) type you have (in my case dumping me to i386) but along the way, it outputs that installation of ndiswrapper failed.  

Tha confused me until I finally thought it through and realized that the light coming on for the wifi pretty much had to mean that ndiswrapper had been installed, correctly with the broadcom .ini.

Don't get me wrong -- this How-To is the best I've seen, period.  But when you're completely new to a system (like I am to Ubuntu) and not terribly good with networks in general, it's easy to get both panicky and frustrated. 

Throw in the fact that some of us still cannot get the standard Network Manger to work and things get very confusing.

Browning>>>

---

I know the settings are correct as I have XP set up on the same pc using same hardware & provided drivers and it works, so it is not a hardware problem.  

The connection shows as eth1, ssid is as stated and WEP key is hexadecimal.  The only setting I changed in XP was the option for 64 or 128 bit hexadecimal.  (It uses 128!, the 26 character key) .  "enable this connection' is checked,   configuration is DHCP, it shows 'WIRELESS CONNECTION ETH1' as active, but default gateway device reverts to eth0 or 'blank' if I disable 'eth0' connection.

I am obviously doing something wrong but for life of me dont know what.

Its a relatively clean install, apart from last updates & installs of wicd, wifi radar, wlassistant & gnome network manager (not all at same time)

### Post by irfank on 2007-07-01
> **compwiz18 said:**
> Use the native bcm43xx driver. This driver is open source and included with the kernel. It can not run at any speed higher then 11mbps, is some what flaky, and supports promiscuous mode. Requires user to be somewhat close to the access point. Is easier to install.

### Post by LowMemory on 2007-07-02
Hello,

First, sorry I did not read all the messages of this thread. :-\"

This post is only to share a (little) problem I had following ndiswrapper installation with Feisty :
my wireless LAN card was not showing up at all and it was impossible to configure it !

The problem was that apt-get install command complained about packages not being authentified (warning : my OS is in french :p ):
```

Log of apt-get --assume-yes install ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils-1.9 
Mon Jul  2 17:05:13 2007

Lecture des listes de paquets...
Construction de l'arbre des dépendances...
Lecture de l'information d'état...
Les paquets suivants ont été automatiquement installés mais ne sont plus nécessaires :
  libgfortran1 libburn2 cdda2wav gwget ntp-simple libfile-basedir-perl
  libgnomeprint-data librpcsecgss3 libfile-mimeinfo-perl libxml1
  libfile-desktopentry-perl ntp libgdk-pixbuf2 libisofs2 libgnomeprint15 x-dev
  libgnomeprint-bin
Utiliser "apt-get autoremove" pour les supprimer
Paquets suggérés :
  ndiswrapper-source
Les NOUVEAUX paquets suivants seront installés :
  ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils-1.9
0 mis à jour, 2 nouvellement installés, 0 à enlever et 2 non mis à jour.
Il est nécessaire de prendre 50,1ko dans les archives.
Après dépaquetage, 225ko d'espace disque supplémentaires seront utilisés.
ATTENTION : les paquets suivants n'ont pas été authentifiés.
  ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils-1.9
E: Il y a des problèmes et -y a été employé sans --force-yes
apt-get died with exit status 100

```

In order to avoid this error, the following line in ndiswrapper_setup can be modified from :
```

output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log apt-get --assume-yes install ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils-1.9`

```
to
```

output=`logsave -a ~/Desktop/bcm4318-setup.log apt-get --force-yes install ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils-1.9`

```
(change --assume-yes to --force-yes)

Launching the script with this modification allowed me to have my wireless LAN card up and running !!

Keep up the good work !

### Post by forsberg on 2007-07-10
> **compwiz18 said:**
> If you don't mind, could you post the steps you followed to get it working for others?  It seems like a common stumbling point.

Sure no problem!

All the adjustments I had to make from the steps in #8 on HOWTO: Installing the acer_acpi module thread


1st:

wget [http://www.archernar.co.uk/acer_acpi/acer_acpi-0.3.tar.gz](http://www.archernar.co.uk/acer_acpi/acer_acpi-0.3.tar.gz)
tar zxvf acer_acpi-0.3.tar.gz
cd acer_acpi-0.3
make
sudo make install
cd ../..

Adjustments:

Link doesnt work, so now download from [http://aceracpi.googlecode.com/files/acer_acpi-0.6.tar.bz2](http://aceracpi.googlecode.com/files/acer_acpi-0.6.tar.bz2) and put it on my home/user/download folder.

tar -jzxvf acer_acpi-0.6.tar.bz2  (need to add j because of it is bz2 and not gz)
cd acer_acpi-0.6

Now the make and make install, this I had a lot of trouble with.  It just didn't work.  After reading, I found out that for the kernel I have, 2.6, I need to make the .ko file, so I did:

make acer_acpi.ko

The next step was the roughest.  The make install SEEMED like it worked because it didn't give me any errors, but really it didn't work because later when I need to run modprobe acer_acpi, it just fails with FATAL: module acer_acpi not found.  I did this to fix it:

cp -v acer_acpi.ko /lib/modules/2.6.16-20-generic/kernel/drivers/char/acer-acpi.ko

Note that 2.6.16-20-generic might be different, so you need to type "uname -r" to find out what it is.

Now I can do modprobe... so continuing on... but first replace su with sudo su (I get authorization failed with just typing su)

sudo su
modprobe acer_acpi
chmod 777 /proc/acpi/acer/wireless
echo "enabled: 1">/proc/acpi/acer/wireless
exit

Now for the final steps:

sudo gedit /etc/init.d/acer_acpi_wireless_enable

Paste that script in the thread in this file and save...

Run the rest:

su
chmod 755 /etc/init.d/acer_acpi_wireless_enable
update-rc.d acer_acpi_wireless_enable start 39 S . start 34 0 6 .
exit

Final check:

ls /etc/rcS.d/S39acer-acpi-wireless-enable
ls /etc/rc0.d/S34acer-acpi-wireless-enable
ls /etc/rc6.d/S34acer-acpi-wireless-enable

This desn't work.  No matter what it gave No such file or directory.  OK, so I manually went into those directories to check for the files, and to my surprise they were there.

Now reboot.   On reboot, wireless goes on automatically.  Type iwconfig in terminal, and it shows 54.0mbps.

### Post by sblanzio on 2007-07-15
> **forsberg said:**
> 
The make install SEEMED like it worked because it didn't give me any errors, but really it didn't work because later when I need to run modprobe acer_acpi, it just fails with FATAL: module acer_acpi not found.  I did this to fix it:

cp -v acer_acpi.ko /lib/modules/2.6.16-20-generic/kernel/drivers/char/acer-acpi.ko

Note that 2.6.16-20-generic might be different, so you need to type "uname -r" to find out what it is.


I can't understand why but i'm still getting the FATAL: saying device not found.

what can i do?

I can only say i had to download the 0.6.1 version

any help would be greatly appreciated

EDIT:

I tried even  0.4 version. No way.
 I append some of my code:

```
 

lspci | grep Broadcom\ Corporation
00:0b.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller (rev 02)

---

Desktop/0.6.1$ sudo make
Password:
make -C /lib/modules/`uname -r`/build SUBDIRS=/home/enrico/Desktop/0.6.1 modules
make[1]: Entering directory `/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.20-15-generic'
  CC [M]  /home/enrico/Desktop/0.6.1/acer_acpi.o
  Building modules, stage 2.
  MODPOST 1 modules
  CC      /home/enrico/Desktop/0.6.1/acer_acpi.mod.o
  LD [M]  /home/enrico/Desktop/0.6.1/acer_acpi.ko
make[1]: Leaving directory `/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.20-15-generic'

---

:~/Desktop/0.6.1$ sudo make install
mkdir -p /lib/modules//extra
cp -v acer_acpi.ko /lib/modules//extra/
`acer_acpi.ko' -> `/lib/modules//extra/acer_acpi.ko'
depmod -a
enrico@enrico-laptop:~/Desktop/0.6.1$ sudo cp -v acer_acpi.ko /lib/modules/2.6.20-15-generic/kernel/drivers/char/acer-acpi.ko
`acer_acpi.ko' -> `/lib/modules/2.6.20-15-generic/kernel/drivers/char/acer-acpi.ko'

---

sudo modprobe acer_acpi
FATAL: Error inserting acer_acpi (/lib/modules/2.6.20-15-generic/kernel/drivers/char/acer-acpi.ko): No such device
(same result even with sudo su)

```

I even tried to copy the .ko file and make sure it exists in the correct place but it still doesnt work.

dmesg says

[  282.172000] acer_acpi: Acer Laptop ACPI Extras version 0.6
[  282.172000] acer_acpi: No or unsupported WMI interface, unable to load.

Please help!

thanks
sblanzio

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-07-17
@**Apex-jb**: Well, looks like everything worked, but the computer can't find any networks...  My suggestion would be to check and make sure that there are networks actually in range (ie sit next to the wireless access point).  This problem has occured before, so I'm guessing that it isn't your fault that it can't find any networks :?  The other idea would be to push the wireless button on your computer so that it lights up (if you have a button)

Sometimes it will just start working out of its own free will.

And welcome to Ubuntu!

---

### Post by Apex-jb on 2007-07-17
> **compwiz18 said:**
> @**Apex-jb**: Well, looks like everything worked, but the computer can't find any networks...  My suggestion would be to check and make sure that there are networks actually in range (ie sit next to the wireless access point).  This problem has occured before, so I'm guessing that it isn't your fault that it can't find any networks :?  The other idea would be to push the wireless button on your computer so that it lights up (if you have a button)

Sometimes it will just start working out of its own free will.

And welcome to Ubuntu!So it actually did work? Hmm... I just reformated again to try and start over. Guess Ill do it again and see what happens. My access point is just downstairs from me and their are ample access points all around here. So I know there is a good signal where Im at. And my laptop does not have an external button.

### Post by Apex-jb on 2007-07-17
Holy ---- its working! I redid the process, still didnt work, looked at the post by raven, not sure everything worked from that post.... but all of a sudden it was showing wireless networks. Thanks

### Post by bigjay517 on 2007-07-18
I have followed the instructions to install the drivers and there is no wireless network card listed in my networking application in System > Adminstration.  Here is my log file.
This is a fresh install of Ubuntu.  After doing this my Wlan light is on.  Since there are no wireless network cards listed I can not connect to the internet.

---------------------------------- EDIT -----------------------------------
I was able to solve this problem on my own.  I compared my log with some other logs of people who you told had a good install.  I figured out that for some reason the ndiswrapper-common file was not installing from the CD.  I connected my self to the internet and ran the apt-get manually and then re-ran your script.  That worked and I am now posting from Ubuntu over my wireless connection.  My only problem now is that I need to turn the Wlan switch off then on after every boot to connect.  That is not a problem but if anyone has any tips all help would be appreciated. Now that my wireless is working this may possibly replace my Windows install on this laptop.  That was the only thing holding my back about a year ago.  Thank you for your script and support.  This is a great community and I am glad to be a part of it. 
```

If you need any further information just tell me which command I should enter into the terminal and I will post the result

I have no idea why but there wasn't any logfile on my desktop after I ran the script :(
(I did everything like described by raven)

My Laptop is an Asus. There is no Wlanswitch.
When I reboot to windows my wireless card is disabled. Then I have to start the asus utility an enable radio
windows wlan Confuguration recognises the card but doesnt find networks because the radio is disabled. mybe this is the hole problem!
we mybe just need to find a way to enable radio unter ubuntu with the standard driver

**EDIT: Problem Solved**
I connected to my lan via Cable an ran Autoupdate first to get the newest kernel
The I installed Ndiswrappergtk and Ndiswrapper Utils from Synaptics
After that I rebooted
The I went to System/Administration/Windows Wireless Drivers
I chose the Currently installed driver and clicked on the remove button and voila:
My wireles LED went on and it worked

### Post by tgbrowning on 2007-07-20
Feisty Fawn

Got a puzzle for you.  I've been dinking around with a second computer, exactly the same set up as the dV2000 I set up for my daughter, but apparently slightly later in HPs production cycle. The BIOS is slightly different and in point of fact, the wifi set up apparently uses a different firmware version for the Broadcom chipset.  

Instead of getting that nice id string that specifies that it's a Broadcom Corporation BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller (rev 02), I get a notice that it's a (rev 03), no brackets, no mention of 802.11g Wireless LAN. 

I went through the procedure three or four times now, getting odd results here and there, but cutting to the chase, I always seem to end up losing my eth0/LAN capability somewhere along the line. 

I've gone through quite a few posting on the web and seem to be playing a slow game of catch-up here, but a lot of this stuff is fairly confusing to me. 

What tests can I run to try and  determine where the problem actually is?  Or, does this new revision break things entirely? 

It's a bit difficult to know where to turn, partly because the damn light for the wifi is always on, period. (Either glowing red for the switch being off or blue for the switch being on). 

Suggestions?

Also, any ideas of how to figure out what is killing the regular ethernet network ability? 

Browning>>>

I can't say I've ever heard of a rev 3 card :?

Did you try downloading the .deb in the first post?  It is the broadcom driver, so it may do something magical that the script doesn't do.

Otherwise, you can run **ndiswrapper -l** (edit: that is a lowercase L and not a one or an i or anything else that resembles that shape) and see what the output is (and if it says that the hardware is detected)

try different ndiswrapper drivers, perhaps, maybe the ones that came with your computer?

### Post by lacerda_sch on 2007-08-03
Hey guys,

sorry, but I didn't have the commitment to read through all the 164 pages, so the answer might be somewhere in there.

I have a BCM4318 wireless card, and I used this guide to set up wireless with ndiswrapper. It worked, although it often took a lot of time to connect. It kept on trying to associate with the router, and suddenly after a seemingly random number of tries it succeeded.

I kept checking var/log/daemon.log to see what the cause of this is, but I couldn't come up with an answer.

Now the problem is that it won't connect anymore. It keeps on trying, but it won't succeed. And even if it would, it is really annoying - having to wait 5 minutes to get associated to a simple router.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-08-03
> **lacerda_sch said:**
> Hey guys,

sorry, but I didn't have the commitment to read through all the 164 pages, so the answer might be somewhere in there.

I have a BCM4318 wireless card, and I used this guide to set up wireless with ndiswrapper. It worked, although it often took a lot of time to connect. It kept on trying to associate with the router, and suddenly after a seemingly random number of tries it succeeded.

I kept checking var/log/daemon.log to see what the cause of this is, but I couldn't come up with an answer.

Now the problem is that it won't connect anymore. It keeps on trying, but it won't succeed. And even if it would, it is really annoying - having to wait 5 minutes to get associated to a simple router.

Does anybody know what is going on? Any help would be greatly appreciated (as it always is).

Some useful information:

/etc/network/interfaces:


output of ifconfig:


output of iwlist scan:


and a typical part of daemon.log | grep NetworkManager:
Haven't seen anything like this in the previous 163 pages, so it is probably a good thing you didn't read through the entire thing :P  I would probably suggest that you should try a different network manager, like wifi-radar or wicd (see my sig).

---

### Post by wba3107 on 2007-08-03
Thank you.  I have been screwing with this for hours.  Big help.  Kinda of new to Ubuntu I am running it on 1 desktop for internet use only, 1 desktop for tv, torrents and videos and now thanks to you my laptop.

---

### Post by zness on 2007-08-04
I was having a frustrating time getting this to work on an Acer Aspire 5100-3372 (Broadcom 4318), trying many different sources for bcmwl5.inf/sys.  Networks were detected but could not be associated.

I finally found a reported bug with ndiswrapper disassociation that prevents a successful attach ([<a href="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ndiswrapper/+bug/85468">https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ndiswrapper/+bug/85468</a>]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ndiswrapper/+bug/85468")).  

The workaround is to turn adapter off and on, or run (as root)

iwconfig ethX essid x mode managed

and reattach to the network.  Works like a (cludgy) charm.

The thread also has helpful init.d and acpi resume scripts to automate the workaround.


-z-

---

### Post by lacerda_sch on 2007-08-07
> **compwiz18 said:**
> Haven't seen anything like this in the previous 163 pages, so it is probably a good thing you didn't read through the entire thing :P  I would probably suggest that you should try a different network manager, like wifi-radar or wicd (see my sig).

The problem is, in order to install a new network manager, I would have to remove the previous one, which I am not that much eager to do (I use this laptop for work and stuff).

And the really disturbing part about my problem is that it sometimes does connect, sometimes it does not. It seems more or less random. I have no idea how that could be. If it worked perfectly, or didn't work at all, I wouldn't be surprised. But sometimes it associates instantly, sometimes it won't work even after 10 tries.

### Post by IndieRockSteve on 2007-08-14
I have a "Broadcom 4318" on my Compaq laptop, and the bcm43xx driver allows me to connect with wpa_supplicant but I can't get it to do any better than 802.11b (11Mb/s) where as before with the ndiswrapper but not using WPA I could get the fully 802.11g (54Mb/s).

I'd like to get both WPA AND 54Mb/s if thats possible. Am I loading something wrong or is there a module option for the bcm43xx driver I need to try?

thanks!

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-08-15
> **IndieRockSteve said:**
> I have a "Broadcom 4318" on my Compaq laptop, and the bcm43xx driver allows me to connect with wpa_supplicant but I can't get it to do any better than 802.11b (11Mb/s) where as before with the ndiswrapper but not using WPA I could get the fully 802.11g (54Mb/s).

I'd like to get both WPA AND 54Mb/s if thats possible. Am I loading something wrong or is there a module option for the bcm43xx driver I need to try?

thanks!

Yep, you just have to compile ndiswrapper.  It isn't hard, the wiki has a nice tutorial: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper), section 5

### Post by quiet101 on 2007-08-16
Never mind.  8)

I did the wiki ndiswrapper install again and then the ndiswrapper_setup and it worked.  I must have messed something up with the last ndiswrapper install attempt I did.

Thanks though... I don't think I would have gotten anywhere without this board.

### Post by IceCap on 2007-08-18
I have a Dell Latitude 840C with TrueMobile 1450 (Broadcom) running Feisty.
  I followed the directions here earlier using ndiswrapper but for some reason it failed at installing it.  So I manually installed the three packages (ndiswrapper common, utilities and ndisgtk).  Then I installed the bcm4318 drivers again.  And it worked, almost.
  When I run Network Tools the wireless card shows up as interface "eth1:avahi" running at 54Mpbs.  The problem is that when I press the configure button it comes with an error message "The Interface Does Not Exist".  When I click on the network icon (Manual Network Connections) I have selected the wireless network and put in the login informaiton and it gets the signal (~40-50% strength) but there is no connection.
  It looks like I need to tell it to use "eth1:avahi" but where and how?

  Thanks

  IC

### Post by boomcat on 2007-08-20
Wow! One hundred and sixty-five pages of instructions and comments and questions on installing just one chipset on just one card under just one Ubuntu release!

### Post by gl0wst1ckn1nja on 2007-08-20
so basically i got mine to work with ndiswrapper and the windows driver.

then i sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/ndiswrapper and replaced the wifi0 with eth1.  save reboot

connect.:guitar:

the only thing is 90% of the time i have to reboot to connect to new networks.  but its better than no wireless at all

bcm4318s can kiss my anus:lolflag:

### Post by boomcat on 2007-08-20
I've been trying for a week to get this Linksys PCMCIA WiFi with the Broadcom 4318 chipset to work in this old Thinkpad T20 laptop. I worked great when it was Win 2K Pro, but now I've loaded Feisty on it and it won't work. 

I've tried Compwiz18's procedure several times without result. The pilot light of the 4318 stays lit, but it is not seen by “iwconfig” or by Network Manager, or Wicd or WiFi Radar.

So I run the procedure again, and boot again, and run it again and so forth but the results are the same: except for the little green pilot light, it is as if that socket were empty.

After work I booted up the T20 (and I should mention that it is connected to a network cable continuously during all this, and the WiFi adapter is plugged in the entire time) and I opened Terminal and did this:

dave@k4rio:~$ **sudo apt-get install bcm43xx-fwcutter **
Password: 
Reading package lists... Done 
Building dependency tree       
Reading state information... Done 
bcm43xx-fwcutter is already the newest version. 
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 0 not upgraded. 
dave@k4rio:~$ **sudo modprobe bcm43xx **
dave@k4rio:~$ 

And then 

dave@k4rio:~$ **iwconfig **
lo        no wireless extensions. 

eth0      no wireless extensions. 

irda0     no wireless extensions. 

eth1      IEEE 802.11b/g  ESSID:"DaveChrisTim"  Nickname:"Broadcom 4318" 
          Mode:Managed  Frequency=2.462 GHz  Access Point: 00:0C:41:6E:FD:5C   
          Bit Rate=11 Mb/s   Tx-Power=19 dBm   
          RTS thr:off   Fragment thr:off 
          Link Quality=58/100  Signal level=-55 dBm  Noise level=-72 dBm 
          Rx invalid nwid:0  Rx invalid crypt:167  Rx invalid frag:0 
          Tx excessive retries:0  Invalid misc:0   Missed beacon:0 

dave@k4rio:~$ 

The damned thing is alive!

Network Manager did not see the new adapter, so I replaced it with Wicd, and Wicd DID see the WiFi, so I configured it for my network and tried Network Tools to ping my router. No response.

So I booted the computer and iwconfig could not find any wireless adapters. So again I ran 

dave@k4rio:~$ **sudo apt-get install bcm43xx-fwcutter **
Password: 
Reading package lists... Done 
Building dependency tree       
Reading state information... Done 
bcm43xx-fwcutter is already the newest version. 
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 0 not upgraded. 
dave@k4rio:~$ **sudo modprobe bcm43xx **
dave@k4rio:~$ 

and the WiFi was back, but it still couldn't ping the router. Every time I boot the T20 it comes up without WiFi but when I enter 

**sudo modprobe bcm43xx**

it comes back, even though it doesn't work.

But it's definitely an improvement over nothing!

Where should I go from here?

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-08-21
@**boomcat**: Looks good so far.  If you haven't already (installing wicd should do this), remove wifi-radar.  It conflicts with the bcm43xx driver if I remember, and make sure that the ndiswrapper module isn't loaded.  ie. **sudo rmmod ndiswrapper** Couple other things to note are that bcm43xx (at least when I tried it) had a bit of trouble with adhoc networks (ie it couldn't connect to them) and you need to be fairly close to the router when setting it up to make sure it works.

If you have any more questions I'm happy to answer them if I can :D

---

### Post by A3sthetix on 2007-08-21
I'm sure only 1/3 of the people using Ubuntu and this WLAN chipset have posted to this board. I think the next release of Ubuntu should address this issue and automatically have the WLAN work.

I'll be trying to install this later on today. Fingers crossed.

---

### Post by boomcat on 2007-08-21
Compwiz,
Here is what I propose to do to get my wireless going on my Thinkpad T20 with the WPC54G ver. 3 PCMCIA card. That's not a typo: WPC54G ver. 3. This uses the Broadcom BCM4318 chip. I double-checked with lspci.

I want to get all 54 Mbps, so I want to use the wrapper technique. This is based on nbound's post &#8220;Edgy Broadcom bcm4318 HOWTO! [Now updated for Feisty]&#8221;. I just added a few steps to help me clarify what I was doing. 


1)Turn off WEP at the router.
2)Make sure the network cable is attached to the laptop and the wireless card is inserted into its slot.
3)Use Synaptic Package Manager to remove Wifi-Radar, because it is suspected of interfering with the driver installation process.
4)Check the blacklist file:

sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

and make sure that bcm43xx is blacklisted.
5) lspci				                 ; should return all network card data
6) sudo rmmod bcm43xx	         ; remove this module from kernel
7) sudo rmmod ndiswrapper	; remove any old wrappers from kernel                                         
eight) sudo ndiswrapper &#8211;e bcmwl5		; remove current driver, if any
9) sudo ndiswrapper &#8211;l		      ; confirm removal
10) cat /etc/modules			 ; make sure that ndiswrapper is listed
11) cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist	; make sure that bcm43xx is there
12)  reboot
13) Unpack the drivers into the home folder from here:

[ftp://ftp.support.acer-euro.com/notebook/aspire_3020/driver/WLan%20Driver%20Broadcom%20802.11g%203.100.46.0.zip](ftp://ftp.support.acer-euro.com/notebook/aspire_3020/driver/WLan%20Driver%20Broadcom%20802.11g%203.100.46.0.zip)

14) sudo ndiswrapper -i /home/dave/bcmwl5.inf	      ; install drivers
15) sudo ndiswrapper &#8211;l					              ; confirm installation

  should return:
  Installed ndis Drivers:
  bcmwl5 driver present, hardware present

  or:
  bcmwl5: driver installed
 device (14E4:4318) present (Alternate ...)

16) sudo modprobe ndiswrapper		; add it to the kernel
17) ifconfig					          ; should return all network card data
18) reboot
19) ifconfig					          ; should return all network card data
20) iwconfig					; should return wireless network card data 
21) sudo iwlist eth1 scan	; should return a list of wireless
                                               ; access points found by eth1
22) install Wicd 

The problem is that I can't get past line 15. Line 15 doesn't return what it should. Instead it does this:

dave@k4rio:~$ sudo ndiswrapper &#8211;l

install/manage Windows drivers for ndiswrapper



usage: ndiswrapper OPTION

-i inffile               install driver described by 'inffile'

-a devid driver  use installed 'driver' for 'devid'

-r driver             remove 'driver'

-l                        list installed drivers

-m                     write configuration for modprobe

-ma                   write module alias configuration for all devices

-mi                    write module install configuration for all devices

-v                      report version information



where 'devid' is either PCIID or USBID of the form XXXX:XXXX,

as reported by 'lspci -n' or 'lsusb' for the card

dave@k4rio:~$ 
 


Do you have any suggestions?

### Post by boomcat on 2007-08-22
Compwiz,

OK, starting on my line 14, and using the tar.gz from the first page of this thread, I did this:

> sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf


driver bcmwl5 is already installed


> sudo ndiswrapper –l


install/manage Windows drivers for ndiswrapper



usage: ndiswrapper OPTION

-i inffile       install driver described by 'inffile'

-a devid driver  use installed 'driver' for 'devid'

-r driver        remove 'driver'

-m               write configuration for modprobe

02:00.0 0280: 14e4:4318 (rev 02)


> sudo ndiswrapper -a 14e4:4318 bcmwl5


driver 'bcmwl5' is not installed (properly)!

First, as you can see, the software version of my 4318 card is "rev 02" although the  actual item is labeled "rev 03"! Grrr!

Second, ndiswrapper doesn't seem to be working here. When I use the  -i option, isn't it supposed to overwrite any previous  driver?  And when I use the -l option it is supposed to return 
info, not help. And notice that the driver is reported to be improperly installed.

Any advice?

### Post by KertaLosataure on 2007-08-22
Hey. This worked for me and I'm running 7.04. You should change the "echo blacklist bcm43xx | sudo /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist" to 

"sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist"
Then go down to the bottom and add the line
"blacklist bcm43xx"

---

### Post by Alex Chiodo on 2007-08-22
Thanks!!! It worked!

Is root access necessary to execute any commands in your instructions?

The ones that involve modify files outside of your home directory do.

### Post by boomcat on 2007-08-23
I finally got it working, using these instructions:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3208474&postcount=16](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3208474&postcount=16)

Apparently I had a lot of detritus on my system from previous busted install attempts: scripts that tried to download files that were no longer there were a major problem. This left a lot of files and directories on my system that confused the situation and gave ambiguous error messages and advisories. Sometimes the ndiswrapper command would return the OPTIONS list; well what does that mean to me? Did the command succeed or not? Sometimes a command would return “file already exists”, and so forth. So these instructions worked great, took about 20 minutes, and I’ve got 54 Mbps wireless from the dreaded BCM4318, ver 02. Thanks to all, especially compwiz18.

---

That's the problem.  You have to have internet access while you run the script.  If this is absolutely impossible for you, post a quick message and I'll help you with installing without internet.

### Post by bgod13 on 2007-09-05
I have a Compaq Presario V2000 with the Broadcom bcm4318 wireless card.  I have been trying to get the wireless working for about five days with other how-to's, and yours finally got the job done... almost.  My only qualm is that I couldn't use WPA after the script.  The network-manager would hang at 28% before dropping.  

But, luckily I found a post thatmentioned that repository versions of ndiswrapper may not support WPA.  So, I compiled the latest ndiswrapper version from source, as seen at the bottom of this [how-to]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff"). 

But, that didn't take care of the problem totally, as seen in a warning message while installing the new ndiswrapper.  The driver for my wireless card was again missing/inactive.  So, I edited the script ndiswrapper_setup by deleting the part that installs the ndiswrapper package.  (As in, just delete the part between "#INSTALLING NDISWRAPPER" and "#DONE INSTALLING NDISWRAPPER") 

I ran it, and now my wireless card seems to be working great here at home with WPA security!  Perhaps you could have a look at changing it to compile from source instead of using the package, but I would never have been able to do this without this post,  I'm not quite proficient enough to start distributing scripts.

---

### Post by bgod13 on 2007-09-05
Oh yeah, and I'm running Feisty 7.04.  After trying Feisty a few times, I tried Gutsy 7.10 because I heard it had "solved" the problem with the bcm43xx card.  It didn't work, and I had a few program crashes in the first five minutes, so I went back to Feisty when I found these instructions above.

---

### Post by dukejones on 2007-09-14
That script sure acted like it was working... but every time I'd connect to a wireless network, my system would hang.  :-(  Very frustrating.

I'm on a Dell Inspiron 1100, Ubuntu 7.04, with a Linksys WPC54Gv3 card (Broadcom 4318 chip inside, of course).  

Eventually, I did a search on the "a" version of the driver I'd overheard rumors of.  (bcmwl5a), found a copy, did an ndiswrapper install on it, and poof, works great.  [Looks like just the inf is different?  No idea why this works, but I'm not complaining.]

Cheers!

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-09-20
Use the package manager to find and install 
```
ndiswrapper-common
ndiswrapper-utils-1.9
```
then rerun the script.

If you need help with that, just ask :D

---

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-09-21
Try running **sudo apt-get update** in a terminal then run the script again.  Make sure to put the CD in the drive and have an active internet connection when you do that though.

### Post by ckonrad on 2007-10-03
i dont really understand why you would need to put the CD in the drive first, i did it without the cd and it worked fine....unless you are assuming that a person didnt already have ndiswrapper installed but ndis isnt on most peoples cds they used to install fiesty anyways ndis isnt part of the fiest ISO at least it wasnt in mine. Some people i know had issues with this solution just because they thought ndis would install from the cd, it isnt on there so might as well just tell people to install using synaptic right off the bat.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-10-03
> **ckonrad said:**
> i dont really understand why you would need to put the CD in the drive first, i did it without the cd and it worked fine....unless you are assuming that a person didnt already have ndiswrapper installed but ndis isnt on most peoples cds they used to install fiesty anyways ndis isnt part of the fiest ISO at least it wasnt in mine. Some people i know had issues with this solution just because they thought ndis would install from the cd, it isnt on there so might as well just tell people to install using synaptic right off the bat.

Some packages (I think both ndiswrapper ones) are on some people's CD, so if you don't put the CD in, it will hang.  It is possible to remove the CD from the repository list, but it's more complicated then simply putting the CD in the drive.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-10-03
> **Infomaniac said:**
> Wicd halts when getting the IP (or something like that) and gives up a bit later
Any help?? :-(

Sounds like you may have entered your encryption key wrong, or you router isn't responding to the request for an IP.

If you're using encryption, check the key.  If you're not, post here and I'll give you a hand.

---

### Post by Infomaniac on 2007-10-04
I'm pretty sure I've typed the correct key...tried it many many times.

And it "answers" with the IP for Windows..anyway, could I choose my IP in Wicd?? Does it NEEDS a DHCP server to work??(it IS working, but I could fix it just in case if wicd lets it)

Thanks

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-10-04
Yep, you can specify an IP in Wicd, just use the Advanced Settings boxes.  Also, you can try putting quotes around your key, ie "key" instead of key.  Sometimes that helps.

---

### Post by 2kideas on 2007-10-05
Well it is now October 2007 and I  just wanted to add that it worked with the Broadcom 4319 chipset on an HP Pavillion DV8000 for a 24 hour newbie to Ubuntu and the whole non Windows world.

Wish I found this 12 hours ago!!  Brilliant and many thanks!!!

Richard Young
2000winningideas.com

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-10-05
> **Infomaniac said:**
> I'm pretty sure I've typed the correct key...tried it many many times.

And it "answers" with the IP for Windows..anyway, could I choose my IP in Wicd?? Does it NEEDS a DHCP server to work??(it IS working, but I could fix it just in case if wicd lets it)

Thanks

Hey, if you're still looking for a fix for this, I think I know what the problem is.  If you are using WPA, then you'll need to recompile ndiswrapper by following the tutorial on the wiki: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper) in section 5

There is a bug that doesn't allow WPA to work with ndiswrapper, if you are using Feisty.  **If you are using Gutsy then everything should work...**

---

### Post by Infomaniac on 2007-10-05
> **compwiz18 said:**
> Hey, if you're still looking for a fix for this, I think I know what the problem is.  If you are using WPA, then you'll need to recompile ndiswrapper by following the tutorial on the wiki: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/Ndiswrapper) in section 5

There is a bug that doesn't allow WPA to work with ndiswrapper, if you are using Feisty.  **If you are using Gutsy then everything should work...**

My God...that's scary :-P

Think I'll just give gutsy a try, then !

That's the same as the first solution on the first post :)

---

### Post by gear_up on 2007-10-15
I have a Gateway MX7118 laptop running 64 bit Ubuntu - Feisty- , which has been perfectly cured using this procedure.  What is notable is that while the wireless card **IS** recognized by the system right after installation of Ubuntu, the method of turning the "radio" on and off via a keyboard combination, specifically, the "Fn+F4" combination, is not supported, or not enabled.  So....

The problem is that without doing the specified procedure as outlined in the initial post here, the "radio" remains disabled, and the key combination is not recognized by Ubuntu either, and therefore, you have no ability to use the wireless.  I'm sure the procedure may solve other issues as well, but for my computer, that was all it took....

After performing the specified procedure, everything came up and worked,  albeit after a system reboot. 

Bravo... well done... thanks!

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-10-18
> **bg1256 said:**
> I am wondering how to undo what was done by this script?  I'm sorry if it's a stupid question.... just not sure what to run to reverse it.

The reason I would want to do this: I don't actually have this card... oops.

Thanks!

sure, just run ```
ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5
``` to take out the driver, then use the package manager to remove ndiswrapper, and that should pretty much clear it up for you.

Good luck :)

---

### Post by Mathiasdm on 2007-10-18
> **compwiz18 said:**
> sure, just run ```
ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5
``` to take out the driver, then use the package manager to remove ndiswrapper, and that should pretty much clear it up for you.

Good luck :)

I'd like to do the same thing and give the Linux bcm43xx driver a try (Gutsy Gibbon by the way).
Do I just do the same as you said, or do I need to do anything else? (I remember something about the bcm43xx driver being blacklisted or something)

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-10-18
> **Mathiasdm said:**
> I'd like to do the same thing and give the Linux bcm43xx driver a try (Gutsy Gibbon by the way).
Do I just do the same as you said, or do I need to do anything else? (I remember something about the bcm43xx driver being blacklisted or something)
Yeah, check /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist and remove bcm43xx if you see it in there.

### Post by juzzblack on 2007-10-28
After trying unsuccessfully to get my built in Broadcom 4318 wireless working, using acer_acpi and ndiswrapper using Gutsy 7.10 on my Acer Aspire 3020, I finally got it working using the native bcm43xx Linux driver and installing the bcm43xx-fwcutter package.

Worked straight away! :)

I followed this thread:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty)

If you're using an Acer laptop, I think you may still need acer_acpi installing first.

acer_acpi can be installed using Synaptic package manager, if you add:

deb-src [http://www.mumblyworld.info/ubuntu](http://www.mumblyworld.info/ubuntu) gutsy main 

 to your software sources list (System>Administration>Software Sources)

And the authentication key - copy and paste this into a terminal:

wget [http://www.mumblyworld.info/ubuntu/depot.key](http://www.mumblyworld.info/ubuntu/depot.key) -O- | sudo apt-key add - 

Hope this helps somebody.

---

### Post by Teh Dave on 2007-10-29

I finally got fed up with Windows XP (strangest error ever, don't want to talk about it), and decided to make the switch to Linux. Decided on UBUNTU because I've heard it's an easy distro to get started with.

Have a custom machine, older Pentium 4, with a linksys PCI-slot Wireless card running (per lspci) the Broadcom 4318 chipset.

Ran this, and can't access my router. {edit = Couldn't access my router beforehand, so this couldn't have made it any worse -_-}
```
tehdave@tehRig:~$ sudo iwconfig

lo       no wireless extensions.


eth0     IEEE 802.11b/g  ESSID:"linksys"  Nickname:"Broadcom 4318"

         Mode:Managed  Frequency=2.422 GHz  Access Point: Invalid
   
      RTS thr:off   Fragment thr:off

         Encryption key:XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XX   Security mode:open

         Rx invalid nwid:0  Rx invalid crypt:0  Rx invalid frag:0
         
         Tx excessive retries:0  Invalid misc:0   Missed beacon:0


tehdave@tehRig:~$ 
```

All the settings are correct (same as the settings on the laptop I'm using right next to the rig), but for some reason, can't connect.

I saw several posts browsing this thread about the UBUNTU native drivers being weak as far as picking up signal, but under Winblows, I never had an issue (same card, same hardware setup, etc.), and my laptop is connecting just fine (so it's an issue with my desktop, obviously.)

If it helps any, the router I'm using is a linksys WRT54GS.

Would have posted a copy of the logfile created by the install script, but unfortunately, none was created by the file I used (apparently latest copy of bcm4318.tar.gz, taken from first post)

### Post by juzzblack on 2007-10-29
Well so far I've not noticed any problem with a weak or slow signal using the native driver. If you're having trouble getting ndiswrapper to work with a Broadcom 4318 card I'd really recommend you give the native driver a try.

Have a look at this link and try following step '1.3. Using the native drivers'

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Wi...bcm43xx/Feisty](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Wi...bcm43xx/Feisty)

Sorry, I can't really help with anything more technical beyond that.

### Post by Guitar John on 2007-10-31
> **juzzblack said:**
> Well so far I've not noticed any problem with a weak or slow signal using the native driver. If you're having trouble getting ndiswrapper to work with a Broadcom 4318 card I'd really recommend you give the native driver a try.

Have a look at this link and try following step '1.3. Using the native drivers'

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Wi...bcm43xx/Feisty](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Wi...bcm43xx/Feisty)

Sorry, I can't really help with anything more technical beyond that.

My wifes laptop had a Belkin USB adapter which works great.  98% signal on the 2nd floor (the wireless router is on the 1st floor).   The problem with that device is that you have this USB stick sticking out the back of the laptop.  It looks rather obscene.  And it could get broken very easily.

After a clean install of Gutsy, I used the* Restricted Drivers Manager* to try to get the PCI card (with Broadcom chipset) working.   Well it worked, but sitting 15 feet from the router (in the same room) the signal strength was only 65-70%.

### Post by juzzblack on 2007-11-01
> **SimonFinch said:**
> Acer 3020 Laptop
Ubuntu 7.10 on a clean install.
Using Restricted Drivers Firmware for Broadcom 43xx

Have NOT done this yet:
[URL="http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=224350&highlight=acer+3020"]HOW TO: Installing the acer_acpi module - activates wireless on Acer
[/URL]

Should I do this?



Yes, I'd say you definitely need to install the acer_acpi module on an Acer laptop because the built in wifi card is software driven on an Acer. So you need this module loaded to turn on your wifi at boot and you should see the wifi light come on on your laptop.

---

### Post by juzzblack on 2007-11-01
> **Guitar John said:**
> My wifes laptop had a Belkin USB adapter which works great.  98% signal on the 2nd floor (the wireless router is on the 1st floor).   The problem with that device is that you have this USB stick sticking out the back of the laptop.  It looks rather obscene.  And it could get broken very easily.

After a clean install of Gutsy, I used the* Restricted Drivers Manager* to try to get the PCI card (with Broadcom chipset) working.   Well it worked, but sitting 15 feet from the router (in the same room) the signal strength was only 65-70%.

I must admit I've not tested my signal strength at any distance from my router yet, as I tend to use the laptop within a couple of metres of my modem anyway. Perhaps using the native driver is not the best solution for everyone if you use your wifi at a fair distance from your router. But if not, I'd still suggest trying it first,  before ndiswrapper, as it seems to be the easiest way to get wifi working with a Broadcom chipset.

---

### Post by Schuby on 2007-11-22
Are you running Gutsy on a Dell Latitude D600 with this wireless chipset?

Does it disappear after installing ndiswrapper?

Well after much fiddling I figured it out!

Bassicly using the script provided does work...sort of.

What you do is go into your package manager and select "ndiswrapper-common" (for some reason it doesn't get installed).  Install this package, and voila!  Works great!

This is after a fresh install of Gutsy followed by the security updates.

Now everything works perfectly.

Good luck!

### Post by jnigro on 2007-11-26

### Post by jmelton on 2007-11-29
Although I appreciate the effort put into this HowTo, most of it is completely irrelevant to those of us with recent G4 Macs (which have a Broadcom 4318 chipset in the wireless card), because ndiswrapper doesn't work on pre-Intel Macs.  It would be great to see an expansion of this HowTo, or a new one, with information about how to set up the 4318 if you have a PPC Mac.  I'm having enormous difficulty with mine.

---

### Post by jmelton on 2007-11-29
[QUOTE=hbuser;3843673]ok, i went with the wrapper method with a few adjustments at the top: 
the tar.gz file didn't download to my desktop, so i extracted it using the...um...the visual interface.  after i cd'd to the directory i ran the setup command.  it ran it, told me i was good to go, and said to restart if i had problems.  after i restarted "wireless" wasnt even an option anymore in "Network" under "System." [QUOTE]

My understanding is that ndiswrapper won't work with a (pre-Intel) Mac.  I made the same mistake as you because so many sources including this HOWTO assume that everybody with a Broadcom 43xx wireless card has a PC, not a Mac.  I'm still working on getting my (iBook G4 Mac) wireless going, too.  Let me know if you have any success.

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2007-11-29
> **jmelton said:**
> Although I appreciate the effort put into this HowTo, most of it is completely irrelevant to those of us with recent G4 Macs (which have a Broadcom 4318 chipset in the wireless card), because ndiswrapper doesn't work on pre-Intel Macs.  It would be great to see an expansion of this HowTo, or a new one, with information about how to set up the 4318 if you have a PPC Mac.  I'm having enormous difficulty with mine.
But not all of us have G4 Macs.... :p

I have no experience with them, otherwise I'd try and give you a hand. My best guess would be try bcm43xx driver.

### Post by TomR60 on 2007-12-08
It works!!

I had dumped Windows 2000 on an old laptop (Dell C610) due to it running so slow.  Installed Ubuntu and everything worked - except the Linksys wireless card in the PCMCIA slot.  This procedure worked, although it was rather cryptic for a newbe to Linux like me.

Thanks again!

### Post by Nackereia on 2007-12-09

The "Dapper and Edgy (and Feisty/Gutsy with ndiswrapper)" method totally worked for me! Thanks! :D
