### Post by buccaneere on 2007-12-11
> **compwiz18 said:**
> **About**


**Dapper and Edgy (and Feisty/Gutsy with ndiswrapper)**

[LIST=1]
[*]Put the CD that you installed Ubuntu with in the CD drive.
[*]Download [this file](http://ubuntuforums.org/attachment.php?attachmentid=48087&d=1193544558) to your Desktop (the Firefox default, so if you haven't changed it, that's where it went/will go).
[*]Open a terminal (click the Applications button, then Accessories, and then Terminal)
[*]Change the current directory to the desktop (copy and paste the following commands **exactly** into your terminal by right clicking anywhere on the terminal and clicking paste)
```
cd ~/Desktop
```
[*]Extract the compressed file
```
tar -xf bcm4318*.tar.gz
```
[*]Run the script, which will install ndiswrapper on your system, and set it up.
```
sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup
```
[*]Use the internet (you will have to open the System menu at the top of the screen, go to Administration, and then click Networking.  Configure the interface eth1 or wlan0, and connect to your wifi network)
[*]If you are an Acer user, you will need to [use the acerhk driver]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2999631&postcount=1595").
[*]If it doesn't work, reboot.
[*]If that doesn't work, read the troubleshooting section below.
[*]If you still can't make it work, try reading [this post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=1189681&postcount=105") by The Raven, which is so long I can't even fit it in here without doubling the length of the post =D> 
[/LIST]


**Troubleshooting**

[LIST]
[*]If the normal Feisty method fails, try the Edgy/Dapper method.  It also has support for Feisty using ndiswrapper.
[*]Try ```
sudo ndiswrapper -a 14e4:4319 bcmwl5
``` and then [CODE]
**[COLOR="Red"]sudo rmmod bcm43xx[/COLOR]**


.

I got to here - remove bcm module 43xx.

The return was 'module doesn't exist'. Module 4318 didn't exist to remove either.

Should it have been there to remove??? I followed all directions up to that point.

The machine is offline (with ethernet also), so logfile will be difficult to post... For what should I look in the logfile?

---

### Post by buccaneere on 2007-12-11
> **buccaneere said:**
> >  compwiz18

 Originally Posted by compwiz18  View Post
About


Dapper and Edgy (and Feisty/Gutsy with ndiswrapper)

   1. Put the CD that you installed Ubuntu with in the CD drive.
   2. Download this file to your Desktop (the Firefox default, so if you haven't changed it, that's where it went/will go).
   3. Open a terminal (click the Applications button, then Accessories, and then Terminal)
   4. Change the current directory to the desktop (copy and paste the following commands exactly into your terminal by right clicking anywhere on the terminal and clicking paste)
      Code:

      cd ~/Desktop

   5. Extract the compressed file
      Code:

      tar -xf bcm4318*.tar.gz

   6. Run the script, which will install ndiswrapper on your system, and set it up.
      Code:

      sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup

   7. Use the internet (you will have to open the System menu at the top of the screen, go to Administration, and then click Networking. Configure the interface eth1 or wlan0, and connect to your wifi network)
   8. If you are an Acer user, you will need to use the acerhk driver.
   9. If it doesn't work, reboot.
  10. If that doesn't work, read the troubleshooting section below.
  11. If you still can't make it work, try reading this post by The Raven, which is so long I can't even fit it in here without doubling the length of the post



Troubleshooting
[list][*]If the normal Feisty method fails, try the Edgy/Dapper method. It also has support for Feisty using ndiswrapper.[*]Try
Code:

sudo ndiswrapper -a 14e4:4319 bcmwl5

and then [code]
**[COLOR="Red"]sudo rmmod bcm43xx[/COLOR]**


I got to here - remove bcm module 43xx.

The return was 'module doesn't exist'. Module 4318 didn't exist to remove either.

Should it have been there to remove??? I followed all directions up to that point.

The machine is offline (with ethernet also), so logfile will be difficult to post... For what should I look in the logfile?

In order to post logfile, I must reconnect with ethernet (and reconfigure to allow it), because of the length of the logfile.

---

----------------------------------------------------------------

	5.)If you haven't already done so, go ahead and move your 'bcm4318[1].tar.gz' package
		to your desktop (a.k.a.- shell, be it Gnome ,KDE; etc.)

----------------------------------------------------------------

### Post by bswindell77 on 2007-12-20
If you have 7.10 you should no longer have a problem with this card or a lot of others. Juat activate the restricted drivers in the System>Admin>Restricted Drivers manager. It will download the software needed then ask show your firmware (driver disk with windows driverers) and that's it. I set up 3 completely different computers with different wifi cards and they all work perfect.

### Post by dugh on 2008-01-06
Don't use these instructions, they don't work and are out of date.

If you've got a broadcom 4318 (like a lot of dells) or something similar, use these instructions, they work for many laptops, even though the title of the thread just says Dell E1505:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=297092](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=297092)


You download the latest ndiswrapper from the sourceforge site (1.51 as of now), uninstall the old ndiswrapper and wireless drivers, compile and install the new ndiswrapper and set it to use your windows driver.
The R151517.exe works for many dells.  Just download what dells recommends you (but get the xp driver, not vista, if there is a difference).

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2008-01-06
> **dugh said:**
> Don't use these instructions, they don't work and are out of date.

If you've got a broadcom 4318 (like a lot of dells) or something similar, use these instructions, they work for many laptops, even though the title of the thread just says Dell E1505:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=297092](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=297092)


You download the latest ndiswrapper from the sourceforge site (1.51 as of now), uninstall the old ndiswrapper and wireless drivers, compile and install the new ndiswrapper and set it to use your windows driver.
The R151517.exe works for many dells.  Just download what dells recommends you (but get the xp driver, not vista, if there is a difference).
These instructions are not old and are not out of date.  They also work.  I use them myself.  Anyway, compiling ndiswrapper is a valid solution if you can't get it to work for some reason.:KS

### Post by joukahainen on 2008-01-22
I got Buffalo WLI-CB-G54HP  (Broadcom 4318 ) working in Ubunty Gutsy. Here is a little Howto :

First install bcm43xx drivers :

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Gutsy](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Gutsy)

In my case I could connect to unencrypted channels after that (Fon router), but WPA connection was not succesfull with network manager (I could see WPA encrypted channels but could not connect).

This was solved by installing Wicd :

1. Open a terminal, and add repository in your /etc/apt/sources.list:

sudo gedit /etc/apt/sources.list 

2. add the following line:

deb [http://apt.wicd.net](http://apt.wicd.net) gutsy extras 

3. Perform update and install the program:

sudo apt-get update
apt-get install Wicd

4. Add Wicd to startup programs

Go to System> Preferences> Sessions. In the Startup Programs tab, click Add and in the Name field with:

Wicd 

In the field Command put:

 /opt/wicd/tray.py

And here is a link to a blog about Wicd and wpa :

[http://racoon97.blogspot.com/2007/10/wicd-finally-wifi-can-operates-under.html](http://racoon97.blogspot.com/2007/10/wicd-finally-wifi-can-operates-under.html)

### Post by rlevini on 2008-01-24
Thanks for the files and the how-to. I have a WMP54GS pci wireless card and was struggling a little. After following the ndiswrapper steps I could scan and see the ap, but not associate with the network. It kept timing out. After retrying the setup steps a bunch of times I finally went and looked at /etc/network/interfaces and saw that eth1 (what my wlan0 gets aliased to) had no entry at all. I added a line 'auto eth1', rebooted and like magic networkmanager was able to join the network. Awesome.

### Post by ionospheric on 2008-02-10
I have wrestled with the Broadcom wireless card in my Dell Latitude laptop for months- sometimes it worked (ndiswrapper), sometimes it didn't, especially in WPA protected networks.
Last week, I solved the problem for good.

1.) I bought an HP nc6400 notebook with an Intel PRO wireless 3945 card in a liquidation sale, and it worked right out of the box.
2.) I will either replace the Broadcom card in my Dell laptop with an Intel or an Atheros one, or sell the Dell laptop.
3.) I will never, EVER, buy a laptop with a Broadcom wireless card, ever again!

I truly appreciate the help in this thread I have received in the past, but Broadcom should have started to support Linux a long time ago. Good bye, Broadcom!

### Post by capitansalva on 2008-02-21
Lettura della lista dei pacchetti in corso...
**Reading of packet list in progress...**

 Generazione dell'albero delle dipendenze in corso...
**Creation of 'dependencies tree' in progress...**

 E: Il pacchetto ndiswrapper-utils non ha candidati da installare
**E: The packet ndiswrapper-utils has no canditates to install**

 Il pacchetto ndiswrapper-utils non ha versioni disponibili, ma è nominato da un altro
 pacchetto. Questo significa che il pacchetto manca, è diventato obsoleto
 o è disponibile solo all'interno di un'altra sorgente
**The packet ndiswrapper-utils has no available versions, but is nominated by another packet. This means that the packet is missing, is obsolete or is only available inside of another source**

 apt-get died with exit status 100
**this is in english ( i think....)** :oD

### Post by mmbates on 2008-02-23
G'day,
I am having some issues with my ASUS WL-138G v2 PCI wireless adapter for a AMD64 custom built system.

I have tried both the "native" bcm43xx driver and the ndiswrapper driver.  Both work...kinda.  

The problem I have is similar to that of lacerda_sch (see post [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3127600#post3127600](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3127600#post3127600)) -- however, I don't know whether (s)he sorted it out or not (the solution suggested was to try a different network manager).

So, the symptoms are (with both drivers) that the driver appears to work ok, I can scan networks within range -- but it will not connect to the routher, or if it does it is very, very unreliable and drops out.

It may also be worth noting that I got myself into a big mess with many different flavours of driver, and it ended up that I couldn't get any of them to work.  So, I did a fresh install and have tried again (using only the methods described in this thread).  I had previously tried turning off IPv6 -- but this did not help the problem either (note, that on the fresh install i.e. all the output below, IPv6 has not been blacklisted).  My Router is set up to accept any MAC address.

I have tried with both network manager and manual connect (see Kevdog's extremely helpful thread: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=571188](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=571188)) -- and neither work.  I am using WEP, but have also tried unsecured -- so it's not that I'm a gumby and am typing my WEP key in wrong.

Some possibly useful output:
```
 $ lshw -C network
WARNING: you should run this program as super-user.
  *-network               
... wired information eth0 ...
  *-network
... wired information eth1...
  *-network
       description: Wireless interface
       product: BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] 802.11g Wireless LAN Controller
       vendor: Broadcom Corporation
       physical id: 6
       bus info: pci@0000:06:06.0
       logical name: eth2
       version: 02
       serial: 00:1d:60:6f:00:fd
       width: 32 bits
       clock: 33MHz
       capabilities: bus_master ethernet physical wireless
       configuration: broadcast=yes driver=ndiswrapper+bcmwl5 driverversion=1.45+Broadcom,02/11/2005, 3.100. latency=32 module=ndiswrapper multicast=yes wireless=IEEE 802.11g
$ cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist | grep bcm43xx
blacklist bcm43xx
blacklist bcm43xx
blacklist bcm43xx

```
So...the ndiswrapper driver is installed and (should) work ok.  It shouldn't conflict with the "native" driver as it is blacklisted (three times no less!).

```
$ iwlist scan
lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth1      Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth2      Scan completed :
          Cell 01 - Address: 00:14:A5:C5:30:F2
                    ESSID:"wantok"
                    Protocol:IEEE 802.11g
                    Mode:Managed
                    Frequency:2.442 GHz (Channel 7)
                    Quality:87/100  Signal level:-40 dBm  Noise level:-96 dBm
                    Encryption key:on
                    Bit Rates:1 Mb/s; 2 Mb/s; 5.5 Mb/s; 11 Mb/s; 6 Mb/s
                              9 Mb/s; 12 Mb/s; 18 Mb/s; 24 Mb/s; 36 Mb/s
                              48 Mb/s; 54 Mb/s
                    Extra:bcn_int=100
                    Extra:atim=0
          Cell 02 - Address: 00:0A:95:F6:FC:BE
                    ...next door neighbours wireless network...
```
So, it scans ok and can see the router that I would like to connect to.  There's a good signal strength (as there should be...the router is only about 1m fromthe card).  So, try connecting (via manual connection, rather than Network Manager):

```
 
$ sudo ifconfig eth2 down
[sudo] password for michael:
$ sudo dhclient -r eth2
There is already a pid file /var/run/dhclient.pid with pid 6142
killed old client process, removed PID file
Internet Systems Consortium DHCP Client V3.0.5
Copyright 2004-2006 Internet Systems Consortium.
All rights reserved.
For info, please visit http://www.isc.org/sw/dhcp/

Listening on LPF/eth2/00:1d:60:6f:00:fd
Sending on   LPF/eth2/00:1d:60:6f:00:fd
Sending on   Socket/fallback
DHCPRELEASE on eth2 to 192.168.0.1 port 67
$ sudo ifconfig eth2 up
$ sudo iwconfig eth2 essid "wantok"
$ sudo iwconfig eth2 key MY_128HEX_KEY
$ sudo iwconfig eth2 key open
$ sudo iwconfig eth2 mode Managed
$ sudo dhclient eth2
There is already a pid file /var/run/dhclient.pid with pid 0
Internet Systems Consortium DHCP Client V3.0.5
Copyright 2004-2006 Internet Systems Consortium.
All rights reserved.
For info, please visit http://www.isc.org/sw/dhcp/

Listening on LPF/eth2/00:1d:60:6f:00:fd
Sending on   LPF/eth2/00:1d:60:6f:00:fd
Sending on   Socket/fallback
DHCPDISCOVER on eth2 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 7
DHCPDISCOVER on eth2 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 15
DHCPDISCOVER on eth2 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 9
No DHCPOFFERS received.
No working leases in persistent database - sleeping.

```
So, make sure that the wired connections aren't hogging anything (clutching at straws by this point):
```

$ sudo ifconfig eth0 down
$ sudo ifconfig eth1 down
$ sudo dhclient eth2
There is already a pid file /var/run/dhclient.pid with pid 6217
killed old client process, removed PID file
Internet Systems Consortium DHCP Client V3.0.5
Copyright 2004-2006 Internet Systems Consortium.
All rights reserved.
For info, please visit http://www.isc.org/sw/dhcp/

Listening on LPF/eth2/00:1d:60:6f:00:fd
Sending on   LPF/eth2/00:1d:60:6f:00:fd
Sending on   Socket/fallback
DHCPDISCOVER on eth2 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 7
DHCPDISCOVER on eth2 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 9
DHCPDISCOVER on eth2 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 13
DHCPDISCOVER on eth2 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 2
No DHCPOFFERS received.
No working leases in persistent database - sleeping.
$

```
A couple of othe outputs that may be of interest:
```
$ cat /etc/network/interfaces
auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

$ sudo ethtool eth2
Settings for eth2:
        Supports Wake-on: d
        Wake-on: d
        Link detected: no
 
```

One thing that I thought it may be is that under the lshw -C network, the card is listed as being 32-bit -- however, the OS and everything else in the machine is 64-bit...could this be the source of the evil (again, clutching at straws, but haven't found an answer to this question yet)?

If anyone has any suggestions or ideas, that would be absolutely tops!

Many thanks,
Michael

The only thing that might be helpful for you is to try using ```
iwconfig essid wantok channel 7 ap 00:14:A5:C5:30:F2
``` to setup up the wireless card.  You may want to run that command 2 or 3 times because sometimes it doesn't work the first time (don't ask me why) and then run dhclient.

### Post by mmbates on 2008-02-24
G'day compwiz,
Thanks for getting back to me again!  I really appreciate the time and thought that you're putting into my problem.

> **compwiz18 said:**

- Yes, I am running AMD64 Gutsy Xubuntu on this machine.  The processor, motherboard and graphics cards are all 64 bit, while the network card seems to be 32 bit.
- Xubuntu is the only OS that I run (or have ever run) on the machine.   My old desktop runs 32 bit Gutsy Xubuntu.  I have a USB network adapter for that machine, but have not tried (very hard) to get it to work on the new computer, as I have to use ndiswrapper and according to the list on the ndiswrapper site, Win98/2k drivers work best ... which aren't very 64 bit compatible.  This has given me an idea ... I could try putting the wireless card in the 32 bit machine and see if I have more success on that one.
- I do have WEP encription on the network, but I have tried it unencrypted with no luck either.  
- The router is set up to accept any MAC address.

### Post by mmbates on 2008-02-25
Well, you wont believe it I stuck the card into my old 32 bit machine, let Xubuntu do its thing and install the firmware drivers (via the Restricted Drivers GUI) and the result ... I connected first try (manually), with not a single tiny incy wincy little problem.

:guitar:  :guitar:  :guitar:  :guitar:  :guitar:  

So there we go, the root of all evil is the 32 bit-ness of the card after all!

There goes many, many, many hours of my life down the drain :mad:

Next stop, a computer store with a 64 bit card that has open source drivers!  It would be interesting to know whether changing the OS to 32 bit would make it work, but I suspect not, as others with the same chipset have seemingly had it working on 64 bit -- so it must be some kind of strange hardware conflict going on inside the guts of the machine.  So, the details of my motherboard and CPU follow for anyone else having the same problem:
Motherboard: Gigabyte GA-MA790FX-DQ6
CPU: AMD Phenom 9600

Thanks so much for your help Compwiz!

cheers,
Michael

---

### Post by Vivian-Synecdoche on 2008-02-28
I just wanted to add my 2 cents worth.

I own an acer with this particular network card and I found it quite a bit of trouble getting this card to work.

But I think I've solved this one - or at least got it to work like this:

First, I followed Compwiz18's instructions to the dot.

1. Insert Ubuntu CD
2. Download bcm4318.all.tar.gz (from first post)
3. Extracted via tar -xf
4. ran the script with: sudo ./ndiswrapper_setup
5. After it finished, I restarted.

At this point, I'd come to step 8 of Compwiz18's post, but I found that forsberg's post strangely unorganized and confused me. 

Instead, I found some help on the INTERWEBs!!!

I found this website on installing the acer_acpi: [http://code.google.com/p/aceracpi/](http://code.google.com/p/aceracpi/)

Here, I scrolled to the bottom and clicked on the link for Ubuntu and downloaded the .deb file for acer_acpi installation.

After that, I just installed it and now the connection's up and at them toobs for a bit.

I hoped this helps someone out there. :D

Thank you to COMPWIZ18 and FORSBERG!

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2008-03-14
> **obocho said:**
> HI guys, 

guide worked for me... BUT, i still have problem when I restart the computer. 
each time I have to write "sudo modprobe ndiswrapper" again...

Any idea

thks

Make sure that ndiswrapper is not in the file /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist (you can run gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist to check -- press alt+f2 then enter that command)

---

### Post by obocho on 2008-03-14
I have checked blacklist, but no... it's not blacklisted. 

thks

---

### Post by vyco10 on 2008-03-15
> **obocho said:**
> HI guys, 

guide worked for me... BUT, i still have problem when I restart the computer. 
each time I have to write "sudo modprobe ndiswrapper" again...

Any idea

thks

I don't know if this will work, but I found it in a HOWTO for the cards with Broadcom 4306 chipsets (*[click here for the HOWTO]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=201902")*):

[QUOTE=ubunturules]Modprobe ndiswrapper didn't put ndiswrapper in the /etc/modules folder for me on Gutsy and I tried it twice. This was the one of the reasons why after reboot a lot of people lose their wireless connection. Make sure you run the following command just to make sure. The following command manually puts ndiswrapper in the /etc/modules folder.

17.
Code:
```
echo 'ndiswrapper' | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```[/QUOTE]

I don't know. Maybe this could be worked into the script?

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2008-03-15
This is in the script, and it is also what I was going to suggest you try next too.

### Post by Toolbelt on 2008-04-02
Compwiz:

I second the request for updated how-to for Hardy.  It seems folks are experiencing another set of issues that have not been seen in previous versions.  Here are two threads in other parts of the Ubuntu forums:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=734003](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=734003)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=738216](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=738216)

I am also responding to a new thread in the kubuntu users list with a Hardy + bcm4318 configuration.

I can not vouch for the validity of the problems or the solutions reported here, only that in the ops' minds they exist and they are not like the reporting I have seen in previous two to three versions of Ubuntu.  I will be installing Hardy in two to three weeks and am hoping that the wheel will not need to be reinvented for Ubuntu + bcm4318.

[RIGHT][INDENT]root@ebh256-laptop:~# echo `ndiswrapper` | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
install/manage Windows drivers for ndiswrapper usage: ndiswrapper OPTION -i inffile install driver described by 'inffile' -a devid driver use installed 'driver' for 'devid' (dangerous) -r driver remove 'driver' -l list installed drivers -m write configuration for modprobe -ma write module alias configuration for all devices -mi write module install configuration for all devices -v report version information where 'devid' is either PCIID or USBID of the form XXXX:XXXX, as reported by 'lspci -n' or 'lsusb' for the card
[/INDENT][/RIGHT]

I am  veryyyyy new to linux and follow these command phonetically so what am i doing wrong? note i did not find a folder /etc/module!!!

regard and thanks I'm starting to be hooked in Linux!!!

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2008-04-11
Sure: the problem is you are using a ` around the word "ndiswrapper", not a ". In other words, remove the ` and use quotation marks, so it looks like:
```
echo "ndiswrapper" | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```

### Post by blackest_knight on 2008-04-26
> **compwiz18 said:**
> [COLOR="Blue"][SIZE="5"]**Hardy**[/SIZE][/COLOR]

This process is vastly simplified in Hardy. The easiest way to do it is to open the Hardware Drivers program (go to the System menu in the top left corner of the screen, and click Administration, and then Hardware Drivers) and check the Broadcom B43 wireless driver box, and reboot.
Done. :KS



I previously had wifi working using ndiswrapper so i had a few issues which i will list here 
first off the Hardware Drivers menu item wasnt there however I looked for it in add and remove software, found it and was told it conflicted with something else and to install through synaptic (it needs to install a package called jockey-gtk) anyway it uninstalled the conflicting package and installed and I could install the b43 driver... 

but it still didn't work this was because I had ndiswrapper in modules so a quick sudo nano /etc/modules and a # in front of ndiswrapper and a reboot later the wireless was on 

i just needed to configure it in networks and job done. 

(if you blacklisted b43 in your blacklist un-blacklist it)

interesting little point the reason for using ndiswrapper was the bcm43xx 
driver could only power the wireless to 18dbm where ndiswrapper would be 25dbm. with the b43 driver its 27dbm for tx power so it really is the way to go for the bcm4318 chipset.

### Post by tonage on 2008-04-26
I got my BCM4318 working.  I had bcmw15 installed in ndiswrapper.  I removed that, then picked the B43 wireless driver and presto.  I assume it had something to do with the ndiswrapper?  Dunno.  May help someone.

I just got this working.  I have not noticed it dropping out or anything yet.  It is working fine as of right now.

### Post by compwiz18 on 2008-04-28
I've spent most of this morning on this problem: here's what I've got so far.

First, you have to remove the ssb module:
```
sudo rmmod ssb
```

Then remove ndiswrapper/;
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
```

The modprobe ndiswrapper:
```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

With some luck, you're in business. With my luck, you hard lock...

---

### Post by compwiz18 on 2008-04-28
Success at last! :D

Basically, this howto has the trick [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4819382](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4819382)

I'm attaching an updated version of this howto; I'd like someone to try it on a Hardy i386 system and a Hardy x86_64 system. On my i386 system, it causes a hard lock. So be warned! I'd like to know if this problem is unique to my system or not - if it is, then I'll update the main post, otherwise... back to the drawing board.

I suspect I may have screwed up ndiswrapper when I was playing with it earlier to get this to work, so that's the reason for my uncertainty.

To use the attachment, follow the steps in the first post, only use this attachment instead of the one linked to in Step 2.

[COLOR="Blue"]EDIT: It seems to like my computer now. Strange.
EDIT 2: Now it doesn't again. It would appear that if you install the driver linked to above, and then use the script, it doesn't explode. Otherwise...[/COLOR]

**THE ATTACHMENT MAY CAUSE YOUR COMPUTER TO LOCK UP. PROCEED AT YOUR OWN RISK.**

### Post by Tesafilmchen on 2008-04-30
Hi Compwiz,
i have to thank you a lot for your good HOWTO, its the first Page i move to after every update :).

Now im on my HP nx6125 on Kubuntu 8.04 and with the B43 i got a very unstable and short range connection. I have tried your updated script (i368 ) for ndiswrapper but it doesnt work. 
I got finally the ndiswrapper to work with the remarks from this site:
[http://blog.roberthallam.org/2008/04/broadcom-4318-ubuntu-hardy-heron-ndiswrapper/](http://blog.roberthallam.org/2008/04/broadcom-4318-ubuntu-hardy-heron-ndiswrapper/)

Installed the bcmwl5.inf 

And added to /etc/rc.local the following lines :

modprobe -r b44
modprobe -r b43
modprobe -r b43legacy
modprobe -r ssb
modprobe -r ndiswrapper
modprobe ndiswrapper
modprobe b44

I did not blacklist anything else than the bcm43xx module.


Now i have a very stable connection :). The dark side is: I have  to configure the connection every start again in the knetworkmanager, and my comp doesnt want to shutdown completely until i hit strg+alt+entf. But i think these are minor problems.

Thx again and keep going

---

No neeed for ndiswrapper!!!

All testing that I've performed (comparing 54M as reported by XP drivers, versus 1M as reported by bw43cutter) show that 1M in Ubuntu (Hardy) is anywhere between 80-100% of WinXP's speed (reported as 54M) (both up and down).

I'm testing using speed tests from dslreports.com and selecting test sites (NYC and NJ) that are less than or near 200 miles from my location.  I've tested somewhat thorougly--from a layman's perspective, but have not yet performed any linear regression analysis (kidding) or have a sizeable pool of results to show.

On a side note, after enabling WPA, Ubuntu showed 6M, without any changes on my part.  I set it at 12M with _no resulting difference in speed_.

I wouldn't worry about the report of 1M, unless it's a confidence problem for ya. :lolflag:

-Paul

### Post by shawn_duggan on 2008-06-15
Thank you - I finally got this working in Hardy after many tries, including compiling my own ndiswrapper, blacklisting things, adding startup scripts...I was getting very frustrated.  This failed for me many times until I did the following:

-after a fresh install, completely update system before messing with drivers
-reboot
-enable b43 in Hardware Drivers
-reboot
-everything worked perfectly
-no trouble at all with WEP.  Did not need other steps.

### Post by External on 2008-07-22
Just a quick remark for users owning an Acer laptop, as the original post mentions you need to install acerhk, OR acer-acpi.

I don't know if this has already been called attention to, but things are just gonna be easier especially in Intrepid, since acer-acpi is already upstream, and is incorporated in the 2.6.25 kernel, the basis of Intrepid. Backport was requested and granted for it to be included in Hardy, so there is no need to be messing around finding acer-acpi any more. (What's more, it's not even supported/debugged/developed anymore, acer-wmi is.)

References:
[http://code.google.com/p/acer-acpi-deb/](http://code.google.com/p/acer-acpi-deb/)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/190677](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/190677)
[http://groups.google.co.uk/group/aceracpi-announce/browse_thread/thread/76f2cb0e08c01c42?hl=en-GB](http://groups.google.co.uk/group/aceracpi-announce/browse_thread/thread/76f2cb0e08c01c42?hl=en-GB)

[PS. Now let's just hope Intrepid is going to be more stable than Hardy, see [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5308402&postcount=212](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5308402&postcount=212) and the thread the post integrates into.]

### Post by stephenr80 on 2009-01-16
sooooo i got it work after some tries

here is my configuration for those who like Xubuntu!

this is my gf's laptop: ACER 4280 with the broadcom wireless

1. xubuntu ibex installed as windows programm
2. don't touch anything of the installation but install all the system patches (system updates)
3. reboot and go to drivers and it will find the driver, you have to turn it on and install it (all auto)
4. disconnect lan cable and reboot!
5. should appear the connection! (light of network in computer turns on aswell)

thanks to the ppl who made this possible, the controller in windows XP for this device it's really really crappy!

btw! i ran a dsl test and reached the max. speed, so i dont think i could do better with ndiswrapper

### Post by manores on 2009-01-25
So after spending a lot of time getting this wireless connection up, I finally have it.

BUT!!!!!!!

It captures 12 freaking wireless network signals and not my own! My Windows laptop sits just next to my linux machine, it can get the signal of my home wireless network, but not my linux machine! It gets signals from 12, TWELVE!, different networks and successfully connects to the internet through one of them, with no password protection.

### Post by mjitkop on 2009-05-04
As Jamaur mentioned, I also got my BCM4318 working with Jaunty last week by enabling the restricted driver listed in System / Administration / Hardware Drivers. 

You may have to restart a couple of times before the driver gets listed in there. It was not listed the very first time for me after installing Jaunty. I tried ndisgtk/ndiswrapper and b43fw_cutter to no avail but then decided to check again in the Hardware Drivers and there it was listed! I don't know if anything I did with ndiswrapper of b43fw_cutter made any difference or if it's just the fact that I restarted several times in between.

If you have tried to do anything with ndiswrapper or b43fw_cutter, you may have to disable them with a set of commands:

```
sudo modprobe -r b43 ssb ndiswrapper wl
sudo modprobe b43
sudo ifconfig wlan0 up
```Then make sure the restricted driver is listed and enabled, hopefully. That seemed to work for me.

---

### Post by physeetcosmo on 2009-06-14
> **reboskyz24 said:**
>  The toggle switch that used to show power to the wireless card doesn't blink (searching) or go steady (connected).  How can I ensure the wireless card is enabled? 

I would check your BIOS first, many times the BIOS defaults the peripherals to "OFF" and even if you "enable" them in the OS, the BIOS is still master and has it shut off.

If it IS enabled in the BIOS, I'd do:
```
lspci
```
or
```
lsusb
```
to verify that the device is actually installed properly. Look for the name of your Wireless card in the list. The reason I say "either" of these commands is that MY wireless card on the eeePC is actually part of the USB bus and you have to list the USB bus rather than the PCI bus to check for it being installed properly. I don't know if your laptop has the same configuration, but I thought I'd cover all the bases. :)

Let us know if you re-install works.

---

### Post by ebooa on 2009-11-18
greetings

i've recently spent nearly 2days with the installation of this driver under ubuntu 9.10 (karmic) and after having busted some crackers this is the conclusion i got.


I tried installing the b43-fwcutter-011 and b43-fwcutter-012 with the wl_apsta_3.130.20.0 and i had no configuration setup for the wireless driver. So i researched a little and decided to try and install the broadcom-wl-4.178.10.4 and the broadcom-wl-4.80.53.0 drivers but still i had no wireless device.
i then decided to use an older driver now considered deprecated for the newer versions the bcm43xx-fwcutter-006 and somehow the wireless activation got detected but when i tried accessing the wireless networks it showed none. 
I then reminded myself that when i had intrepid (8.04) i had to do some editing under the modprobe.d blacklisted files. After i edited the blacklist files at modprobe.d i restarted the networking and it worked just fine with all wireless networking detecting and without losing connection for the past 2hours.

Therefore this is what i think it worked out amongst all of this and hope it turns out helpfull if anyone needs it.

1.ºGo to these links and download bcm43xx-fwcutter-006.tar.bz2 
[http://developer.berlios.de/project/showfiles.php?group_id=4547](http://developer.berlios.de/project/showfiles.php?group_id=4547)

and

[http://bu3sch.de/b43/fwcutter/b43-fwcutter-012.tar.bz2](http://bu3sch.de/b43/fwcutter/b43-fwcutter-012.tar.bz2) (direct link)

At your terminal go to the folder where you downloaded bcm43xx-fwcutter to  and do as it follows
 "sudo tar xvjf bcm43xx-fwcutter-006.tar.bz2"
"cd bcm43xx-fwcutter-006"
"make"
"cd .."

do this same protocol for b43-fwcutter-012.tar.bz2

2.º Go to this link and download the drivers there into a folder of your choice [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx)

Here i used this link to configure the bcm43xx firmware
[http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Drivers/b43#fw-bcm43xx](http://linuxwireless.org/en/users/Drivers/b43#fw-bcm43xx) 

i looked for the b43-fwcutter-012 tutorial at the top of the guide and the bcm43xx tutorial at the bottom of the page to the the exports to /lib/firmware

Apart from the "wget" commands in the tutorial, i ran them exactly as they are explained.I also used the b43-fwcutter to install the following firmware

"sudo b43-fwcutter --unsupported -w /lib/firmware broadcom-wl-4.80.53.0/kmod/wl_apsta_mimo.o"


3.º After having done this i did a "sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bcm43.conf" and edited it as it follows

# Warning: This file is autogenerated by bcmwl-kernel-source. All changes to this file will be lost.
#blacklist b43
blacklist b43legacy
#blacklist ssb
blacklist bcm43xx 

4.º I then ran the collowing command under root mode "/etc/init.d/networking restart"

 It reconfigured my network devices but i still had to reboot it. After that it got connected immediately.



All this might sound noobish to some advanced users but it worked out for me and if you guys can make it out i hope it turns out to work as well as it is for me.

---

### Post by Sugi on 2010-06-12
Hello everyone,
Just wanted to let everyone know, I had great success with "Compaq Presario V5000" in 10.04 Lucid Lynx with wireless Broadcom b43.  This was by far the easiest it has ever been.  Nice!

The Process / Fix (Just like the original poster's thread comment):
1. Go to Hardware Drivers:
Terminal > $ /usr/bin/jockey-gtk
OR (Both does the same thing!)
System > Admninistration > Hardware Drivers.

2. Enable Broadcom B43 Wireless Drivers

3. Reboot and Enjoy! (MUST REBOOT)

I remember working on this same laptop within hardy and hating myself for even trying, but I was just unlucky during that time.

Sugi

Hello Aggienator,
It sounds like you have more then one instant of the package manager running, 
IE: Software Center, Synaptic, apt-get, and Aptitude.  
Make sure to close down all others and then process.

P.S.:
I might have explained my above post incorrectly.  If so, it's fixed now.
Terminal > $ "**/usr/bin/jockey-gtk**" is the same thing as "**System > Admninistration > Hardware Drivers**".

Good Luck,
Sugi

Hello Aggienator,
Can you try to reinstall the network manager for your ubuntu studio again please.

Do this with the terminal:
sudo apt-get install network-manager network-manager-gnome
or
Within the cd of your ubuntu studio:
/media/cdrom0/pool/main/n/network-manager-applet

For safe keeping, reinstall the Broadcom drivers from the "Hardware Drivers" again.

Back to your post from before,

Network Manager, Language, Battery, Sound Mixer, and E-mail.

Hi,
 
I'm a novice but this is what worked for me on an acer aspire 3000 with the same wifi card - not sure if all the steps are completely nessesary but i'll include everything just in case:
 
1) Clean install
2) You should get an "install driver" notification on the status bar - I ignored that for now and applied all the ubuntu updates first.
3) Install the b43 driver either from the status bar icon or admin>hardwaredrivers
4) activate driver in admin>hardware drivers
 
Now you need the firmware for the wifi card so use fwcutter
 
5) In terminal use command **sudo apt-get install b43-fwcutter **it automatically fetched and installed firmware in correct location.
 
6) Then it worked!
 
As I said - I'm a novice so somebody else could probably point out the difference between what I did and what your doing - you my not need a re-install - maybe just fwcutter???

### Post by physeetcosmo on 2010-12-06
> **eliadero said:**
> ...I installed today the ubuntu 10.10 distribution and it has the drivers included they only need to be activated via System> Additional drivers...it finds the driver and with one click presto!!!

This is pretty typical of any Linux Distro, if it is within their scope/goal of development. If there is enough demand for a product, the support is developed into the Nix distro.

It's interesting in that all of the INDIVIDUAL devices are not needed to be "supported", just the silicon and configuration of what most people call the "chip set".

To me, being an Electrical Engineer, it is funny to watch companies come out with "new and revolutionary" products, which are really only slightly different than their other products...as far as actual design is concerned. 

Glad you are enjoying Ubuntu! 8-)

### Post by DroidYoung on 2011-08-30
As I'm running Natty Narwhal I used this from [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx#b43%20-%20Internet%20access](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx#b43%20-%20Internet%20access) and it updated the firmware, installed the driver and started and found my Orange Livebox straight away. Works better than my XP connection too!! :D

> 
Since Ubuntu 11.04 **Natty Narwhal** additional installation of the package **firmware-b43-installer** can be helpful and/or necessary, respectively: 
~$ sudo apt-get install b43-fwcutter firmware-b43-installeror, if you need a legacy driver, use: 
~$ sudo apt-get install firmware-b43legacy-installer
